question: What does the acronym DOI stand for?
option_a: Data Object Interface
option_b: Digital Object Identifier
option_c: Document Origin Indicator
option_d: Directory Of Information
option_e: Digital Ownership Index
explanation_correct: A DOI is a Digital Object Identifier: a persistent identifier standardised as ISO 26324, used to identify digital, physical, or abstract objects such as journal articles and datasets.
explanation_incorrect: The other options are plausible-sounding expansions but are not the real name; the term emphasises identifying an 'object', not ownership or a directory.

question: What does ORCID stand for?
option_a: Official Registry of Contributor IDs
option_b: Open Researcher and Contributor ID
option_c: Open Registry of Cited Institutional Data
option_d: Organisational Research Contribution Index
option_e: Online Research Citation Identification
explanation_correct: ORCID stands for Open Researcher and Contributor ID; it provides a free, unique, persistent identifier that distinguishes one researcher from another.
explanation_incorrect: ORCID identifies people, not institutions or citations, so options referring to institutional data or citation indexes are incorrect.

question: What kind of entity does a ROR identifier identify?
option_a: Datasets
option_b: Research organizations
option_c: Funding grants
option_d: Individual researchers
option_e: Journal articles
explanation_correct: ROR (Research Organization Registry) provides open persistent identifiers for research organizations, helping disambiguate institution names in scholarly metadata.
explanation_incorrect: Researchers are identified by ORCID and outputs by DOIs; ROR is specifically for affiliations/organizations.

question: Which of these is an example of a correctly formed DOI?
option_c: https://ror.org/02mhbdp94
option_e: doi://article/12345
explanation_correct: A DOI has a prefix beginning with '10.' and a suffix separated by a forward slash, e.g. 10.1000/182; it is made actionable as https://doi.org/10.1000/182.
explanation_incorrect: The other strings are a different identifier type (an ORCID iD, a ROR ID, an ISBN) or an invented syntax.

question: What does the doi.org resolver actually do when you click a DOI link?
option_a: It assigns a brand-new DOI to the object every time the link is clicked
option_b: It verifies the identity of the author before allowing the article to load
option_c: It calculates and updates the article's live citation count on each visit
option_d: It stores a permanent, cached copy of the article on the doi.org server
option_e: It redirects the request to the resource's current web location
explanation_correct: The doi.org proxy uses Handle System technology to resolve the DOI and redirect the user to the current URL registered for that object, so links keep working when the underlying URL changes.
explanation_incorrect: DOIs do not host content, count citations, or verify identity; resolution is purely redirecting to the registered location.

question: In a full ORCID iD such as https://orcid.org/0000-0002-1825-0097, why might the final character be the letter 'X'?
option_a: It indicates that the researcher wishes to remain fully anonymous
option_b: It is a checksum digit that can equal the value 10
option_c: It marks the record as belonging to a deactivated account
option_d: It shows that the record has been set to fully private
option_e: It denotes an institutional rather than personal iD
explanation_correct: The last character of an ORCID iD is a checksum computed with the ISO/IEC 7064 MOD 11-2 scheme; when the check value is 10 it is written as 'X'.
explanation_incorrect: 'X' is not a status flag; it carries no meaning about privacy, activation, or account type.

question: Which three organizations jointly operate the Research Organization Registry (ROR)?
option_a: ORCID, ISNI, and GRID, three long-standing identifier registries
option_b: UNESCO, OECD, and the EU, three major international bodies
option_c: Crossref, DataCite, and the California Digital Library
option_d: Clarivate, Elsevier, and Springer Nature, three commercial publishers
option_e: arXiv, bioRxiv, and Zenodo, three well-known preprint and data repositories
explanation_correct: ROR is run as a collaborative initiative by the California Digital Library, Crossref, and DataCite, and its data is openly available under CC0.
explanation_incorrect: ROR grew out of (and effectively succeeded) GRID, but GRID/ISNI do not operate ROR; the commercial publishers listed are not involved.

question: What is the technical relationship between the DOI system and the Handle System?
option_a: Handles can only be created by DOI registration agencies
option_b: DOIs replaced the Handle System entirely once the DOI standard was adopted
option_c: The DOI system uses Handle System technology for the resolution step
option_d: The Handle System is a commercial product owned by Crossref
option_e: They are completely unrelated identifier schemes with no shared infrastructure
explanation_correct: The DOI system relies on Handle System technology (from CNRI, administered via the DONA Foundation) for resolution; a DOI is effectively a Handle in the '10.' namespace with an added metadata and social layer.
explanation_incorrect: DOIs did not replace Handles and are not unrelated to them; the Handle System is an open specification, not a Crossref-owned product.

question: What distinguishes ROR from earlier organization identifiers like GRID or ISNI in the scholarly context?
option_a: ROR only covers research organizations located within the United States
option_b: ROR is maintained and fully controlled by a single commercial vendor
option_c: ROR is the only registry among the three that charges a subscription fee
option_d: ROR identifies individual researchers rather than organizations
option_e: ROR data is open under CC0 and focused on research affiliations
explanation_correct: ROR positions itself as the first openly available (CC0, open API and data dump) organization identifier focused specifically on affiliations in scholarly metadata, embedded as a default in Crossref, DataCite, and ORCID metadata.
explanation_incorrect: ROR is free and global, community-operated rather than single-vendor, and identifies organizations, not people.

question: How does a PURL (Persistent Uniform Resource Locator) differ fundamentally from a DOI?
option_a: A PURL guarantees that its target content will never change or move
option_b: A PURL can only ever be used to point to research datasets
option_c: A PURL is essentially an HTTP redirection service with no metadata layer
option_d: A PURL is resolved through the same Handle System infrastructure as a DOI
option_e: PURLs can only be issued and registered exclusively by Crossref
explanation_correct: A PURL is a curated HTTP URL that redirects to a target (originally an OCLC service, now run by the Internet Archive); unlike a DOI it does not use the Handle System and has no registration-agency metadata or governance layer.
explanation_incorrect: PURLs do not use the Handle System, are not Crossref-issued, are not limited to datasets, and like any redirect cannot guarantee the target content is unchanged.

question: Following the 2021-2022 partnership with DataCite, how are IGSN IDs (for physical samples) now implemented technically?
option_a: As ARKs minted specifically by the California Digital Library
option_b: As functionally DataCite DOIs, with metadata structured to the DataCite schema
option_c: As a wholly separate identifier system with its own independent resolver
option_d: As ORCID iDs that have been extended to cover physical objects
option_e: As Handle records that sit outside the DOI namespace entirely
explanation_correct: Since the IGSN e.V.-DataCite partnership, IGSN IDs for material samples are functionally DataCite DOIs whose metadata follows the DataCite Metadata Schema, giving samples the same infrastructure as other DataCite-registered objects.
explanation_incorrect: IGSN IDs are no longer a fully separate system; they are not ORCID iDs, ARKs, or Handles outside the DOI namespace.

question: What do the four letters in the FAIR data principles stand for?
option_a: Formatted, Archived, Interlinked, Referenced
option_b: Federated, Authenticated, Immutable, Reviewed
option_c: Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable
option_d: Free, Accessible, Indexed, Reproducible
option_e: Fast, Automated, Integrated, Reliable
explanation_correct: FAIR stands for Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable; the principles guide how research data and metadata should be managed to maximise reuse, including by machines.
explanation_incorrect: 'Free' is a common misconception: the 'A' is Accessible (under clear conditions), not free, and FAIR does not by itself mean open.

question: What is re3data primarily used for?
option_a: Calculating and publishing journal impact factor scores every year
option_b: Finding and comparing research data repositories
option_c: Detecting plagiarism in submitted research manuscripts
option_d: Assigning permanent ORCID iDs to individual researchers
option_e: Hosting preprints before they undergo formal peer review
explanation_correct: re3data.org is a global, cross-disciplinary registry of research data repositories that researchers, funders, and librarians use to find where to deposit or locate data.
explanation_incorrect: re3data does not compute metrics, issue identifiers for people, screen for plagiarism, or host preprints; it is a directory of repositories.

question: A 'data management plan' (DMP) is best described as:
option_a: A piece of code written to run a specific statistical analysis on a dataset
option_b: A marketing plan designed to promote and advertise a published dataset
option_c: A license that transfers data ownership to a funder
option_d: A document describing how research data will be managed and preserved
option_e: A paid subscription that grants ongoing access to a data repository
explanation_correct: A DMP is a formal document, often required by funders, setting out how data will be handled during and after a project, including storage, metadata, sharing, and preservation.
explanation_incorrect: A DMP is not a license, marketing document, or analysis code; it is planning documentation.

question: A dataset is in a restricted-access repository requiring an application to obtain it, but it has rich standardized metadata and a clear reuse license. Can it still be considered FAIR?
option_a: No, because restricted data cannot have a persistent identifier
option_b: No, because FAIR and access controls are mutually exclusive
option_c: Yes, but only if the data is also released under a CC0 public domain license
option_d: Yes, because FAIR permits access controls when data remains discoverable
option_e: No, because FAIR requires all data to be openly and freely available
explanation_correct: FAIR is not the same as open: the 'Accessible' principle explicitly allows authentication and authorisation, provided data and metadata are findable and the access procedure is clearly specified.
explanation_incorrect: 'As open as possible, as closed as necessary' captures the idea; FAIR does not mandate CC0 or unrestricted access.

question: Which research output does the Zenodo repository accept?
option_a: Only preprints from the field of physics are accepted for deposit
option_b: Only peer-reviewed journal articles that have already been published
option_c: Only outputs that originate from EU-funded research projects
option_d: Only raw datasets collected from experiments run at CERN
option_e: A wide range of outputs, with a DOI assigned to each
explanation_correct: Zenodo, operated by CERN with OpenAIRE, is a catch-all repository accepting data, software, and many other output types without restrictions on format, size, or discipline, and assigns a DataCite DOI to deposits.
explanation_incorrect: Zenodo is open to all researchers and output types, not limited to CERN, EU projects, physics, or journal articles.

question: Under the NIH Data Management and Sharing (DMS) Policy effective January 25, 2023, at minimum when must scientific data underpinning a publication be shared?
option_a: Sharing is encouraged but never required
option_b: No later than the time of the associated publication
option_c: Within five years of the grant ending
option_d: Only if another researcher formally requests it
option_e: Ten years after data collection
explanation_correct: The NIH DMS Policy requires that, at a minimum, scientific data supporting a publication be shared by the time the publication appears; other data must be shared by the end of the project.
explanation_incorrect: The policy sets sharing as an expectation with defined timing, not something optional or triggered only by external request.

question: What do the CARE Principles for Indigenous Data Governance add relative to the FAIR principles?
option_a: They completely replace FAIR with much stricter technical requirements
option_b: They function as a licensing scheme, similar to Creative Commons
option_c: They define standardized metadata schemas for use by repositories
option_d: They add an ethical, rights-based layer emphasizing Indigenous authority
option_e: They apply only to genomic and biomedical data specifically
explanation_correct: CARE (Collective benefit, Authority to control, Responsibility, Ethics) complements the primarily technical FAIR principles by foregrounding Indigenous Peoples' rights, self-determination, and the ethical purposes of data use.
explanation_incorrect: CARE complements rather than replaces FAIR, is not limited to genomics, and is a governance framework, not a license or metadata schema.

question: Which statement best captures the difference between 'findable' and 'accessible' in FAIR?
option_a: Findable applies only to individual data files, while accessible applies only to software packages
option_b: Findable requires the user to make a payment to a repository, whereas accessible never does
option_c: They are simply synonyms that mean exactly the same thing in every context
option_d: Findable means locatable via a PID and metadata; accessible means retrievable via a standard protocol
option_e: Findable means the data has been openly licensed; accessible means it is indexed by Google Search
explanation_correct: Findability concerns persistent identifiers and rich, indexed metadata so resources can be located; accessibility concerns retrieving them via standardized, open protocols (with authentication where needed) once located.
explanation_incorrect: The two are distinct steps (locate vs retrieve), not synonyms, and neither is defined by licensing or payment.

question: Who developed the CARE Principles and in what venue?
option_a: The OECD, as part of a formal international treaty signed in 2015
option_b: UNESCO, as part of its 2021 Recommendation on Open Science
option_c: The original FAIR authors, as an appendix to their 2016 paper
option_d: The US National Institutes of Health, through internal policy guidance
option_e: The Global Indigenous Data Alliance, around 2019-2020
explanation_correct: CARE was developed by the Global Indigenous Data Alliance through the Research Data Alliance's International Indigenous Data Sovereignty Interest Group, with a formative article in the Data Science Journal in 2020.
explanation_incorrect: CARE is not an OECD, UNESCO, or NIH product, and it is distinct from the 2016 FAIR paper.

question: In open access publishing, what is 'gold' open access?
option_a: Depositing an accepted manuscript in an institutional repository to make it freely readable
option_b: A ranking tier awarded to journals based on their overall prestige and cost
option_c: Paying a fee directly to the publisher in order to read a single article
option_d: Publishing in an open access journal where the version of record is immediately free to read
option_e: Access that is restricted only to readers holding a special gold-tier subscription
explanation_correct: Gold open access means the final version of record is made openly available directly by the journal, often (though not always) funded by an article processing charge.
explanation_incorrect: Depositing in a repository is green OA, not gold; gold is about where the version of record is made open, not a ranking or subscription tier.

question: What is 'green' open access?
option_a: Paying an article processing charge directly to the publishing journal
option_b: A specific type of Creative Commons license applied only to green content
option_c: Access to the article that automatically expires after exactly one year
option_d: Self-archiving a copy of the article in a repository
option_e: Publishing exclusively in journals that focus on environmental themes
explanation_correct: Green open access refers to self-archiving a version of a work (often the author accepted manuscript) in an institutional or subject repository, providing free access without necessarily paying a fee.
explanation_incorrect: Green OA is about the repository route, not journal subject matter, APCs, or a specific license; embargoes may apply but are not its definition.

question: The 2002 statement widely credited with an early, influential definition of 'open access' is the:
option_a: Budapest Open Access Initiative
option_b: Plan S
option_c: Leiden Manifesto
option_d: San Francisco Declaration on Research Assessment
option_e: Barcelona Declaration
explanation_correct: The Budapest Open Access Initiative (February 2002) gave one of the first and most influential definitions of open access and named two strategies: self-archiving and open access journals.
explanation_incorrect: The Leiden Manifesto and DORA concern research assessment; Barcelona concerns open research information; Plan S is a 2018 funder policy.

question: What is the difference between an 'author accepted manuscript' (AAM) and the 'version of record' (VoR)?
option_a: The version of record is always, by definition, simply an early preprint of the work
option_b: The AAM is peer-reviewed but not publisher-formatted; the VoR is the final formatted version
option_c: They are functionally identical versions with no meaningful difference between them
option_d: The AAM is the very first draft written before any peer review; the VoR is that same AAM
option_e: The AAM designation applies only to datasets, never to journal articles themselves
explanation_correct: The AAM (post-print) reflects the content after peer review but before the publisher's copyediting and typesetting; the VoR is the final, formatted published article. Green OA policies often permit sharing the AAM.
explanation_incorrect: The AAM is not the pre-review draft (that is a preprint), and the VoR is not a preprint; they are different stages of the same article.

question: 'Diamond' (or 'platinum') open access is best described as:
option_a: Access to journal content that is limited only to readers holding premium subscriptions
option_b: Journals that are free to read and free to publish in, with no author-facing charges
option_c: Open access journals that charge unusually high article processing charges to authors
option_d: The practice of self-archiving a manuscript in green open access after an embargo period
option_e: A hybrid journal model that mixes subscription access with paid open access options
explanation_correct: Diamond (platinum) open access journals levy no charges on either readers or authors, and are typically run by universities, societies, or scholar-led groups with in-kind or grant support.
explanation_incorrect: Diamond OA specifically means no APCs; it is not a high-fee model, a subscription tier, hybrid, or the green route.

question: In a 'hybrid' open access journal, what does the model involve?
option_a: A subscription journal offering paid open access to individual articles
option_b: A repository that combines elements of both the green and gold OA routes
option_c: A journal that mixes together preprints and raw research datasets
option_d: A journal that alternates between fully open and fully closed issues
option_e: A journal that is entirely open access and charges no fees at all
explanation_correct: A hybrid journal is a subscription-based journal in which authors can pay an article processing charge to make their individual article open access while the rest of the journal remains behind a paywall.
explanation_incorrect: Hybrid is not fully open, not a repository, and not about mixing content types or alternating issues; Plan S notably discouraged funding hybrid APCs.

question: 'Bronze' open access is distinguished from gold and green primarily because:
option_a: It is a type of article that is published specifically in a diamond open access journal
option_b: The article is free to read on the publisher's site but has no open license
option_c: It always requires the manuscript to be deposited in an institutional repository
option_d: It requires authors to pay the single highest article processing charge available
option_e: It is a designation that applies only to monographs and other long-form books
explanation_correct: Bronze OA describes articles free to read on the publisher's website but carrying no explicit open license, meaning reuse rights are undefined and the free access can be removed at the publisher's discretion.
explanation_incorrect: Bronze is defined by the absence of a clear license, not by repository deposit, journal type, fees, or format.
review_note: 'Bronze OA' is a widely used term from Piwowar et al. 2018; the Durham guide is a secondary source. Confirm the definition framing before publishing.

question: What problem does cOAlition S's Rights Retention Strategy aim to solve?
option_a: The problem of the same study being published more than once in duplicate
option_b: The problem of peer review taking an unusually long time to complete
option_c: The high cost that article processing charges impose on authors and their funders
option_d: Publisher embargoes that would otherwise block immediate green open access
option_e: The lack of persistent identifiers such as DOIs for published journal articles
explanation_correct: The Rights Retention Strategy has funders require grantees to attach a CC BY license (via a prior obligation) to their author accepted manuscripts, so authors can make them openly available immediately regardless of publisher embargo terms.
explanation_incorrect: The strategy targets rights and embargoes, not APC costs, identifiers, review speed, or duplication.

question: cOAlition S announced that its financial support for open access publishing under 'transformative arrangements' would end at which point?
option_a: At the end of 2024, with future deals expected to be 'pure publish'
option_b: In 2018, at the exact moment Plan S itself was first launched
option_c: Only after the year 2030, once current agreements have expired
option_d: Support is entirely open-ended and has no planned end date at all
option_e: This kind of arrangement was never actually funded by cOAlition S
explanation_correct: cOAlition S confirmed that support for transformative arrangements (transitional deals meant to flip subscription spend to open access) ends after 2024, with the expectation that subsequent agreements would be 'pure publish'.
explanation_incorrect: Transformative arrangements were explicitly time-limited transitional tools, not unfunded, permanent, or tied only to Plan S's launch year.
review_note: Verify the exact end-of-2024 wording against a current cOAlition S page, as the 2026-2030 strategy may restate this.

question: What is a journal's Impact Factor intended to measure?
option_a: The overall quality of one single individual article
option_b: The typical number of authors listed per published paper
option_c: The annual salary paid to the journal's editor
option_d: The total number of paying subscribers to the journal
option_e: The average recent citation count of articles in that journal
explanation_correct: The Journal Impact Factor is a journal-level average: citations in a given year to the journal's items from the two preceding years, divided by the number of citable items in those two years.
explanation_incorrect: It is a journal-level average, not a measure of an individual article's quality, author counts, subscriber numbers, or editorial pay.

question: DORA, a widely signed declaration on research assessment, stands for:
option_a: Digital Open Research Archive
option_b: San Francisco Declaration on Research Assessment
option_c: Data Openness and Reuse Agreement
option_d: Declaration of Open Research Access
option_e: Directory of Research Assessment
explanation_correct: DORA is the San Francisco Declaration on Research Assessment (2012), which calls for not using journal-based metrics such as the Impact Factor as a proxy for the quality of individual research.
explanation_incorrect: The other expansions are invented; DORA is specifically about reforming how research and researchers are assessed.

question: The h-index of a researcher is defined such that they have an h-index of h when:
option_a: h papers have each been cited at least h times
option_b: They have published exactly h papers
option_c: They have worked for h years
option_d: Their most-cited paper has h citations
option_e: h co-authors have cited them
explanation_correct: A researcher has an h-index of h if h of their publications have each received at least h citations; it tries to balance productivity and citation impact in one number.
explanation_incorrect: The h-index is not just a paper count, a single paper's citations, years of service, or co-author behaviour.
review_note: Definition is standard (Hirsch 2005); Wikipedia used as a convenience source. Consider citing Hirsch's PNAS paper.

question: What is the central recommendation of DORA regarding the Journal Impact Factor?
option_a: Apply the Impact Factor only when evaluating open access journals
option_b: Do not use journal metrics as a proxy for individual article quality
option_c: Recalculate the Impact Factor using a five-year window instead of two
option_d: Replace the Impact Factor entirely with the h-index as the standard metric
option_e: Use the Impact Factor as the primary criterion in hiring decisions
explanation_correct: DORA's general recommendation is explicitly to not use journal-based metrics, such as Journal Impact Factors, as a surrogate for the quality of individual research articles or a researcher's contributions in hiring, promotion, or funding.
explanation_incorrect: DORA discourages, rather than reconfigures, reliance on the JIF; it does not merely swap in the h-index or change the calculation window.

question: The Leiden Manifesto (2015) is best described as:
option_a: A technical standard for assigning persistent identifiers to research outputs
option_b: A voluntary code of conduct adopted by academic publishers
option_c: Ten principles for the responsible measurement of research performance
option_d: An annual ranking that orders universities around the world
option_e: A funder mandate that requires all research to be published openly
explanation_correct: The Leiden Manifesto, published in Nature in 2015, offers ten principles for the responsible use of metrics in research evaluation, stressing that quantitative measures should support, not replace, expert judgement.
explanation_incorrect: It is not a mandate, ranking, identifier standard, or publisher code; it is guidance on using metrics responsibly.

question: Which are among the four 'core commitments' of the CoARA Agreement on Reforming Research Assessment?
option_a: Mandate the adoption of a single, standardized global CV format
option_b: Base assessment on qualitative judgement, and abandon inappropriate use of journal metrics
option_c: Formally rank every university in the world on an annual basis
option_d: Increase institutional reliance on the Journal Impact Factor and h-index scores
option_e: Require that all research outputs be published under gold open access
explanation_correct: CoARA's core commitments include recognising diversity of contributions, basing assessment primarily on qualitative judgement (peer review central) with responsible quantitative use, abandoning inappropriate use of metrics like the JIF and h-index, and avoiding rankings of organizations.
explanation_incorrect: CoARA moves away from JIF/h-index reliance and organization rankings, and is about assessment reform, not OA mandates or CV formatting.

question: A frequently criticized technical feature of the Journal Impact Factor is that:
option_a: It is a figure that each journal's own editor is free to set independently
option_b: The numerator counts all items, but the denominator counts only 'citable items'
option_c: It is a metric that counts only citations an article makes to itself
option_d: It is a figure that Clarivate recalculates fresh every single day
option_e: It is a metric that deliberately excludes all review articles from its count
explanation_correct: A well-known criticism is the numerator/denominator asymmetry: citations to all content (including editorials and letters) can count in the numerator, while the denominator counts only 'citable items' such as articles and reviews, which can inflate the ratio.
explanation_incorrect: The JIF is published annually (not daily), does not count only self-citations, is computed by Clarivate rather than editors, and includes review articles.

question: Who prepared the CoARA Agreement, and roughly when was it finalized?
option_a: DORA's own steering group, working independently back in 2012
option_b: The full membership of UNESCO, acting together in 2021
option_c: The European Commission acting entirely on its own, back in 2018
option_d: The EUA, Science Europe, and the European Commission, finalized in July 2022
option_e: Clarivate and Elsevier, working together as commercial partners in 2020
explanation_correct: The Agreement on Reforming Research Assessment was drafted by a team including the EUA, Science Europe, and the European Commission (with expert input) and published on 20 July 2022; CoARA, the coalition to implement it, launched in December 2022.
explanation_incorrect: It was a multi-stakeholder European effort finalized in 2022, not a solo Commission, commercial, UNESCO, or DORA product.

question: The Barcelona Declaration on Open Research Information (launched 2024) primarily commits signatories to:
option_a: Require every scholarly journal in the world to adopt diamond open access
option_b: Create a single, unified global ranking system for individual researchers
option_c: Formally adopt the Journal Impact Factor as the field's standard metric
option_d: Abolish the practice of formal peer review across scholarly publishing
option_e: Make openness the default and support open infrastructures over closed sources
explanation_correct: The Barcelona Declaration commits organizations to make open research information (metadata about publications, affiliations, citations, etc.) the default, to use and support services enabling openness, and to sustain open infrastructures rather than rely on closed proprietary databases.
explanation_incorrect: It concerns the openness of research information/metadata, not abolishing peer review, endorsing the JIF, mandating diamond OA, or ranking researchers.

question: Under the widely used US federal definition, the three core forms of research misconduct are:
option_a: Self-citation, salami slicing, and ghostwriting
option_b: Fabrication, falsification, and plagiarism
option_c: Copyright infringement, libel, and fraud
option_d: Plagiarism, lateness, and bias
option_e: Bias, negligence, and error
explanation_correct: The US Office of Research Integrity defines research misconduct as fabrication, falsification, or plagiarism (FFP) in proposing, performing, reviewing, or reporting research.
explanation_incorrect: Honest error and differences of opinion are explicitly excluded, and while other practices are problematic, the canonical trio is FFP.

question: What does COPE, the organization, stand for?
option_a: Center for Open Preprint Evaluation
option_b: Consortium of Publishing Enterprises
option_c: Committee on Publication Ethics
option_d: Council of Peer Editors
option_e: Coalition for Open Publishing Ethics
explanation_correct: COPE is the Committee on Publication Ethics, which provides guidance and resources on publication ethics to editors and publishers.
explanation_incorrect: The other expansions are invented; COPE focuses specifically on publication ethics guidance.

question: What is 'plagiarism' in the research-integrity sense?
option_a: Publishing the exact same completed study a second time in another venue
option_b: Appropriating another person's ideas, results, or words without appropriate credit
option_c: Deliberately declining to share the underlying data behind a study
option_d: Fabricating data that was never actually collected in the first place
option_e: Openly disagreeing with the comments made by peer reviewers
explanation_correct: Plagiarism is the appropriation of another person's ideas, processes, results, or words without giving appropriate credit.
explanation_incorrect: Fabricating data is fabrication, and republishing the same work is duplicate publication; those are distinct from plagiarism.

question: Under the US federal definition, what is the difference between fabrication and falsification?
option_a: Fabrication is specifically about manipulating text, while falsification concerns manipulating images
option_b: Falsification is, by definition, always the result of an accidental mistake
option_c: Fabrication is specifically about misusing funding, while falsification concerns misrepresenting authorship
option_d: Fabrication is making up data; falsification is manipulating materials or altering results
option_e: The two terms are simply identical and describe exactly the same underlying behavior
explanation_correct: Fabrication is inventing data or results and recording or reporting them; falsification is manipulating research materials, equipment, or processes, or changing or omitting data or results, such that the research is not accurately represented.
explanation_incorrect: The two are distinct but related; neither is defined by medium (text vs image), intent as 'accidental', or funding/authorship.

question: What is the primary role of COPE for its members?
option_a: It formally investigates and legally prosecutes researchers accused of misconduct
option_b: It is responsible for assigning DOIs to articles that have been retracted
option_c: It produces a formal ranking of journals based on their ethical standards
option_d: It provides guidance, flowcharts, and a forum to discuss and handle publication-ethics cases
option_e: It provides direct financial support to fund studies that attempt replication
explanation_correct: COPE offers members guidance in the form of core practices, flowcharts, discussion documents, and a forum to discuss individual cases; it educates and advises rather than adjudicating or prosecuting.
explanation_incorrect: COPE does not prosecute, mint identifiers, rank journals, or fund research; its role is guidance and support.

question: In 2023, the Retraction Watch database was acquired and made openly available by which organization, integrating retraction data into its public API?
option_a: Clarivate
option_b: DataCite
option_c: Elsevier
option_d: ORCID
option_e: Crossref
explanation_correct: In September 2023 Crossref acquired the Retraction Watch database and made it openly available, with retraction data now surfaced through the Crossref REST API and kept updated in partnership with Retraction Watch.
explanation_incorrect: It was Crossref, not a commercial database vendor, DataCite, or ORCID, that opened this data.

question: The Hong Kong Principles (2019) are primarily concerned with:
option_a: Providing detailed guidance on how to properly format a data management plan
option_b: Producing an official ranking of universities based on their research output
option_c: Assessing researchers in ways that strengthen integrity
option_d: Setting formal upper limits on the article processing charges journals may bill
option_e: Defining the technical syntax used for constructing persistent identifiers
explanation_correct: The Hong Kong Principles, from the 6th World Conference on Research Integrity (2019), set out five principles for assessing researchers in ways that reinforce integrity, such as valuing complete reporting and open science practices.
explanation_incorrect: They address researcher assessment and integrity, not DMP formatting, APC caps, identifier syntax, or rankings.

question: 'HARKing', a questionable research practice, refers to:
option_a: Citing one's own previously published work within a new paper
option_b: Conducting peer review of a manuscript while remaining fully anonymous
option_c: Formally registering a study's protocol before data collection begins
option_d: Hypothesizing after the results are known, presented as if predicted in advance
option_e: Sharing a study's underlying data publicly before the paper is published
explanation_correct: HARKing stands for Hypothesizing After the Results are Known: framing an exploratory finding as though it were an a priori prediction, which inflates apparent confirmatory support and is a target of practices like preregistration.
explanation_incorrect: HARKing is not about data sharing, anonymous review, self-citation, or preregistration itself (which counteracts HARKing).

question: Approximately how many retractions did the combined Crossref/Retraction Watch dataset cover when the data was opened, reflecting the scale of the retraction record?
option_a: Tens of thousands, roughly 40,000-50,000
option_b: Fewer than 100 retractions in total
option_c: Exactly 1,000 retractions in total
option_d: Around 500 retractions in total
option_e: About 2 million retractions in total
explanation_correct: Reporting around the 2023 acquisition described the combined dataset as covering on the order of tens of thousands of retractions (roughly 40,000-50,000), reflecting how the retraction record has grown.
explanation_incorrect: The scale is tens of thousands, not hundreds, exactly a thousand, or millions.
review_note: Exact totals vary by source and grow over time; treat the 40k-50k range as approximate and re-check before publishing.

question: In the influential 2019 US National Academies framing, 'reproducibility' most precisely means:
option_a: Having your work cited by others
option_b: Publishing in a reproducible journal
option_c: Getting consistent results from a new study with newly collected data
option_d: Consistent results using the same data, code, and analysis steps
option_e: Repeating an experiment in a different country
explanation_correct: The National Academies define reproducibility as obtaining consistent results using the same input data, computational steps, methods, code, and conditions of analysis (computational reproducibility).
explanation_incorrect: Using new data to re-ask the question is replicability, not reproducibility, in this framing; the other options are unrelated.

question: What is 'preregistration' of a study?
option_a: Submitting a completed manuscript to a journal before it has actually been written
option_b: Paying a required publication fee to the journal well in advance
option_c: Formally registering for and obtaining a personal ORCID iD
option_d: Publicly specifying hypotheses and analysis plans before collecting or examining the data
option_e: Registering in advance to attend an upcoming academic conference
explanation_correct: Preregistration means documenting a study's hypotheses and planned analyses in a public registry before data are collected or analyzed, helping distinguish confirmatory from exploratory work.
explanation_incorrect: It is not conference sign-up, prepaying fees, submitting an unwritten manuscript, or obtaining an identifier.

question: In the 2019 National Academies distinction, 'replicability' refers to:
option_a: Consistent results across studies using newly collected data
option_b: Formally translating a published paper into a different language
option_c: Depositing and archiving a dataset within a trusted repository
option_d: Simply rerunning the exact same code on the exact same original dataset
option_e: Reformatting an already-published paper to submit to a new journal
explanation_correct: Replicability is obtaining consistent results across different studies that each answer the same scientific question with their own newly collected data, in contrast to reproducibility, which reuses the same data.
explanation_incorrect: Rerunning the same code on the same data is reproducibility; the other options are unrelated tasks.

question: How does a Registered Report differ from ordinary preregistration?
option_a: The study protocol is peer reviewed before data collection and can receive 'in-principle acceptance'
option_b: The study protocol is only ever reviewed after the paper has already been published
option_c: A Registered Report and ordinary preregistration are, in every respect, identical
option_d: This publishing format applies only to formal randomized clinical trials
option_e: A Registered Report requires no form of peer review at any stage at all
explanation_correct: In a Registered Report, a Stage 1 manuscript (introduction and methods) is peer reviewed before data collection; if accepted in principle, the journal commits to publishing the completed study regardless of whether results are positive, reducing publication bias.
explanation_incorrect: Unlike plain preregistration, the protocol undergoes journal peer review and carries a publication commitment; it is not review-free or limited to clinical trials.

question: The TOP Guidelines, adopted by many journals, primarily address:
option_a: Setting standard limits on the article processing charges journals may bill
option_b: Transparency and openness standards for journal data and code policies
option_c: Defining the technical syntax used for persistent identifier strings
option_d: Producing an annual ranking of universities based on research output
option_e: Standardizing the copyright transfer terms used in publishing agreements
explanation_correct: The Transparency and Openness Promotion (TOP) Guidelines, from the Center for Open Science, give journals a menu of standards such as data, analysis code, and materials transparency, each implementable at increasing levels of stringency.
explanation_incorrect: TOP is about openness/transparency policy, not identifiers, fees, rankings, or copyright transfer.

question: Which set of problems are Registered Reports specifically designed to reduce?
option_a: Peer reviewer shortages
option_b: Plagiarism and image duplication
option_c: Slow typesetting and high APCs
option_d: Bias, p-hacking, and HARKing
option_e: Data storage costs
explanation_correct: By fixing hypotheses and analyses before results are known and guaranteeing publication of sound studies, Registered Reports target publication bias, p-hacking (exploiting analytic flexibility), and HARKing.
explanation_incorrect: They are not aimed at typesetting, APCs, plagiarism, storage costs, or reviewer supply.

question: In the TOP Guidelines, what does having 'three levels' for each standard mean?
option_a: It means that three completely separate journals must formally agree on a decision
option_b: It means the review process for a manuscript happens across three separate rounds
option_c: Each standard can be implemented with increasing stringency, from disclosure to verification
option_d: It means the TOP Guidelines contain only three standards in total
option_e: It means authors are given exactly three separate attempts to submit their manuscript
explanation_correct: Each TOP standard can be adopted at one of three levels of increasing rigor, roughly: disclose whether the practice was followed, require the practice (e.g., share the data), or verify/certify compliance, letting journals adopt openness incrementally.
explanation_incorrect: The levels describe stringency of a single standard, not journal counts, a total number of standards, submission attempts, or review rounds.

question: Why does the 2019 National Academies report argue that a lack of replicability is not always a sign of error or misconduct?
option_a: Non-replication can arise from real complexity and can itself drive discovery
option_b: Because academic journals, as a rule, never actually publish replication studies
option_c: Because every single instance of non-replication is ultimately caused by fraud
option_d: Because replicating a scientific study is considered impossible in principle
option_e: Because the terms replicability and reproducibility are, in fact, identical concepts
explanation_correct: The report notes that some non-replication is expected and even useful: it can result from the genuine complexity of systems under study and can prompt new hypotheses, so it should not be automatically equated with error or misconduct.
explanation_incorrect: The report does not claim replication is impossible, that non-replication is always fraud, or that the two terms are synonymous.

question: Which organization created and maintains the TOP Guidelines and the associated 'TOP Factor' concept?
option_a: UNESCO, through its open science division
option_b: The Center for Open Science (COS)
option_c: Crossref, as part of its metadata services
option_d: Elsevier, as a commercial publisher
option_e: Clarivate, through its Web of Science platform
explanation_correct: The Center for Open Science developed the TOP Guidelines (introduced in Science in 2015) and the TOP Factor, an alternative journal metric scoring journals on their transparency policies rather than citations.
explanation_incorrect: It is COS, not a commercial metrics vendor, Crossref, or UNESCO, that stewards TOP.

question: Crossref is best described as:
option_a: A government agency responsible for regulating scholarly publishing
option_b: A university ranking service that compares institutions annually
option_c: A social networking platform designed specifically for scientists
option_d: A nonprofit that registers DOIs and metadata for scholarly content
option_e: A commercial publisher that produces its own line of academic journals
explanation_correct: Crossref is a not-for-profit membership organization, founded in 2000, that registers DOIs and rich metadata for journal articles, books, and other scholarly outputs, and offers services to find, cite, and link them.
explanation_incorrect: Crossref is not a publisher, ranking service, government body, or social network; it is scholarly infrastructure.

question: OpenAlex is:
option_a: A preprint server where researchers post manuscripts before formal review
option_b: A free and open index of scholarly works, authors, institutions, sources, and more
option_c: A commercial database like Web of Science with subscription-only access
option_d: A reference manager used to organize and format bibliographic citations
option_e: A tool used by publishers specifically to detect plagiarism
explanation_correct: OpenAlex, run by the nonprofit OurResearch, is a free, openly licensed (CC0) index of scholarly entities: works, authors, sources, institutions, topics, publishers, and funders, offered with a free API.
explanation_incorrect: OpenAlex is open rather than subscription-only, and it is an index, not a preprint server, reference manager, or plagiarism tool.

question: What does the Unpaywall service help users do?
option_a: A service that ranks academic journals by their overall prestige
option_b: A tool that helps users bypass all forms of copyright restriction
option_c: A cloud service that lets researchers store their own personal data
option_d: A payment platform used specifically to pay article processing charges
option_e: Find legal, freely available open access versions of scholarly articles
explanation_correct: Unpaywall, from OurResearch, locates legally available open access copies of articles by harvesting repositories and publisher sites, delivered via a browser extension and API.
explanation_incorrect: Unlike Sci-Hub, Unpaywall uses only legitimate open sources; it does not process payments, rank journals, or host user data.

question: What is the primary difference in scope between Crossref and DataCite?
option_a: Crossref focuses mainly on registering DOIs for publications like journal articles
option_b: DataCite is, in fact, a commercial publisher that sells subscriptions
option_c: Crossref primarily handles research data while DataCite handles journal articles
option_d: Neither Crossref nor DataCite actually issues any DOIs at all
option_e: Crossref and DataCite are, in fact, simply the same organization
explanation_correct: Both are DOI registration agencies, but Crossref's core community is scholarly publications (articles, books, conference papers) while DataCite specializes in DOIs for datasets, software, samples, and other outputs; they collaborate (e.g., on ROR).
explanation_incorrect: Their emphases are not reversed, they are distinct organizations, both do issue DOIs, and DataCite is a nonprofit membership body, not a publisher.

question: What does SPARC (in the scholarly-communications context) stand for and do?
option_a: SPARC: advocates for open access, open data, and open education
option_b: A commercial publisher that sells subscriptions to its own journals
option_c: A commercial metrics firm that sells citation and impact data
option_d: A data repository whose main function is storing research datasets
option_e: A commercial ranking agency that produces annual university rankings
explanation_correct: SPARC is the Scholarly Publishing and Academic Resources Coalition, a nonprofit whose members are libraries and academic organizations, advocating for open access, open data, and open education and driving policy change.
explanation_incorrect: SPARC is an advocacy coalition, not a ranking agency, publisher, metrics vendor, or repository.

question: The Principles of Open Scholarly Infrastructure (POSI) are organized around which three areas?
option_a: Speed, cost, and marketing
option_b: Peer review, editing, and typesetting
option_c: Hardware, software, and staffing
option_d: Findability, accessibility, and reuse
option_e: Governance, sustainability, and insurance
explanation_correct: POSI groups its principles into governance (e.g., stakeholder-governed, cannot lobby), sustainability (e.g., transparent operations, revenue from services not data), and insurance (e.g., open source, data available for others to replicate if the organization fails).
explanation_incorrect: POSI is not organized around marketing, publishing production steps, the FAIR letters, or generic resource categories.

question: Which organization is chiefly known for research, advocacy, and tools (such as 'Infra Finder') to help sustain and fund open scholarly infrastructure?
option_a: arXiv, a preprint server for physics and math
option_b: Elsevier, a large commercial publisher
option_c: Clarivate, a commercial analytics company
option_d: ORCID, a researcher identifier registry
option_e: Invest in Open Infrastructure (IOI)
explanation_correct: Invest in Open Infrastructure (IOI) is an independent nonprofit that produces research, advisory work, and tools like Infra Finder to help the community understand, coordinate investment in, and sustain open research infrastructure.
explanation_incorrect: IOI is distinct from commercial vendors, from the identifier body ORCID, and from the preprint server arXiv.

question: FORCE11 is best known as the community associated with which of the following?
option_a: The Budapest Open Access Initiative, an early open access declaration
option_b: The Handle System, the technology underlying DOI resolution
option_c: The FAIR principles and the data/software citation principles
option_d: The Journal Impact Factor, a widely used citation metric
option_e: The h-index, a common measure of researcher productivity
explanation_correct: FORCE11 (Future of Research Communications and e-Scholarship) is a community that helped produce foundational frameworks including the FAIR guiding principles, the Joint Declaration of Data Citation Principles, and the Software Citation Principles.
explanation_incorrect: FORCE11 is not the origin of the JIF or h-index, the Handle System, or the Budapest Open Access Initiative.

question: Under POSI, what does the 'living will' / insurance concept require of an open infrastructure organization?
option_a: That the organization must never make any changes to its underlying software
option_b: That the organization must store all of its data on a single physical server
option_c: That its code is open source and its data can be preserved elsewhere
option_d: That the organization must be owned outright by a single university
option_e: That the organization must charge ongoing subscription fees to its users
explanation_correct: POSI's insurance principles call for open source software and depositing data with trusted archives so the community could re-create the service if the organization failed, reducing lock-in and single points of failure.
explanation_incorrect: The concept is about survivability and openness, not freezing software, single ownership, mandatory subscriptions, or single-server storage.

question: What is a 'preprint'?
option_a: An official announcement released directly to news media outlets
option_b: A printed proof copy of an article sent back to its author
option_c: An article that has since been formally withdrawn by its journal
option_d: The complete printed edition of a physical academic journal
option_e: A manuscript shared publicly before formal peer review
explanation_correct: A preprint is a manuscript that authors post publicly (often on a preprint server) before, or in parallel with, submission to a peer-reviewed journal, enabling early sharing and feedback.
explanation_incorrect: A preprint is not a proof copy, press release, retraction, or the print edition of a journal.

question: arXiv, founded in 1991, is a preprint server that primarily serves which fields?
option_a: Fine arts disciplines such as painting, sculpture, and design
option_b: Physics, mathematics, computer science, and related quantitative fields
option_c: Clinical medicine exclusively, with no other fields represented
option_d: Business management topics exclusively, with no other fields
option_e: Law and the humanities, excluding the quantitative sciences
explanation_correct: arXiv, started by Paul Ginsparg in 1991, hosts preprints in physics, mathematics, computer science, quantitative biology, statistics, and related quantitative disciplines.
explanation_incorrect: arXiv's coverage is the quantitative sciences; it is not focused on law, humanities, clinical medicine, arts, or business.

question: Does posting a preprint on arXiv mean the paper has been peer reviewed?
option_a: No, arXiv automatically rejects every piece of unreviewed work submitted
option_b: No, arXiv moderates submissions for suitability but does not peer review them
option_c: Yes, but this is true only for submissions in the field of physics
option_d: Only if the submitting author pays an additional posting fee
option_e: Yes, arXiv conducts full formal peer review of every single submission
explanation_correct: arXiv applies a moderation process to check that submissions are topical and of scholarly value, but it explicitly does not peer review them; peer review happens if and when the work is submitted to a journal.
explanation_incorrect: arXiv does not conduct peer review in any field, does not reject all unreviewed work (that is its main content), and does not gate this on payment.

question: Which laboratory launched bioRxiv in 2013, and what does the server host?
option_a: Cornell University; mathematics preprints
option_b: The Broad Institute; clinical trials
option_c: Cold Spring Harbor Lab; life sciences
option_d: CERN; physics data
option_e: EMBL; chemistry articles
explanation_correct: bioRxiv was launched in 2013 by Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory as an open preprint server for the life sciences; in 2025 its operation moved to a new nonprofit, openRxiv.
explanation_incorrect: CERN runs services like Zenodo, and arXiv (Cornell-hosted for years) covers the quantitative sciences; bioRxiv is specifically life sciences.

question: What is a common relationship between preprints and subsequent journal publication?
option_a: Preprints are expected to entirely replace journals as a publishing venue
option_b: Journals, as a matter of policy, always reject work that was preprinted
option_c: Posting a preprint automatically triggers a retraction of the work
option_d: Preprinted work can never subsequently be submitted to a journal
option_e: Many preprints are later published in journals, remaining available
explanation_correct: Preprinting and journal publication are typically complementary: a large share of preprints are subsequently published in journals, while the preprint version remains publicly accessible and citable.
explanation_incorrect: Preprinting does not preclude journal submission, retract the work, or universally trigger rejection; most journals now accommodate preprinted manuscripts.

question: COAR Notify is an initiative to:
option_a: It provides an automated API specifically for detecting plagiarism
option_b: It is a service specifically responsible for assigning DOIs to preprints
option_c: It produces a formal ranking of repositories based on their overall size
option_d: It is an initiative aimed at entirely replacing institutional repositories
option_e: Standardize messages linking repositories with peer-review services
explanation_correct: COAR Notify, from the Confederation of Open Access Repositories, develops community conventions built on W3C Linked Data Notifications so repositories can interoperate with external services such as overlay journals and peer-review platforms in a decentralized way.
explanation_incorrect: It is about interoperable notifications, not ranking repositories, minting DOIs, replacing repositories, or plagiarism detection.

question: 'Open peer review' can refer to several practices. Which is one recognized form?
option_a: Reviewer identities and/or review reports are made public rather than kept anonymous
option_b: Reviews of the study are formally conducted before it has even been designed
option_c: Only the handling editor is permitted to read the submitted reviews
option_d: All review reports are permanently destroyed once a decision is made
option_e: Authors are given the opportunity to formally review their own papers
explanation_correct: Open peer review is an umbrella term for practices that increase transparency, such as revealing reviewer identities, publishing the review reports alongside the article, or both, in contrast to traditional closed anonymous review.
explanation_incorrect: Open peer review does not mean destroying reviews, restricting them to the editor, reviewing before study design, or self-review.
review_note: 'Open peer review' has many definitions (Ross-Hellauer 2017). JOSS is cited as an example of open review; consider a dedicated source on the taxonomy.

question: In 2025, operational responsibility for bioRxiv and medRxiv moved to which type of entity?
option_a: The two preprint servers were permanently shut down entirely
option_b: A large, established commercial academic publisher
option_c: A newly established nonprofit organization (openRxiv)
option_d: A government agency responsible for scientific publishing
option_e: A body that produces annual rankings of universities
explanation_correct: In 2025, stewardship of bioRxiv and its sister site medRxiv transferred from Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory to openRxiv, a newly created independent nonprofit set up to sustain the servers.
explanation_incorrect: The servers were not sold to a commercial publisher, taken over by a government or ranking body, or shut down.

question: Overlay journals, which COAR Notify and platforms like Episciences support, are best described as:
option_a: Journals that place paid advertisements directly on top of their articles
option_b: Journals that operate with no formal editors overseeing content at all
option_c: Journals that exclusively publish raw datasets rather than articles
option_d: Journals whose articles live on preprint servers, not the journal's own hosting
option_e: Traditional print journals that happen to feature digital cover designs
explanation_correct: An overlay journal organizes peer review and editorial selection but points to versions of record hosted on external preprint servers or repositories, layering a journal's curation 'over' existing open content.
explanation_incorrect: Overlay journals are about where content is hosted and curated, not advertising, print formats, dataset-only scope, or the absence of editors.

question: Why is citing research software with a persistent identifier increasingly encouraged?
option_a: Research software is fundamentally incapable of ever being cited
option_b: Citing software this way is believed to directly raise a journal's impact factor
option_c: Citing research software with an identifier is legally mandated in every country
option_d: Research software is understood to have no identifiable individual authors
option_e: So that software creators receive credit and others can identify the exact version used
explanation_correct: Citing software (e.g., via a DOI to an archived release) gives developers scholarly credit and lets readers identify and reuse the exact version, supporting reproducibility.
explanation_incorrect: Software can and increasingly should be cited; it has authors, and citing it is about credit and reproducibility, not journal metrics or a universal legal mandate.
review_note: URL is the FORCE11 Software Citation Principles announcement; confirm the exact link resolves before publishing.

question: What is a CITATION.cff file?
option_a: A software license that explicitly forbids any form of reuse
option_b: A configuration file used to set up continuous-integration pipelines
option_c: A plain-text YAML file that tells people how to cite the software
option_d: A citation that refers specifically to a single associated paper only
option_e: A compiled, ready-to-run binary version of the software
explanation_correct: A CITATION.cff file is a human- and machine-readable YAML file placed in a repository that provides citation metadata (authors, title, version, DOI) so the software can be cited correctly.
explanation_incorrect: It is not a binary, a restrictive license, a CI configuration, or limited to citing a single paper.

question: The Journal of Open Source Software (JOSS) is unusual because it:
option_a: It publishes research exclusively within the field of physics
option_b: It does not employ any form of formal peer review at all
option_c: It only accepts and publishes closed-source, proprietary code
option_d: Is a diamond OA journal that peer-reviews the software itself
option_e: It charges authors unusually high publication fees to submit
explanation_correct: JOSS is a free-to-read, free-to-publish (diamond OA) journal whose open peer review, conducted on GitHub, evaluates the software package itself alongside a short paper.
explanation_incorrect: JOSS charges no author fees, requires open-source submissions, does use (open) peer review, and is not discipline-limited.

question: When a GitHub repository contains a CITATION.cff file, what happens on GitHub?
option_a: A DOI is automatically and immediately minted for the repository
option_b: The underlying code is automatically sent out for formal peer review
option_c: Nothing at all happens; GitHub simply ignores the file entirely
option_d: The entire repository is automatically archived and preserved
option_e: GitHub displays a 'Cite this repository' option generating formatted citations
explanation_correct: GitHub reads a CITATION.cff file and surfaces a 'Cite this repository' button that offers formatted citations (e.g., APA and BibTeX) drawn from the file's metadata.
explanation_incorrect: GitHub does not ignore the file, auto-archive the repo, peer review the code, or mint DOIs by itself (Zenodo integration handles DOIs separately).

question: How does the JOSS review process primarily differ from a traditional journal's?
option_a: It reviews only the manuscript's abstract and nothing else
option_b: It is conducted openly in public GitHub issues, reviewing the software itself
option_c: It is conducted as a closed, double-blind process like most journals
option_d: The outcome is decided solely by the software's accumulated citation count
option_e: The submitted software package has no assigned reviewers at all
explanation_correct: JOSS reviews take place openly in public GitHub issues, where reviewers check that the software installs, runs, is documented, and is reusable, in addition to a short accompanying paper.
explanation_incorrect: JOSS review is open rather than closed/blind, does use reviewers, examines the whole package rather than just an abstract, and is not decided by metrics.

question: A researcher wants a citable, archived snapshot of a specific software release with a DOI. A common approach is to:
option_a: Archive the release on Zenodo via GitHub to mint a DOI
option_b: Simply email the source code directly to a journal's editor
option_c: Post the software as a preprint manuscript on arXiv
option_d: Register a personal ORCID iD specifically for the software
option_e: Rely on the CITATION.cff file itself to formally issue a DOI
explanation_correct: Connecting a GitHub repository to Zenodo automatically archives each tagged release and assigns it a DataCite DOI, producing a citable, preserved snapshot of that exact version.
explanation_incorrect: Emailing code, ORCID (for people), and arXiv are not archival DOI-minting routes; CITATION.cff records how to cite but does not itself issue a DOI.

question: The FORCE11 Software Citation Principles were created chiefly to:
option_a: Establish software as a legitimate, citable research product
option_b: Formally ban the citation of research software in publications
option_c: Define the syntax and rules of a new programming language
option_d: Replace traditional journal articles entirely with raw code
option_e: Set standardized licensing fees for research software
explanation_correct: The Software Citation Principles (FORCE11, 2016) articulate that research software is a legitimate scholarly product deserving citation, and set out principles such as importance, credit, unique identification, and accessibility.
explanation_incorrect: They promote (not ban) software citation, do not replace articles, define no language, and set no fees.
review_note: Confirm the exact FORCE11 URL and 2016 publication framing before publishing.

question: Which fiscal/organizational sponsor is associated with JOSS and Open Journals?
option_a: Springer Nature
option_b: The European Commission
option_c: Elsevier
option_d: Clarivate
option_e: NumFOCUS, a nonprofit
explanation_correct: JOSS is published under Open Journals and operates with fiscal sponsorship from NumFOCUS, a nonprofit that supports open-source scientific computing projects.
explanation_incorrect: JOSS is not backed by the commercial publishers listed or directly by the European Commission.

question: A key reproducibility reason to archive software in a repository like Zenodo rather than only linking to GitHub is that:
option_a: GitHub repositories are not indexed or made visible to search engines
option_b: Zenodo automatically executes and runs the archived code for the user
option_c: GitHub, as a platform, does not permit projects to use open licenses
option_d: GitHub repositories can be changed, moved, or deleted
option_e: Zenodo formally peer reviews the quality of the archived software
explanation_correct: A GitHub URL points to a mutable, deletable repository, so it is not a reliable long-term reference; archiving a specific release in Zenodo yields a preserved, version-pinned snapshot with a DOI suitable for citation and reproducibility.
explanation_incorrect: The issue is mutability/preservation, not search visibility, code execution, licensing support, or peer review (Zenodo does not review software).

question: Plan S, launched in 2018, is best described as:
option_a: A specific pricing plan set independently by individual academic publishers
option_b: A ranking system that compares universities by their research output
option_c: A technical standard governing the format of persistent identifiers
option_d: An initiative by research funders requiring immediate open access
option_e: A centralized repository used specifically for storing research data
explanation_correct: Plan S is an initiative launched in 2018 by cOAlition S, a group of research funders coordinated with Science Europe, requiring that publications from funded research be made immediately open access with no embargo.
explanation_incorrect: Plan S is a funder policy, not a publisher price plan, identifier standard, ranking, or repository.

question: The 2022 US OSTP memo (the 'Nelson memo') is best known for requiring that federally funded research be:
option_a: Kept under a mandatory 12-month embargo before being made public
option_b: Formally sold to recover the government's research costs
option_c: Made accessible only to employees of the federal government
option_d: Published exclusively in journals based within the United States
option_e: Made freely available immediately, with no embargo period
explanation_correct: The 2022 OSTP memo directs federal agencies to make peer-reviewed publications from funded research freely available immediately upon publication, eliminating the previously allowed 12-month embargo.
explanation_incorrect: The memo removes the embargo rather than keeping it, and does not restrict journals to the US, limit access to employees, or require sales.

question: What is the UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science (2021)?
option_a: A registry specifically responsible for assigning persistent identifiers
option_b: The first international standard-setting instrument on open science
option_c: An annual ranking that compares universities by their openness
option_d: A binding international treaty enforced through formal sanctions
option_e: An academic journal that is directly published by UNESCO itself
explanation_correct: Adopted in November 2021 by UNESCO's member states, the Recommendation is described as the first international standard-setting instrument on open science, providing shared definitions, values, and principles.
explanation_incorrect: It is a recommendation (not a sanction-backed treaty), not a journal, ranking, or identifier registry.

question: Under Plan S, what is the general stance on publishing in 'hybrid' journals?
option_a: This particular stance is understood to apply only to academic books
option_b: Compliant only under a transitional 'transformative' arrangement
option_c: Hybrid journals are formally banned outright for every researcher
option_d: Publishing through the hybrid route is, in fact, the preferred path
option_e: Publishing in a hybrid journal is, in every case, considered compliant
explanation_correct: Plan S does not consider standalone hybrid publishing compliant; it only accepted hybrid open access within time-limited transformative arrangements intended to transition journals fully to open access.
explanation_incorrect: Hybrid is neither preferred nor automatically compliant; Plan S restricts (rather than universally bans) it and applies broadly to research articles.

question: A core mechanism the Nelson memo emphasizes, beyond free access to publications, is:
option_a: Entirely eliminating the practice of formal peer review
option_b: Requiring that individual readers be charged for accessing data
option_c: Immediate access to underlying data and use of persistent identifiers
option_d: Deliberately restricting access to persistent identifiers
option_e: Extending the length of embargoes that apply to shared data
explanation_correct: Alongside zero-embargo access to publications, the 2022 OSTP guidance emphasizes providing access to the underlying data and encourages persistent identifiers for researchers, awards, and outputs.
explanation_incorrect: The memo shortens rather than lengthens data timelines, promotes rather than restricts PIDs, and says nothing about ending peer review or charging readers.

question: Which service did Jisc launch in November 2024 to replace Sherpa Romeo, Sherpa Juliet, and Sherpa Fact?
option_a: Unpaywall, a browser extension for finding open articles
option_b: Open Policy Finder
option_c: OpenAlex, an open scholarly metadata index
option_d: re3data, a registry of data repositories
option_e: DOAJ, a directory of open access journals
explanation_correct: In November 2024 Jisc combined its Sherpa services (Romeo for publisher OA policies, Juliet for funder policies, and Fact) into a single platform called Open Policy Finder.
explanation_incorrect: Unpaywall, OpenAlex, re3data, and DOAJ are separate services not created from the Sherpa consolidation.

question: How does the cOAlition S Rights Retention Strategy interact with a subscription journal's embargo?
option_a: A prior CC BY licence lets authors share the AAM immediately, overriding the embargo
option_b: This mechanism only ever becomes relevant once the embargo period has fully ended
option_c: This particular mechanism has, in practice, no meaningful effect on embargoes at all
option_d: This mechanism works by requiring authors to pay the publisher directly for compliance
option_e: This particular mechanism works by lengthening the publisher's original embargo period
explanation_correct: Because grantees grant a CC BY license to their AAM before submission (a prior obligation to the funder), they can deposit it openly on acceptance regardless of a publisher's stated embargo, enabling zero-embargo green OA.
explanation_incorrect: The strategy defeats rather than lengthens or waits out embargoes, and it avoids, rather than requires, additional payment to the publisher.

question: Which statement about the UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science is most accurate?
option_a: It is a recommendation whose provisions apply only to European countries
option_b: It formally mandates specific article processing charge amounts for journals
option_c: It frames open science broadly, including infrastructures and societal engagement
option_d: It defines open science narrowly as simply meaning open access to journal articles
option_e: It is intended to formally replace existing national research laws
explanation_correct: The UNESCO Recommendation takes a broad view of open science, encompassing open access and open data but also open infrastructures, open engagement of societal actors, and open dialogue with other knowledge systems, including Indigenous knowledge.
explanation_incorrect: It is not limited to article access or to Europe, sets no APC levels, and is a recommendation rather than a replacement for national law.

question: As of the mid-2020s, what was the implementation status of the US federal public access (Nelson memo) requirements?
option_a: The Nelson memo, in fact, was never implemented by any federal agency
option_b: Implementation of the memo's requirements was formally postponed all the way to 2040
option_c: Every existing agency public-access policy was formally struck down
option_d: Zero-embargo policies expected by end of 2025, mostly in effect
option_e: Only a single federal agency ever formally adopted these requirements
explanation_correct: Agencies were directed to have compliant public-access policies (no embargo) in place by roughly the end of 2025; by 2026 most agency policies were in effect, with some still being finalized or paused pending further guidance.
explanation_incorrect: The requirements were not struck down, ignored, limited to a single agency, or deferred to 2040.
review_note: Federal policy implementation can shift with administration guidance; re-verify current status before publishing.

question: Why do scholarly systems use persistent identifiers instead of relying on ordinary web addresses (URLs)?
option_a: URLs load more slowly across the internet than persistent identifiers do, especially on slow connections
option_b: Ordinary URLs often break when websites are reorganized, but persistent identifiers do not
option_c: URLs are technically incapable of ever pointing to PDF files or other document formats online
option_d: Copyright law legally requires that persistent identifiers be used for all formally published academic work
option_e: A URL can only ever be opened and used by one single reader at any given moment in time
explanation_correct: Web addresses change when sites are redesigned or content moves ('link rot'). A persistent identifier is a stable name whose registered target URL can be updated behind the scenes, so citations and links keep working.
explanation_incorrect: PIDs are not about speed, file formats, or legal requirements; they solve the problem of references breaking over time.

question: What kinds of things are RRIDs (Research Resource Identifiers) used to identify?
option_a: Key research resources used in a study, such as antibodies and cell lines
option_b: Research funding agencies that provide grants to universities
option_c: Peer-reviewed journals that publish the resulting articles
option_d: University departments where the research takes place
option_e: Individual journal articles describing the study's findings
explanation_correct: RRIDs uniquely identify key research resources cited in the methods sections of papers - for example antibodies, cell lines, model organisms, plasmids, and software tools - so studies can be reproduced and resources tracked across the literature.
explanation_incorrect: Articles get DOIs, funders and institutions get ROR IDs, and journals get ISSNs; RRIDs are specifically for the research resources used to conduct experiments.
review_note: Confirm rrids.org as the canonical link; scicrunch.org/resources is an alternative.

question: How does an individual researcher typically get a DOI assigned to one of their outputs?
option_a: By sending an email directly to the DOI Foundation and requesting one be issued
option_b: By depositing the output with a service that is a member of a DOI registration agency
option_c: By signing up for and paying an annual personal DOI subscription fee to Crossref
option_d: By registering the DOI themselves directly within the underlying Handle System
option_e: By simply filling in and submitting a free registration form directly on doi.org
explanation_correct: Individuals do not register DOIs directly. DOIs are registered by members of registration agencies - for example, depositing a dataset in Zenodo gets it a DataCite DOI, and publishing with a Crossref member journal gets the article a Crossref DOI.
explanation_incorrect: The DOI Foundation and doi.org do not take registrations from individuals, and there is no personal DOI subscription; registration happens through member organizations.

question: What does an ORCID iD look like?
option_a: A username the researcher freely chooses for themselves, similar to @jsmith on social media
option_b: A 16-digit number displayed in four groups of four, expressed as a URL
