option_c: An email address that is automatically generated and always ends in @orcid.org
option_d: A DNA-style barcode image that is printed on the researcher's institutional ID card
option_e: A fixed 10-character alphanumeric code that always begins with the letters 'OR'
explanation_correct: An ORCID iD is a 16-character identifier displayed in four groups of four digits (the last character may be an X), expressed as a URL, e.g. https://orcid.org/0000-0002-1825-0097.
explanation_incorrect: ORCID iDs are assigned numbers, not chosen usernames or email addresses, and they follow a fixed 16-digit format.

question: What does it cost an individual researcher to register and use an ORCID iD?
option_a: A one-time registration fee charged when the account is first created
option_b: An annual subscription fee billed directly to the researcher
option_c: A small fee charged per publication linked to the ORCID record
option_d: It is free only for researchers based in low-income countries
option_e: Nothing - registration and use are free for individuals
explanation_correct: ORCID iDs are free for individual researchers to register and use. ORCID is sustained by fees from member organizations (universities, publishers, funders) that integrate with the registry.
explanation_incorrect: There are no individual charges anywhere in the world; the membership model applies to organizations, not people.

question: Conceptually, what is the key difference between a DOI and a URL?
option_a: There is no real difference; the two terms are simply used interchangeably
option_b: A DOI only works when accessed from within a university library
option_c: A URL is inherently more permanent and stable over time than a DOI
option_d: A DOI contains the full text of the article embedded inside it
option_e: A DOI is a persistent name for the object itself, unlike a URL
explanation_correct: A DOI identifies the object (an article, dataset, etc.) independently of where it is hosted; resolution maps that name to the current location. A URL identifies a location, which can change or disappear.
explanation_incorrect: URLs are generally less stable than DOIs, DOIs contain no content themselves, and the two are related but not interchangeable concepts.

question: What does an ISSN identify?
option_a: An individual edition of a specific printed book
option_b: A single article published within a journal
option_c: An individual human researcher working in a field
option_d: A serial publication such as a journal or magazine
option_e: A single research dataset deposited in a repository
explanation_correct: The ISSN (International Standard Serial Number) identifies serial publications - journals, magazines, newspapers - as ongoing titles; print and electronic versions of the same journal get separate ISSNs.
explanation_incorrect: Books get ISBNs, articles typically get DOIs, researchers get ORCID iDs, and datasets usually get DOIs; the ISSN is specifically for serial titles.

question: When you deposit a dataset in the Zenodo repository, which persistent identifier is automatically created for it?
option_a: An ISSN, since Zenodo primarily indexes serial publications
option_b: A DOI (registered through DataCite)
option_c: An ORCID iD identifying whoever made the deposit
option_d: An RRID for the specific resource that was deposited
option_e: An ISBN, since Zenodo treats deposits like books
explanation_correct: Zenodo automatically registers a DataCite DOI for every deposit, making the dataset citable and persistently findable.
explanation_incorrect: ORCID iDs identify people, RRIDs identify research resources like antibodies, and ISBNs/ISSNs identify books and serials - none of these are minted for a dataset deposit.

question: Who decides what information on an ORCID record is publicly visible?
option_a: The researcher's current academic employer or institution
option_b: The researcher's national government or funding ministry
option_c: The publisher of the researcher's most recent article
option_d: ORCID staff members who review each record individually
option_e: The researcher who owns the record
explanation_correct: ORCID records are controlled by the individual researcher, who can set each item's visibility to 'everyone', 'trusted parties', or 'only me'.
explanation_incorrect: Employers, publishers, and ORCID itself can add data only with the researcher's permission, and the researcher always controls visibility.

question: Which statement about what DOIs can be assigned to is accurate?
option_a: DOIs are reserved for peer-reviewed journal articles only
option_b: DOIs can only ever be assigned to purely digital-born content
option_c: DOIs are only available for outputs published less than ten years ago
option_d: DOIs can only be assigned to works written in the English language
option_e: DOIs are assigned to many output types, not just journal articles
explanation_correct: The DOI system identifies objects of any type - journal articles, books and chapters, datasets, software releases, preprints, reports, and even physical objects - regardless of language or age; older works are often assigned DOIs retrospectively.
explanation_incorrect: There are no restrictions to articles, digital-born content, English, or recent works; the system is deliberately general.

question: In the DOI '10.5281/zenodo.123456', what does the '10.5281' part represent?
option_a: The exact year in which the DOI was first registered
option_b: The total number of times the object has been cited elsewhere
option_c: The prefix identifying the registrant
option_d: A checksum digit used purely for error detection purposes
option_e: The object's total file size measured in megabytes
explanation_correct: Every DOI has a prefix (starting '10.') that is assigned to a registrant by a registration agency, and a suffix chosen by the registrant; 10.5281 is the prefix used by Zenodo.
explanation_incorrect: The prefix carries no information about dates, citations, file size, or checksums; it simply identifies who registered the DOI.

question: What does it mean for an identifier to be 'resolvable'?
option_a: It can be shortened into a compact link for social media sharing
option_b: It automatically expires and must be renewed on an annual basis
option_c: Entering it in a web browser leads you to the object it identifies
option_d: It can be automatically translated into a variety of other languages
option_e: It is simultaneously stored and duplicated across multiple databases
explanation_correct: A resolvable identifier can be turned into an actionable link - e.g. https://doi.org/10.1000/182 or https://ror.org/02mhbdp94 - that takes you to the identified object or a page describing it.
explanation_incorrect: Resolvability is about the identifier leading somewhere useful on the web, not about translation, expiry, shortening, or replication.
review_note: Factsheet URL structure changes occasionally; verify link resolves.

question: What does a ROR identifier look like?
option_a: ROR:1234-5678-9012, formatted like a credit card number
option_b: An email-style address, such as smu@ror.org, unique to the institution
option_c: A 16-digit number displayed in four separate blocks
option_d: https://ror.org/ followed by a 9-character string
option_e: ror.org/ followed directly by the institution's full official name
explanation_correct: A ROR ID is a URL of the form https://ror.org/ plus a unique 9-character alphanumeric string, e.g. https://ror.org/02mhbdp94; the string is opaque and deliberately does not encode the organization's name.
explanation_incorrect: ROR IDs do not use the 16-digit ORCID format, institution names, or email-style syntax; they are short opaque strings under the ror.org domain.

question: Why do many journals ask authors to provide an ORCID iD during manuscript submission?
option_a: To verify and confirm that the author has already paid the manuscript submission fee
option_b: To reliably link the publication to the correct person even when names are shared or change
option_c: To confirm the author's institutional affiliation is prestigious enough
option_d: To let editors quickly check the author's citation count before assigning peer reviewers
option_e: Because providing an ORCID iD is a legal requirement for authors in most countries
explanation_correct: Names are ambiguous: many researchers share names, and names change. An ORCID iD distinguishes the individual unambiguously and allows systems (publishers, funders, Crossref) to push verified publication data to the researcher's record automatically.
explanation_incorrect: ORCID is unrelated to fees, prestige checks, or legal mandates, and it does not expose citation counts to editors; it exists to solve name ambiguity and enable trusted data exchange.

question: What is the difference between a Crossref DOI and a DataCite DOI?
option_a: DataCite DOIs are functionally different from Crossref DOIs and, as a result, cannot ever be cited within journal articles
option_b: Crossref DOIs are provided completely free of charge, while resolving a DataCite DOI requires an ongoing paid subscription
option_c: The two agencies operate entirely separate resolution systems, so a DataCite DOI and a Crossref DOI are technically incompatible
option_d: Both are standard DOIs; the difference is the registration agency and the community each primarily serves
option_e: Crossref DOIs are permanent and never expire, whereas DataCite DOIs are only valid for a fixed number of years before lapsing
explanation_correct: Crossref and DataCite are both DOI registration agencies; a DOI from either resolves identically through the DOI system. They differ in the communities served and the metadata schema used - Crossref centers on published literature, DataCite on datasets, software, and similar outputs.
explanation_incorrect: All DOIs share one resolution system and do not expire; the distinction is organizational and metadata-related, not technical resolution or cost to resolve.

question: What is the main purpose of ORCID's 'trusted party' permissions?
option_a: To let ORCID sell a researcher's personal data directly to academic publishers for profit
option_b: To verify the researcher's passport identity before allowing them to create an account
option_c: To make the researcher's entire ORCID record permanently public with no privacy controls
option_d: To transfer full ownership of the researcher's record to their employing institution
option_e: To allow an authorized organization to read from or write to the ORCID record automatically
explanation_correct: Researchers can grant trusted organizations (publishers, funders, universities) permission to add validated information to their record or read limited-visibility data, enabling automatic, authenticated updates rather than manual entry.
explanation_incorrect: Trusted-party permissions do not transfer ownership, force full publicity, verify legal identity, or involve selling data; they are scoped, revocable authorizations.

question: An institution wants to reliably link all of its researchers' outputs to the institution in metadata. Which identifier best supports this?
option_a: The institution's RRID
option_b: Each researcher's ORCID iD
option_c: A DOI for the institution's website
option_d: The institution's ISSN
option_e: The institution's ROR ID
explanation_correct: ROR IDs identify research organizations, so including the institution's ROR ID in the affiliation metadata of outputs enables reliable, machine-readable linking of outputs to that institution regardless of how its name is written.
explanation_incorrect: ORCID identifies the individual researchers, not the institution; ISSNs, RRIDs, and website DOIs do not identify organizations for affiliation purposes.

question: How is a physical sample given a persistent identifier under the current IGSN implementation?
option_a: Through a completely separate resolver system that has nothing to do with DOIs
option_b: Through ORCID, which was extended in 2021 to also cover physical samples
option_c: As a DataCite DOI, following the IGSN's 2021 partnership with DataCite
option_d: As a Handle record that sits entirely outside the standard DOI namespace
option_e: As a ROR ID, since ROR was expanded to include physical samples in 2021
explanation_correct: Since the 2021 IGSN e.V.-DataCite partnership, IGSN IDs for material samples are implemented as DataCite DOIs with sample-appropriate metadata, giving physical samples the same infrastructure as other DataCite outputs.
explanation_incorrect: IGSN IDs are no longer a wholly separate system and are not ORCID iDs, ROR IDs, or Handles outside the DOI namespace.

question: What problem does including a funder's identifier (e.g., via Crossref's Open Funder Registry / ROR funder records) in output metadata help solve?
option_a: It removes the need for the manuscript to undergo any form of peer review at all
option_b: It simply pads out the article's total word count without adding any real informational value
option_c: It enables funders and institutions to reliably track outputs arising from specific grants
option_d: It is the factor that determines exactly how much a journal will bill in processing charges
option_e: It automatically strips out and anonymizes the identities of every listed author
explanation_correct: Funder identifiers in metadata let funders and analysts link outputs back to specific funders and (with grant IDs) awards, supporting compliance monitoring and impact reporting without relying on inconsistent free-text funder names.
explanation_incorrect: Funder IDs do not affect review, APCs, word count, or anonymity; they solve the name-disambiguation problem for funding attribution.
review_note: Crossref has been migrating funder identification toward ROR; verify current framing of Open Funder Registry vs ROR.

question: Why does the same print and online version of a journal usually have two different ISSNs?
option_a: Because ISSNs expire after a fixed number of years and must be formally renewed
option_b: Because each ISSN is meant to identify one single issue rather than the whole title
option_c: Because the journal switched publishers, and each publisher assigns its own ISSN
option_d: Because one ISSN is intended for the authors and the other for the readers
option_e: Because separate ISSNs are assigned to different media versions of the same serial
explanation_correct: ISSN rules assign distinct ISSNs to different media (print vs electronic) of the same title; a 'linking ISSN' groups these versions together for collocation.
explanation_incorrect: ISSNs are not per-issue, per-audience, or renewal-based; the two-ISSN situation reflects distinct media manifestations of one title.

question: What is a DOI 'suffix' and who assigns it?
option_a: A checksum digit that is calculated and assigned by the DOI Foundation
option_b: A short code automatically assigned by the reader's own web browser
option_c: The article's citation count, which is appended to the DOI automatically
option_d: The registrant-chosen string after the prefix and slash
option_e: The publication year, which Crossref inserts into the DOI automatically
explanation_correct: A DOI has a prefix (assigned by the registration agency) and a suffix chosen by the registrant; suffixes can be arbitrary opaque strings or follow a local scheme (e.g., zenodo.123456), as long as they are unique under that prefix.
explanation_incorrect: The suffix is not a checksum, citation count, browser code, or automatically assigned year; it is controlled by the registrant.

question: Why is it recommended to display DOIs as full https URLs (e.g., https://doi.org/10.xxxx) rather than 'doi:10.xxxx'?
option_a: Because writing a DOI using the 'doi:' prefix is considered technically illegal
option_b: Because the 'doi:' form actually resolves to an entirely different object
option_c: Because using a full https URL is legally required to protect the work's copyright
option_d: Because the URL form is simply a few characters shorter to type out
option_e: Because the URL form is clickable/actionable and follows current DOI display guidelines
explanation_correct: Current DOI display guidance recommends presenting DOIs as full https://doi.org/ URLs so they are directly clickable and actionable in web and PDF contexts; the older 'doi:' prefix form is not directly clickable.
explanation_incorrect: The doi: form is not illegal and resolves to the same object; the recommendation is about usability and being actionable, not length or copyright.
review_note: Verify the DOI Handbook / display guidelines URL resolves; DOI.org restructured its site.

question: What kind of identifier is an ARK (Archival Resource Key)?
option_a: A standard used exclusively for identifying user accounts on social media platforms
option_b: A special type of ORCID iD reserved specifically for identifying institutions
option_c: A specific subtype of the ISSN reserved only for archival and museum materials
option_d: An identifier scheme that can only ever be issued directly by Crossref itself
option_e: A persistent identifier scheme, often used by libraries and archives, with no DOI-style fee model
explanation_correct: ARKs are persistent identifiers used widely by memory institutions; any organization can become a Name Assigning Authority, and ARKs can identify digital, physical, or conceptual objects without the registration-agency fee structure of DOIs.
explanation_incorrect: ARKs are not ISSNs, ORCID-like person IDs, Crossref-exclusive, or social handles; they are a distinct low-cost PID scheme.

question: An author has published under several name variants over their career. How does an ORCID iD help readers and systems?
option_a: It provides a single stable identifier tying together all of the author's outputs
option_b: It forces every journal the author publishes with to use one fixed spelling
option_c: It automatically goes back and corrects the spelling in the author's old papers
option_d: It hides all of the author's earlier name variants from public view
option_e: It merges the author's record together with same-named colleagues' records
explanation_correct: The ORCID iD is a persistent identifier for the person, so outputs published under different name variants can all be connected to the one iD, disambiguating the author from others and across name changes.
explanation_incorrect: ORCID does not rewrite past papers, hide names, or merge distinct people; it links one person's outputs under a stable identifier.

question: What is RAiD (Research Activity Identifier) designed to identify?
option_a: Individual researchers, in the same way that ORCID identifies people
option_b: Physical samples, in the same way that IGSN identifiers are used
option_c: Research projects or activities, connecting the people and outputs involved
option_d: Journal articles only, in the same way that a DOI identifies a single work
option_e: Funding currencies used specifically to track international grant amounts
explanation_correct: RAiD is an ISO standard (ISO 23527) persistent identifier for research projects/activities, acting as a connecting record that links the contributors, outputs, funders, and facilities associated with a project over time.
explanation_incorrect: RAiD is not for people (ORCID), samples (IGSN), or single articles (DOI); it identifies the project/activity as a whole.
review_note: Confirm raid.org is canonical and ISO number (23527) is current.

question: What is the 'PID graph' concept in scholarly infrastructure?
option_a: A chart that ranks scholarly journals purely by how many DOIs each one registered last year
option_b: A visualization tool that maps out all of the citations received by one single article
option_c: A graph that tracks how quickly DOI resolution has sped up or slowed down over time
option_d: A proprietary analytics product that is developed, owned, and sold exclusively by Clarivate
option_e: The interconnected network formed when persistent identifiers are linked to each other via metadata
explanation_correct: The PID graph is the interconnected web created when PIDs (ORCID, DOI, ROR, grant IDs, etc.) reference one another in metadata, so machines can traverse relationships - e.g., from a person to their outputs to the funders and institutions involved.
explanation_incorrect: The PID graph is not a ranking, a single-article citation map, a proprietary vendor product, or a performance metric; it is the linked network of PIDs and their relationships.
review_note: FREYA project popularized 'PID graph'; verify a current canonical source if the FREYA site is stale.

question: Under the Handle System that underpins DOIs, what is a 'naming authority' (prefix)?
option_a: The internet service provider through which a reader happens to be connected
option_b: A checksum value used solely to detect typing errors in the identifier
option_c: A licensing body that is operated and run directly by a national government
option_d: The portion of a handle that identifies the entity responsible for a set of identifiers
option_e: The authority responsible for calculating a journal's official impact factor
explanation_correct: In the Handle System, a handle has a naming authority (prefix) and a local name (suffix). The DOI system operates the '10' naming authority and delegates sub-prefixes to registrants who manage their own local names.
explanation_incorrect: The naming authority is a delegation mechanism within the Handle System, not an ISP, government body, checksum, or metric authority.

question: Why can two different DOIs legitimately point to closely related versions of the same work (e.g., a preprint and the published article)?
option_a: It is simply a registration error, and one of the two duplicate DOIs should always be deleted
option_b: Because preprints are not considered eligible to be assigned a DOI in the first place
option_c: Because DOIs identify specific objects or versions, and their relationships are expressed in metadata
option_d: Because DOIs are occasionally and randomly duplicated by mistake within the registration system
option_e: Because the doi.org resolver sometimes automatically splits one registered DOI into two separate ones
explanation_correct: Different versions or manifestations are distinct objects and can each carry their own DOI; the relationship between them (such as a preprint being a version of the article) is captured in relation metadata so systems can link them.
explanation_incorrect: Version DOIs are intentional, not errors or duplicates; preprints can and do have DOIs, and the resolver does not split identifiers.

question: Zenodo issues both a version-specific DOI and a 'concept DOI' for a record. What does the concept DOI do?
option_a: It always resolves to the latest version of the record
option_b: It is a special DOI that identifies the author rather than the deposit itself
option_c: It applies only to software deposits and is never issued for datasets
option_d: It automatically expires the moment a new version is uploaded
option_e: It points to a single, randomly chosen version among all the versions
explanation_correct: Zenodo mints a concept DOI that represents the record across all its versions and resolves to the most recent version, alongside a distinct DOI for each specific version, letting citers reference either 'this exact version' or 'the record in general'.
explanation_incorrect: The concept DOI is stable, not random or expiring, applies to all deposit types, and identifies the record's version group, not the author.
review_note: Verify Zenodo help URL path (site was migrated).

question: What distinguishes ISNI from ORCID, given both can identify people?
option_a: ISNI can only ever be used to identify researchers who are currently alive, and cannot be applied to anyone who has passed away
option_b: ISNI is technically incapable of identifying the authors of creative, scholarly, or artistic works of any kind
option_c: ISNI is a broad ISO standard covering many creative and scholarly domains, unlike the research-focused ORCID
option_d: The two registries are operated by exactly the same organization, share one combined database, and are functionally identical in every respect
option_e: ORCID has existed for considerably longer than ISNI, having been established first and later adopted much more widely
explanation_correct: ISNI (ISO 27729) identifies the public identities of contributors across books, music, film, and research, often assigned by agencies; ORCID targets the research community with researcher-controlled records and deep integration into scholarly workflows. ORCID iDs also exist within the ISNI number space by arrangement.
explanation_incorrect: ISNI is not limited to living researchers or unable to identify authors, and the two are distinct organizations serving different (though overlapping) scopes.

question: Why is metadata quality, not just the existence of a DOI, considered critical for persistent identifiers to deliver value?
option_a: Because rich, accurate, openly available metadata is what enables discovery, linking, and analytics across the PID graph
option_b: Readers are technically unable to click on or follow a DOI link at all unless rich metadata has first been attached to it
option_c: Metadata is actually the main factor that determines the price a registrant is charged to register a DOI
option_d: A DOI will completely and permanently stop resolving the very moment any of its associated metadata is removed
option_e: Metadata is actually the primary factor used by Clarivate to calculate a journal's official impact factor score
explanation_correct: A DOI alone only resolves to a location; the connective and analytic value - finding, citing, linking outputs to people, institutions, and funders - comes from complete, accurate, open metadata registered with the identifier.
explanation_incorrect: DOIs still resolve without rich metadata and their clickability is independent of it; metadata does not set prices or impact factors, but it is what makes the PID ecosystem useful.

question: The DOI system's global resolution is administered by which body, and through what technical infrastructure?
option_a: Crossref, using its own internally built proprietary database system
option_b: ORCID, through calls made directly to its public researcher API
option_c: The DONA Foundation, which administers the Global Handle Registry
option_d: ICANN, through the same infrastructure that underpins the domain name system
option_e: UNESCO, through a dedicated international treaty-based registry
explanation_correct: The DOI system relies on the Handle System's Global Handle Registry, administered under the DONA Foundation (with multiple credentialed administrators), for the global resolution layer; Crossref and DataCite are registration agencies operating above it.
explanation_incorrect: Resolution is not run by Crossref, ORCID, ICANN's DNS, or UNESCO; it uses the DONA-administered Handle infrastructure.

question: What is a key governance implication of ORCID iDs residing within the ISNI number range?
option_a: ORCID iDs are drawn from a reserved block of the ISNI namespace so the two schemes do not collide
option_b: It means ORCID and ISNI numbers can be used completely interchangeably across every system
option_c: It means ISNI has the technical ability to delete or modify records held by ORCID
option_d: It means researchers must pay a fee to ISNI in order to keep their ORCID iD active
option_e: It means every ORCID record is ultimately owned and controlled by ISNI's governing agency
explanation_correct: By arrangement, ORCID iDs occupy a reserved range within the ISNI space to guarantee non-collision, but ORCID governs its own registry and records remain under researcher control; the schemes are coordinated, not merged.
explanation_incorrect: ISNI does not own or delete ORCID records, there is no fee to ISNI, and the identifiers are not blindly interchangeable across all systems despite the shared number space.
review_note: Nuanced governance point; verify current ISNI-ORCID coordination language before publishing.

question: From a preservation standpoint, why is a persistent identifier's promise sometimes described as a 'social' commitment rather than a purely technical guarantee?
option_a: Because persistence ultimately depends on organizations continuing to maintain the identifier's metadata and update its target
option_b: Because the underlying resolution technology itself is fundamentally unreliable and prone to frequent failure
option_c: Because which identifiers persist over time is effectively decided by popularity votes cast by users on social media
option_d: Because persistent identifiers are, in practice, a tool used almost exclusively by social scientists and no one else
option_e: Because the persistence of every identifier is permanently guaranteed as a matter of binding international law
explanation_correct: PID systems provide the mechanism for stable references, but a DOI only keeps working if a responsible party keeps its target and metadata current; persistence is therefore contingent on ongoing organizational commitment and funding, not automatic.
explanation_incorrect: The point is not that the technology fails or that law or popularity guarantees persistence; it is that stewardship must back the technical capability.

question: What challenge does 'PID metadata provenance' address in trust-sensitive workflows?
option_a: Storing copies of DOIs offline so that they remain available for use even during internet outages
option_b: Recording who asserted a given piece of metadata and by what authority
option_c: Automatically translating a piece of metadata into a wide range of other spoken languages
option_d: Compressing large metadata files down so that they take up considerably less storage space
option_e: Simply counting up the total number of DOIs that currently exist across the whole system
explanation_correct: Provenance metadata captures the source and authority of assertions - for instance, whether an affiliation was self-claimed or validated by the institution - which matters when systems make trust decisions such as auto-updating records or reporting compliance.
explanation_incorrect: Provenance is about the trustworthiness and origin of assertions, not storage, compression, translation, or counting of identifiers.
review_note: Confirm ORCID provenance documentation URL resolves.

question: What is metadata in the context of research data?
option_a: Structured information that describes data
option_b: The raw, completely unprocessed measurements that were collected during the study
option_c: A backup copy of the dataset that is kept stored on a separate server
option_d: The specific software program that was used to analyze the data
option_e: A formal citation that points to a separate, related journal article
explanation_correct: Metadata is 'data about data': descriptive information (title, creator, date, methods, variables, license) that makes a dataset findable, understandable, and reusable by people and machines.
explanation_incorrect: Metadata is not the raw data, a backup, analysis software, or a citation; it is the descriptive layer around the data.

question: Why are open file formats (e.g., CSV, TXT, PNG) generally preferred over proprietary ones (e.g., XLSX, a vendor's binary format) for long-term data preservation?
option_a: Open formats remain readable without needing specific commercial software
option_b: Proprietary formats are technically incapable of storing numeric values
option_c: Open formats are always smaller in file size than proprietary ones
option_d: Open formats automatically generate and attach metadata to the file
option_e: Open formats always compress data more efficiently than proprietary ones
explanation_correct: Open, non-proprietary formats are documented and not tied to particular software or vendors, so data stored in them is far more likely to remain accessible years later, even if specific applications disappear.
explanation_incorrect: The advantage is long-term readability and independence from specific software, not guaranteed smaller size, compression, or automatic metadata.
review_note: Verify UK Data Service recommended-formats URL.

question: What is the '3-2-1 rule' commonly recommended for research data backup?
option_a: Use 3 passwords, 2 encryption keys, 1 firewall
option_b: Back up 3 times a day, 2 times a week, 1 time a month
option_c: Keep data for 3 years, then 2, then delete after 1
option_d: Keep 3 copies on 2 media types, with 1 copy off-site
option_e: Share with 3 people, 2 institutions, 1 repository
explanation_correct: The 3-2-1 backup rule advises keeping at least three copies of your data, stored on two different types of media or systems, with at least one copy off-site, to guard against loss from hardware failure, disaster, or error.
explanation_incorrect: 3-2-1 refers to copies, media types, and off-site storage - not a schedule, retention countdown, security stack, or sharing plan.
review_note: Verify DataONE best-practices URL; concept is widely documented.

question: What is a README file in a dataset?
option_a: A plain-text file explaining a dataset's contents and structure
option_b: A formal license agreement that governs how the dataset may be used
option_c: A log recording everyone who has downloaded the data
option_d: A compressed zip archive containing all of the data files
option_e: A citation for the dataset that is generated automatically by the repository
explanation_correct: A README is a plain-text (or Markdown) document accompanying a dataset that describes its files, variables, methods, units, and any information a future user needs to understand and correctly reuse the data.
explanation_incorrect: A README is documentation, not a license, download log, archive, or citation, though it may point to those.

question: What is a 'data repository'?
option_a: An introductory textbook used to teach the basic concepts of statistics
option_b: A software application used to manage and format bibliographic citations
option_c: A spreadsheet program used to enter figures and perform simple calculations
option_d: A type of external hard drive commonly used to back up personal files
option_e: An online service that stores and preserves research datasets
explanation_correct: A data repository is a platform (institutional, disciplinary, or general-purpose like Zenodo or Dryad) that curates and preserves datasets and makes them accessible, usually with a persistent identifier such as a DOI.
explanation_incorrect: A repository is not analysis software, a backup drive, a citation manager, or a textbook; it is a service for depositing and sharing data.

question: Why is it good practice to assign a license to a shared dataset?
option_a: Licenses substantially increase the overall file size of a dataset
option_b: Reusers may not know what they're legally allowed to do with the data
option_c: Licenses guarantee that the data itself is accurate and correct
option_d: Attaching a license automatically assigns the dataset a DOI
option_e: Licenses cause the dataset to be hidden from search engines
explanation_correct: An explicit license (e.g., CC0 or CC BY) tells reusers what they may legally do with the data; absent a clear license, reuse rights are uncertain and cautious users may avoid the data even if it is downloadable.
explanation_incorrect: Licensing addresses legal clarity, not file size, correctness, identifier assignment, or discoverability.

question: Which is an example of general (cross-disciplinary) data repository as opposed to a domain-specific one?
option_a: GenBank (for genetic sequences)
option_b: Protein Data Bank (for protein structures)
option_c: UniProt (for protein sequences)
option_d: Zenodo (accepts data from any field)
option_e: ICPSR (for social science data)
explanation_correct: Zenodo, Figshare, and Dryad are general-purpose repositories accepting data from any discipline, whereas GenBank, the Protein Data Bank, ICPSR, and UniProt are domain-specific repositories for particular data types.
explanation_incorrect: The other options are all domain-specific repositories tied to specific data types, not general-purpose ones.

question: When is the best time to start thinking about how research data will be managed?
option_a: Only after the data has already been permanently lost
option_b: At the proposal/planning stage, before data collection begins
option_c: Only when a funder formally audits the project's compliance
option_d: Only after the resulting paper has already been published
option_e: Only once the researcher has reached retirement age
explanation_correct: Data management is most effective when planned at the outset - in the proposal or project-planning stage - so that decisions about formats, storage, documentation, consent, and sharing are made before data is collected, not retrofitted later.
explanation_incorrect: Waiting until publication, an audit, data loss, or retirement forfeits the benefits of planning and risks unrecoverable problems.

question: What does 'sensitive data' typically refer to in research data management?
option_a: Any data file that happens to be larger than one gigabyte in total size
option_b: Data that could cause harm or legal issues if disclosed
option_c: Data that simply happens to be unusually difficult or time-consuming to analyze
option_d: Data that is, for the time being, simply stored locally on a researcher's own laptop
option_e: Data belonging to a study that has not yet gone through a full round of formal peer review
explanation_correct: Sensitive data includes personal or identifiable information, health records, location of endangered species, culturally protected knowledge, and similar - data whose disclosure could harm individuals or communities or breach law or ethics.
explanation_incorrect: Sensitivity concerns potential harm and legal/ethical constraints, not file size, storage location, analytic difficulty, or review status.
review_note: Verify UK Data Service legal-ethical URL.

question: What is the purpose of a 'data availability statement' in a journal article?
option_a: To provide a simple word count for the published article
option_b: To list the full names of the article's authors
option_c: To tell readers how the underlying data can be accessed
option_d: To state the journal's official impact factor score
option_e: To confirm that the authors' funding amount was sufficient
explanation_correct: A data availability statement tells readers where the data behind the findings can be found (e.g., a repository DOI), the conditions for access, or the reasons data cannot be shared, supporting transparency and reuse.
explanation_incorrect: It concerns access to the underlying data, not authorship, metrics, length, or funding adequacy.

question: Which license waiver is commonly recommended for maximizing reuse of research data specifically?
option_a: CC0, a public domain dedication with no conditions attached
option_b: A signed non-disclosure agreement between two parties
option_c: The GPL, a license originally designed for software
option_d: Marking the dataset 'all rights reserved' with no reuse permitted
option_e: CC BY-NC-ND, which restricts both commercial use and adaptation
explanation_correct: CC0 is widely recommended for data because it waives copyright and related rights entirely, avoiding attribution-stacking problems when data from many sources are combined; norms of scholarly citation still encourage crediting the source.
explanation_incorrect: CC BY-NC-ND restricts commercial and derivative use, GPL is a software license, 'all rights reserved' and NDAs block reuse; none maximizes data reuse the way CC0 does.

question: What is 'file naming convention' good practice intended to achieve?
option_a: To make the files completely and permanently invisible to every other person
option_b: To automatically assign a unique DOI to each and every individual file
option_c: To deliberately maximize the length of every single file name possible
option_d: To fully encrypt the contents of every file the moment it is created
option_e: To use consistent, descriptive, machine-friendly file names
explanation_correct: A file naming convention uses consistent, meaningful elements (project, date in ISO format, version, no spaces or special characters) so files remain sortable, findable, and understandable to collaborators and your future self.
explanation_incorrect: Good naming is about clarity and machine-friendliness, not hiding, encrypting, lengthening, or identifying files with DOIs.

question: What is 'version control' in the context of managing data and code?
option_a: Deleting every file except for the newest saved version
option_b: Automatically converting every file into PDF format
option_c: Tracking changes over time so you can see what changed and revert
option_d: Renaming files using a completely random naming scheme
option_e: Encrypting each saved version so it cannot be opened
explanation_correct: Version control tracks the history of changes to files (often with tools like Git for code), allowing collaborators to see and understand changes, avoid conflicting edits, and roll back to earlier states.
explanation_incorrect: Version control preserves history rather than deleting it, and is unrelated to encryption, random renaming, or format conversion.

question: Does making data 'FAIR' mean the data must be freely and openly available to everyone?
option_a: Yes, FAIR and open access are ultimately just two names for the same concept
option_b: Yes, FAIR-compliant data must always and without exception be released under a CC0 license
option_c: No - FAIR concerns findability, accessibility (under clearly stated conditions), interoperability, and reusability
option_d: No, FAIR data can never legitimately be shared with any other person or organization
option_e: Yes, but this openness requirement only applies to data from publicly funded research
explanation_correct: FAIR is about good data stewardship, not openness; the 'A' means accessible under clearly specified conditions, which can include authentication and authorization. Sensitive data can be FAIR while access is appropriately restricted ('as open as possible, as closed as necessary').
explanation_incorrect: FAIR is not synonymous with open, does not mandate CC0, and does not forbid sharing; access can be conditional.

question: What is a 'machine-actionable' Data Management Plan (maDMP)?
option_a: A DMP whose entire text has been written from start to finish by an artificial intelligence system
option_b: A DMP that exists only as a single, static, unstructured PDF document with no underlying structure
option_c: A DMP that is somehow capable of automatically running the project's statistical analysis itself
option_d: A DMP that has been written entirely and exclusively using formal programming code
option_e: A DMP whose information is structured so that systems can exchange, update, and act on it automatically
explanation_correct: Machine-actionable DMPs represent plan information in structured, standardized form (e.g., via the RDA DMP Common Standard) so that identifiers, systems, and services can read, populate, and update the plan across the research lifecycle instead of it being a one-off document.
explanation_incorrect: A maDMP is about structured, interoperable information exchange, not being written in code, run as software, saved as PDF, or authored by AI.
review_note: Verify RDA DMP Common Standards WG URL.

question: What does CoreTrustSeal certify?
option_a: That every dataset it hosts is factually accurate
option_b: That a data management plan has been formally approved
option_c: That a repository meets core trustworthy-repository requirements
option_d: That the data is stored using an open file format
option_e: That a researcher has completed mandatory data-handling training
explanation_correct: CoreTrustSeal is a certification a data repository can earn by demonstrating it meets the Core Trustworthy Data Repositories Requirements, covering areas like preservation, security, and quality - a signal of repository trustworthiness, not of any single dataset's accuracy.
explanation_incorrect: CoreTrustSeal assesses repositories, not the correctness of individual datasets, researcher training, file formats, or DMP approval.

question: For data that cannot be fully open due to privacy or ethics, what is 'controlled access' (managed access)?
option_a: Metadata is discoverable but the data is released only to approved users under conditions
option_b: Permanently deleting the entire dataset outright, so that it can never possibly be misused by anyone
option_c: Publishing the data completely openly after simply removing the participants' names from the file
option_d: Storing the data exclusively on paper, kept inside a single locked filing cabinet in one office
option_e: Sharing the data only privately and exclusively with the funder's own internal administrative staff
explanation_correct: Controlled access keeps sensitive data findable (open metadata) while gating the data behind an approval process - application, review, and a data-use agreement - so legitimate reuse is possible without exposing protected information.
explanation_incorrect: Controlled access is not deletion, is more than simple anonymization, and is not restricted to paper storage or funder-only sharing.
review_note: Verify UK Data Service access-control URL.

question: What is the difference between anonymization and pseudonymization of personal data?
option_a: Anonymization irreversibly removes identifiability; pseudonymization uses reversible codes instead
option_b: Anonymization and pseudonymization are, in practical terms, simply identical concepts describing the exact same process
option_c: Pseudonymization provides considerably stronger and more complete protection for privacy than full anonymization does
option_d: Anonymization is a technique that, by definition, can only ever be applied to photographic and video images
option_e: Pseudonymization works by permanently and irreversibly deleting the underlying personal data outright
explanation_correct: Truly anonymized data cannot be traced back to individuals and generally falls outside data-protection law; pseudonymized data uses codes/keys so re-identification remains possible with the key, so it is still regulated personal data.
explanation_incorrect: The two are not identical, anonymization is the stronger de-identification, and pseudonymization retains (does not delete) re-linkable data.

question: What is a 'data paper' (published in a data journal)?
option_a: A peer-reviewed publication that describes a dataset
option_b: A preliminary preprint version of a dataset that has not yet been formally published
option_c: A formal editorial notice retracting a previously published journal article
option_d: A published academic paper that does not actually contain any underlying data
option_e: A journal article notable mainly for being printed on recycled paper stock
explanation_correct: A data paper is a scholarly, citable, peer-reviewed article whose purpose is to describe a dataset in depth so others can find, understand, and reuse it; the emphasis is on the data and its documentation rather than on hypotheses or results.
explanation_incorrect: A data paper does contain/point to data, is a formal publication (not a preprint or retraction), and the term has nothing to do with the physical medium.

question: Which metadata standard is specifically used to describe datasets when minting DOIs through DataCite?
option_a: MARC21, a format used mainly in library catalogues
option_b: BibTeX, a format used for reference lists in LaTeX
option_c: ISSN-L, a linking identifier used for serial titles
option_d: The DataCite Metadata Schema
option_e: HTML, the basic markup language used for web pages
explanation_correct: DataCite defines its own Metadata Schema (a set of properties like Creator, Title, Publisher, PublicationYear, ResourceType) that registrants use when creating DOIs for datasets and other outputs through DataCite.
explanation_incorrect: MARC21 is for library catalog records, BibTeX for reference lists, HTML for web pages, and ISSN-L is an identifier - none is the DataCite dataset schema.

question: What is the Research Data Alliance (RDA)?
option_a: A commercial company whose main business is selling data-hosting and storage services
option_b: A single, centralized global data repository that stores every dataset in one place
option_c: A private philanthropic funder that distributes grants specifically for data collection projects
option_d: An international organization that builds infrastructure for open data sharing
option_e: A commercial ranking service that publishes annual rankings of data journals
explanation_correct: The RDA is a member-driven international organization whose working and interest groups develop and adopt community recommendations and standards (e.g., data citation, DMP common standards) to reduce barriers to data sharing across disciplines and borders.
explanation_incorrect: RDA is not a company, funder, repository, or ranking; it is a standards-and-community body.

question: Why is depositing data in a domain-specific repository (e.g., GenBank, PDB, ICPSR) often preferred over a general one when a suitable one exists?
option_a: Domain repositories enforce community metadata standards, formats, and curation appropriate to that data type
option_b: General repositories are not legally permitted to hold any research data whatsoever
option_c: General repositories are set up to automatically delete uploaded data after exactly one year
option_d: Domain-specific repositories are preferred mainly because they simply assign a larger number of DOIs
option_e: Domain repositories are always completely free to use, while general ones always charge fees
explanation_correct: Discipline-specific repositories apply the metadata standards, validation, formats, and curation expected in their field, making deposits more discoverable and reusable by that community; funders and journals often mandate them for specific data types.
explanation_incorrect: The preference is about fit-for-purpose standards and curation, not cost, retention, DOI counts, or any prohibition on general repositories.

question: What is a 'data dictionary' (codebook)?
option_a: A curated list recommending which specific data repositories a researcher ought to use for deposit
option_b: A document that defines each variable/field in a dataset
option_c: A general-purpose dictionary defining technical jargon that is commonly used within a given field
option_d: A complete, separately stored backup copy of the entire dataset kept on another server
option_e: A formal licensing agreement document that is attached directly alongside the dataset
explanation_correct: A data dictionary or codebook lists and defines every variable in a dataset (name, description, data type, units, permitted values, and codes for categories/missing data), which is essential for others to interpret and reuse the data accurately.
explanation_incorrect: It is variable-level documentation, not a jargon glossary, repository list, backup, or license.

question: Under the FAIR principles, what does 'Interoperable' primarily require of data and metadata?
option_a: That data and metadata use shared, standard formats so they can be combined
option_b: That the dataset in question has undergone a full round of formal peer review
option_c: That the data is made available to absolutely everyone completely free of charge
option_d: That the data is physically and exclusively stored within a single named country
option_e: That the dataset has accumulated an unusually high number of citations over time
explanation_correct: Interoperability means using standardized, formal, and widely used vocabularies, ontologies, and formats (and qualified references to other data/metadata) so that datasets can be integrated and machines can process them together.
explanation_incorrect: Interoperability is about shared standards and combinability, not cost, storage location, peer review, or citations.

question: What distinguishes 'data sharing' from 'data publishing'?
option_a: The two terms are identical in every conceivable respect and can always be used completely interchangeably
option_b: Data publishing is legally prohibited entirely unless the underlying dataset has an associated, formally filed patent
option_c: Data sharing always and necessarily requires assigning the dataset its own dedicated, persistent DOI beforehand
option_d: Data publishing means formally releasing curated data with documentation, a PID, and a license so it's citable
option_e: Data sharing simply means formally selling the dataset outright to an interested commercial third party
explanation_correct: Data publishing is a formal act - depositing curated, documented data in a repository with a PID and license so it is discoverable, citable, and preserved - whereas 'sharing' can range from informal transfer on request to that formal publication.
explanation_incorrect: Sharing on request typically lacks a DOI and preservation; publishing is not illegal or patent-dependent, and neither implies selling data.

question: An author writes 'data available upon reasonable request' as their data availability statement. What is a well-documented limitation of this approach?
option_a: It always and necessarily constitutes a clear violation of the dataset's underlying copyright
option_b: It requires whoever is requesting the data to pay a formal processing fee upfront
option_c: It typically results in the dataset being assigned an incorrect or mismatched DOI
option_d: It is a specific phrase that has been explicitly and formally banned by every academic journal
option_e: Studies have found such requests frequently go unanswered or are refused
explanation_correct: Research on 'available on request' statements has shown that a large fraction of such requests receive no response or are declined, meaning the data is effectively not available; funders and journals increasingly prefer deposit in a repository.
explanation_incorrect: The limitation is practical non-availability, not copyright violation, fees, universal bans, or DOI errors.
review_note: General finding; verify a representative source before publishing.

question: What is the primary aim of the TRUST Principles for digital repositories?
option_a: To rank data repositories against one another purely according to their overall storage size
option_b: To provide a framework of principles - Transparency, Responsibility, User focus, Sustainability, Technology
option_c: To completely replace and entirely supersede the existing FAIR principles altogether
option_d: To formally certify, one dataset at a time, the scientific quality of individual research data
option_e: To set the specific article processing charges that individual journals are permitted to bill
explanation_correct: The TRUST Principles (Transparency, Responsibility, User focus, Sustainability, Technology) give repositories and their stakeholders a shared framework and vocabulary for building and signaling trustworthiness, complementing FAIR (which concerns the data).
explanation_incorrect: TRUST addresses repository trustworthiness, not rankings, dataset certification, APCs, or replacing FAIR.

question: How do the FAIR and CARE principles relate when working with Indigenous data?
option_a: CARE is intended to completely replace FAIR outright, rendering all of its earlier technical principles entirely obsolete going forward
option_b: FAIR is explicitly designed to override every single CARE consideration in absolutely any situation that might arise
option_c: They fundamentally and irreconcilably conflict with each other, meaning the two frameworks can never be applied together in practice
option_d: CARE is relevant only to software and purely genomic datasets, and has no bearing whatsoever on any other type of data
option_e: They are applied together: FAIR guides technical stewardship while CARE adds people-and-purpose governance
explanation_correct: CARE complements FAIR by foregrounding Indigenous Peoples' rights and self-determination; in practice the two are used together ('be FAIR and CARE'), and CARE considerations can require restricting or governing access even where FAIR technical stewardship is met.
explanation_incorrect: CARE neither replaces nor inherently conflicts with FAIR, is not limited to software, and is not overridden by FAIR; they are designed to coexist.

question: What is the significance of assigning a DOI to a specific version of a dataset rather than only to 'the dataset' in general?
option_a: It permanently and irreversibly deletes any earlier versions of the dataset
option_b: It prevents the dataset from ever being formally cited by anyone
option_c: It makes the underlying data become legally proprietary going forward
option_d: It lets a citation point to the exact data version used
option_e: It automatically strips out identifying details and anonymizes the dataset
explanation_correct: Datasets evolve; version-specific DOIs let a paper cite the precise version analyzed, so results can be checked against the same data even after the dataset is later updated - supporting reproducibility and unambiguous credit.
explanation_incorrect: Versioned DOIs enable (not prevent) precise citation, do not make data proprietary, do not delete prior versions, and do not affect anonymization.

question: Why can over-aggressive anonymization sometimes conflict with FAIR/reuse goals, and how is this tension typically managed?
option_a: Stripping too much detail can destroy analytic value, so tiered/controlled access is used instead
option_b: It cannot happen; anonymization never has any real effect on how reusable data eventually becomes
option_c: Anonymization always and automatically improves the potential for meaningful data reuse afterward
option_d: The recommended solution is to simply never share sensitive data with any other researchers at all
option_e: The recommended solution is to publish every raw identifier completely unredacted for transparency
explanation_correct: Heavy de-identification can remove variables essential for reuse; a common resolution is a tiered approach - an open, safe version plus controlled access to more detailed data under agreements - balancing privacy with analytic utility.
explanation_incorrect: Anonymization can reduce utility, so the answer is neither 'never share' nor 'publish identifiers' but graduated/controlled access.
review_note: Verify UK Data Service anonymisation URL.

question: What role do 'data citations' with persistent identifiers play in the scholarly ecosystem beyond simple attribution?
option_a: None at all; formal data citations only ever provide simple, symbolic credit to their creators
option_b: They make datasets first-class, trackable research outputs
option_c: They substantially and permanently increase the dataset's overall file size on disk
option_d: They completely and entirely replace the need to ever peer review the underlying data
option_e: They fully and irreversibly anonymize the dataset the moment it is formally cited
explanation_correct: Formal data citation with PIDs lets datasets be tracked like articles - connecting publications to underlying data, enabling metrics of reuse, and supporting recognition of data work in research assessment - in line with the Joint Declaration of Data Citation Principles.
explanation_incorrect: Data citation does more than attribute; it does not change file size, replace review, or anonymize data.
review_note: FORCE11 Data Citation Principles DOI; verify it resolves.

question: A funder requires data to be shared 'as open as possible, as closed as necessary'. How should a researcher with sensitive human-subjects data comply?
option_a: Simply refuse outright to share any portion of the data whatsoever
option_b: Share the data completely openly, regardless of what participants originally consented to
option_c: Deposit open metadata and provide data via controlled access with consent
option_d: Delete the entire dataset immediately as soon as the funded project ends
option_e: Share the data only privately, and only with the funder's own internal staff
explanation_correct: Compliance means maximizing openness where safe: making the existence and description of the data openly discoverable while releasing the sensitive data itself through controlled/managed access governed by consent, ethics approval, and agreements.
explanation_incorrect: The principle rules out both blanket refusal and reckless open release; deletion and funder-only sharing also fail the openness expectation.

question: Why is 'reproducibility of data processing' aided by sharing analysis code and a described computational environment alongside the data?
option_a: Because the code and environment let others regenerate the results
option_b: Because sharing the analysis code somehow makes the underlying data physically smaller
option_c: Because sharing the code entirely replaces the need to also share the underlying data
option_d: Because sharing the analysis code automatically anonymizes the entire dataset
option_e: Because it assigns a personal, individual DOI to the researcher who wrote the code
explanation_correct: Data alone often cannot be turned back into the reported results without the processing steps; sharing the code and a specified environment (versions, dependencies) lets others rerun the pipeline and obtain the same processed outputs.
explanation_incorrect: Sharing code aids reproducibility, not file size, data replacement, anonymization, or researcher identification.

question: What is a key reason funders increasingly require Data Management Plans to be updated during a project rather than written once?
option_a: Simply to increase the overall amount of administrative paperwork that researchers are required to complete
option_b: Because the dataset's officially assigned DOI automatically changes partway through the funded project
option_c: Because formal data management plans legally expire after a fixed, non-renewable period of time
option_d: Because data needs and options often become clearer as a project proceeds
option_e: Because funder reviewers formally demand that plans be updated by the research team on a strict daily basis
explanation_correct: Research realities shift as projects proceed; treating the DMP as a living document (revisited at milestones) keeps decisions about storage, formats, consent, and sharing accurate and actionable, which is why maDMPs and periodic review are encouraged.
explanation_incorrect: The aim is realistic, current management, not extra paperwork for its own sake, legal expiry, daily updates, or identifier changes.

question: How does the concept of 'data provenance' support trustworthy reuse?
option_a: It records the origin and full history of the data
option_b: It works by anonymizing the identities of every individual contributor listed on the dataset
option_c: It works mainly by compressing the dataset down so that it takes up less storage space overall
option_d: It works by deliberately hiding or obscuring where the underlying data originally came from
option_e: It works by setting and permanently fixing the dataset's official recorded citation count
explanation_correct: Provenance documents where data came from and every transformation applied, enabling reusers to evaluate reliability, reproduce processing, and trust conclusions drawn from the data.
explanation_incorrect: Provenance reveals rather than hides origins and is unrelated to compression, citation counts, or anonymization.

question: What is a central governance idea behind Indigenous data sovereignty that CARE operationalizes?
option_a: That only professional academic researchers may ever legitimately decide on access
option_b: That all Indigenous data must, without exception, always be made fully open
option_c: That national governments hold outright ownership over all Indigenous data
option_d: That such data can, as a matter of principle, never legitimately be digitized
option_e: That Indigenous Peoples have the right to govern data about their communities
explanation_correct: Indigenous data sovereignty holds that Indigenous Peoples have authority over data about them - its collection, ownership, access, and use - and CARE turns this into practical principles (collective benefit, authority to control, responsibility, ethics) for data governance.
explanation_incorrect: The principle is self-determination and authority, not mandatory openness, government ownership, a digitization ban, or researcher-only control.

question: In debates about research data infrastructure sustainability, why is reliance on a single disciplinary repository sometimes seen as a risk, and what mitigates it?
option_a: There is no meaningful risk whatsoever in relying on a single disciplinary repository long-term, since funding for such services is always secure
option_b: The recommended mitigation is simply to delete the data before the repository can lose its funding, so nothing is left at risk
option_c: If it loses funding, data could be stranded; mitigations include certification and escrow arrangements
option_d: The real underlying risk is that the hosted data becomes far too easy for other researchers around the world to find and reuse
option_e: The real underlying risk is that persistent identifiers like DOIs will eventually become completely free to register for everyone
explanation_correct: Concentrating a field's data in one service creates a single point of failure; the response is to require preservation guarantees, certification (e.g., CoreTrustSeal), and plans for transferring or escrowing holdings if the repository fails - echoing POSI-style 'insurance' thinking.
explanation_incorrect: The concern is continuity, addressed by preservation and succession planning, not by deleting data or worrying about findability or DOI pricing.

question: How does the 'as machine-actionable as possible' vision for DMPs connect to the broader PID ecosystem?
option_a: By linking persistent identifiers so the plan connects to real research activity
option_b: It does not connect to persistent identifiers or the wider PID ecosystem in any meaningful way at all, since it was designed before those systems existed
option_c: By completely replacing every existing persistent identifier scheme with the DMP document itself, so ORCID, ROR, and DOIs are no longer needed
option_d: By assigning an entirely separate, unique DOI to each and every individual sentence written within the plan, down to the smallest clause
option_e: By making the use of persistent identifiers such as ORCID and ROR entirely optional in the plan, leaving each institution to decide for itself
explanation_correct: maDMPs are designed to interoperate with the PID graph: referencing ORCID, ROR, grant, and output identifiers lets systems auto-fill and update plans and connect the plan to real outputs, moving DMPs from static compliance documents to living, connected records.
explanation_incorrect: The vision integrates with, rather than replaces or trivializes, PIDs; it does not assign per-sentence DOIs.

question: Why might 'FAIR' data still fail to be genuinely reusable in practice, illustrating a limitation of the principles?
option_a: FAIR data is automatically and unconditionally guaranteed to be fully reusable by anyone who ever accesses it
option_b: Because the FAIR principles explicitly and specifically forbid attaching any documentation or context to a dataset
option_c: Because FAIR is about being findable and processable, not necessarily usable
option_d: Because data that satisfies the FAIR principles can, by definition, never actually be downloaded or retrieved by users
option_e: Because satisfying the FAIR principles requires stripping out every license so that reuse becomes fully unrestricted
explanation_correct: FAIR can be satisfied at a metadata/technical level while the data remains hard to reuse because it lacks rich contextual documentation, sufficient quality, clear usable licensing, or the domain knowledge a reuser needs - a recognized gap between 'FAIR-compliant' and 'actually reusable'.
explanation_incorrect: FAIR does not guarantee reuse and does not forbid documentation or licensing; the point is that technical FAIRness is necessary but not sufficient.

question: What tension can arise between the FAIR principle of interoperability and the diversity of disciplinary standards, and how is it typically addressed?
option_a: The tension is resolved simply by deleting all of the shared metadata associated with the data entirely
option_b: Interoperability strictly requires that every dataset everywhere use exactly one single shared file format
option_c: The recommended solution is to abolish all existing disciplinary metadata standards and vocabularies entirely
option_d: There is no real tension at all, since one single universal metadata standard already fully exists
option_e: Different fields have their own vocabularies, so interoperability relies on crosswalks and shared mappings
explanation_correct: Because disciplines legitimately use different, mature standards, interoperability is pursued through shared minimum metadata, crosswalks, and mappings that let heterogeneous data be linked and combined - not by imposing one universal format that would ignore domain needs.
explanation_incorrect: No single universal standard exists or is desirable; the answer is bridging standards, not abolishing them or forcing one format.

question: What does a Creative Commons 'CC BY' license require of someone reusing the work?
option_a: They must pay a licensing fee before being permitted to reuse the work
option_b: They may not use the reused work for any commercial purpose
option_c: They must give appropriate credit (attribution) to the creator
option_d: They may not make any changes or create adaptations of the work
option_e: They must share the reused work only with enrolled students
explanation_correct: CC BY (Attribution) is the most permissive Creative Commons license: reusers may copy, adapt, and redistribute the work, including commercially, provided they give appropriate credit to the creator.
explanation_incorrect: CC BY imposes no fee, no non-commercial restriction, no no-derivatives restriction, and no audience limits; its only core condition is attribution.

question: What is the Directory of Open Access Journals (DOAJ)?
option_a: A commercial publisher that sells access to its journal titles
option_b: A ranking system that orders journals by their impact factor score
option_c: A citation manager used to organize and format bibliographic references
option_d: A database that indexes preprints rather than published journal articles
option_e: A curated index of vetted open access journals meeting quality criteria
explanation_correct: DOAJ is an independent, community-curated directory that indexes open access journals meeting defined criteria for quality and transparency, helping authors and readers find trustworthy OA journals.
explanation_incorrect: DOAJ is not a publisher, an impact-factor ranking, a preprint database, or a reference manager; it is a vetted directory of OA journals.

question: What does the 'NC' element in a license like CC BY-NC mean?
option_a: It means no credit or attribution is required at all
option_b: The work may not be used for commercial purposes
option_c: It means no changes or adaptations may ever be made
option_d: It means no copies of the work may ever be made
option_e: It means the license is simply not intended for children
explanation_correct: 'NC' stands for NonCommercial: reusers may use and adapt the work but not for primarily commercial purposes; this is more restrictive than CC BY and is not considered fully 'open' by some open-access definitions.
explanation_incorrect: NC restricts commercial use specifically; it is not about derivatives (ND), attribution, copying, or audience age.

question: What does the 'ND' element in a CC BY-ND license restrict?
option_a: It restricts whether the work may be shared with others at all
option_b: It restricts whether the work may be read by the public
option_c: It restricts whether the original author must be given credit
option_d: Making and distributing adapted or modified versions (no derivatives)
option_e: It restricts where geographically the work may legally be used
explanation_correct: 'ND' means NoDerivatives: reusers may copy and share the unchanged work (with attribution) but may not distribute modified or adapted versions; this limits some reuses like translations or remixes.
explanation_incorrect: ND restricts derivative works, not sharing verbatim, attribution, reading, or geography.

question: An institutional repository is best described as:
option_a: A commercial database that is sold to libraries by a for-profit publishing company
option_b: A citation index used primarily to track how often published articles are referenced
option_c: A subscription journal that happens to be formally owned and operated by a university
option_d: A government agency responsible for distributing competitive research funding grants
option_e: An archive run by a university to preserve and share its research outputs
explanation_correct: An institutional repository is a service run by an institution to store and openly disseminate the scholarly outputs of its community (articles, theses, reports, data), often supporting green open access self-archiving.
explanation_incorrect: It is not a commercial database, a journal, a funder, or a citation index; it is the institution's own open archive.

question: What is an 'embargo period' in the context of green open access?
option_a: An outright ban preventing the article from ever being formally published
option_b: A fee that readers must pay in order to gain early access to an article
option_c: A publisher-set delay before a self-archived version can be made open
option_d: The total length of time that formal peer review typically takes to complete
option_e: A period during which the publisher's entire journal website is offline
explanation_correct: An embargo is a publisher-imposed delay (e.g., 6, 12, or 24 months) after publication before an author may make a repository copy openly available; funders increasingly reject embargoes in favor of immediate access.
explanation_incorrect: An embargo delays open availability of a deposited version; it is not a publishing ban, a fee, review time, or downtime.
review_note: Verify Open Policy Finder URL (former Sherpa Romeo).

question: What does CC0 (CC Zero) do?
option_a: Adds the strictest and most restrictive set of licensing conditions possible
option_b: Applies exclusively to software and cannot be used for other kinds of works
option_c: Requires attribution and forbids the work from being used commercially
option_d: Waives copyright, placing the work effectively in the public domain
option_e: Reserves all rights, meaning no reuse of the work is permitted at all
explanation_correct: CC0 is a public-domain dedication by which a creator waives their copyright and related rights as far as legally possible, letting anyone use the work for any purpose without conditions; it is commonly recommended for data.
explanation_incorrect: CC0 removes rather than adds restrictions, requires no attribution, is not software-specific, and is the opposite of 'all rights reserved'.

question: Which statement about article processing charges (APCs) is accurate?
option_a: Every single open access journal in existence charges an article processing charge
option_b: APCs are functionally the same thing as the subscription fees libraries pay
option_c: Fees some OA journals charge authors, though many OA journals charge none
option_d: Article processing charges are illegal under most national copyright laws
option_e: APCs are fees that are paid directly by individual readers of the article
explanation_correct: An APC is a publication fee levied on the author side in some OA models to fund making the article open; however, a large share of OA journals - including diamond/platinum journals - charge no APC at all.
explanation_incorrect: Not all OA journals charge APCs, APCs are author-side (not reader) fees, they are legal, and they differ from library subscriptions.

question: What is the difference between 'gratis' and 'libre' open access?
option_a: The two terms are identical and describe exactly the same licensing arrangement
option_b: Gratis open access means the article is paid for; libre means it is free
option_c: Gratis OA means free to read; libre OA also grants reuse rights via a license
option_d: The libre designation applies only to open access books, not to articles
option_e: Gratis open access always requires the author to pay a processing charge
explanation_correct: Gratis OA removes the price barrier (free to read) but not permission barriers; libre OA removes both, adding an open license that grants reuse rights (e.g., CC BY). Bronze OA is typically gratis-only.
explanation_incorrect: The two differ precisely on reuse rights; gratis is not 'paid', libre is not book-only, and gratis does not require an APC.
review_note: Suber's gratis/libre distinction; verify a stable canonical URL before publishing.

question: The 2003 Berlin Declaration is significant in open access history because it:
option_a: It created and launched the very first preprint server for scientific papers
option_b: An early statement promoting open access, with signatories committing to it
option_c: It formally banned the continued operation of subscription-based journals
option_d: It was the founding statement that established Creative Commons licensing
option_e: It was the declaration that first established the Journal Impact Factor
explanation_correct: The Berlin Declaration on Open Access to Knowledge in the Sciences and Humanities (2003) is one of the foundational OA statements (alongside Budapest and Bethesda), with many institutions signing to commit to advancing open access.
explanation_incorrect: It did not create a preprint server, ban subscriptions, establish the JIF, or found Creative Commons.

question: Which best describes 'open access' at its core?
option_a: Free membership in a scholarly society that grants access to its journals
option_b: Online, free-of-charge access to scholarly literature, often with rights to reuse it
option_c: Access that is restricted only to a particular journal's paying subscribers
option_d: Access to scholarly literature that is limited to a single country
option_e: A discount applied to the article processing charges authors must pay
explanation_correct: Open access refers to scholarly outputs being available online free of charge to readers, and in its fuller ('libre') sense with an open license granting reuse rights, removing price and often permission barriers.
explanation_incorrect: OA is not a society membership, a subscriber tier, an APC discount, or geographically limited access.

question: What does DOAB (the Directory of Open Access Books) do?
option_a: Indexes peer-reviewed open access books and chapters to make them discoverable
option_b: Assigns ISBNs to newly published open access monographs and chapters
option_c: Sells discounted e-book versions of open access academic books
option_d: Publishes an annual ranking of academic book publishers
option_e: Provides paid review services for authors of academic books
