explanation_correct: DOAB is a discovery service that indexes quality-assured, peer-reviewed open access academic books and chapters from many publishers, helping readers and libraries find open monographs.
explanation_incorrect: DOAB is a directory, not a bookseller, ISBN agency, ranking, or review service.

question: Why is CC BY often the license required or preferred by open access funders?
option_a: Because it explicitly prevents the work from being used for any commercial purpose
option_b: Because it entirely forbids anyone from reusing the licensed work
option_c: Because it happens to be the only completely free Creative Commons license
option_d: Because it maximizes reuse while still ensuring the author is credited
option_e: Because it deliberately hides the identity of the work's original author
explanation_correct: Funders such as those in cOAlition S require CC BY because it permits the widest reuse - copying, adapting, mining, redistributing, even commercially - while guaranteeing attribution, aligning with open-access goals.
explanation_incorrect: CC BY enables rather than forbids reuse, allows commercial use, is not the only free license, and requires (not hides) credit.

question: Can a researcher who publishes in a subscription (paywalled) journal still provide green open access to the article?
option_a: No, green open access only ever works for articles published in fully open access journals
option_b: Yes, but only after the author pays an additional, separate article processing charge
option_c: No, subscription journals never permit any form of open access whatsoever
option_d: Yes, by self-archiving a permitted version under the publisher's policy
option_e: No, unless the article happens to be more than ten years old already
explanation_correct: Green open access is independent of the journal's own model: an author in a subscription journal can deposit a permitted version (commonly the author accepted manuscript) in a repository, according to the publisher's self-archiving policy or a funder rights-retention agreement, making it freely accessible at no APC.
explanation_incorrect: Green OA is not limited to OA journals, does not require an APC, and is not gated by article age; many subscription journals permit self-archiving of some version, and rights retention can secure it regardless.
review_note: Verify Open Policy Finder URL (former Sherpa Romeo).

question: How does the CC BY-SA (ShareAlike) license differ from CC BY?
option_a: BY-SA requires derivatives to be shared under a compatible license
option_b: BY-SA removes the requirement that the original author be given credit
option_c: BY-SA forbids the creation of any derivatives or adaptations at all
option_d: BY-SA applies only to photographs and other visual images
option_e: BY-SA forbids the licensed work from being used commercially
explanation_correct: CC BY-SA requires attribution and additionally that any adapted/derivative works be licensed under the same or a compatible ShareAlike license, keeping downstream versions equally open - a copyleft mechanism used by, e.g., Wikipedia.
explanation_incorrect: BY-SA still permits commercial use and derivatives and still requires attribution; the distinguishing feature is the ShareAlike condition, and it is not medium-specific.

question: What is a 'transformative agreement' (e.g., read-and-publish or publish-and-read)?
option_a: A formal corporate merger between two previously separate academic publishing companies that share resources
option_b: A license under which the author transfers full and permanent copyright ownership of the article to their funder
option_c: A blanket policy adopted by funders that bans institutions from subscribing to any hybrid journals at all
option_d: A contract bundling subscription access with open access publishing for an institution
option_e: A structural reform initiative aimed specifically at changing how the peer-review process is conducted
explanation_correct: Transformative agreements bundle reading access and open-access publishing into one deal, repurposing library subscription spend to cover OA publishing of the institution's articles, with the stated aim of transitioning journals to full open access.
explanation_incorrect: They are contracts about access and publishing, not publisher mergers, copyright transfers to funders, hybrid bans, or review reforms.

question: What is 'Subscribe to Open' (S2O)?
option_a: If enough subscribers renew, that year's content opens with no author APCs
option_b: A model where libraries subscribe specifically to keep a journal's content permanently closed
option_c: A discount program that reduces the fees individual authors have to pay
option_d: A particular type of green open access achieved through self-archiving
option_e: A subscription that grants early access to preprints before formal publication
explanation_correct: Under Subscribe to Open, a journal converts to open access year by year if its usual subscribers continue paying; when the threshold is met, that year's content is made openly available and no author-facing APCs are charged.
explanation_incorrect: S2O opens content (not keeps it closed), is not an author discount, green route, or preprint scheme; it leverages existing subscription spend to fund OA.

question: Why is CC BY-NC-ND sometimes criticized when applied to scholarly articles despite being 'open to read'?
option_a: It costs authors noticeably more in fees to publish than a plain CC BY license would ever require
option_b: Its NonCommercial and NoDerivatives terms block many legitimate reuses
option_c: It effectively and permanently bans anyone at all from reading the article once it is published
option_d: It removes the requirement that the original author be formally credited when the work is reused
option_e: It is not actually recognized by Creative Commons as a real, legally valid license option
explanation_correct: CC BY-NC-ND permits free reading but its NC and ND conditions prohibit commercial reuse and adaptation, which excludes uses like translation, remixing for teaching, or text/data mining by companies - so it does not meet fuller definitions of open access.
explanation_incorrect: The criticism is about restricted reuse, not cost, attribution removal, a reading ban, or license validity.

question: Latin America's scholarly publishing ecosystem (SciELO, Redalyc, AmeliCA) is notable for predominantly using which OA model?
option_a: Hybrid open access publishing that charges unusually high processing fees
option_b: Bronze open access, where articles are free to read but carry no license
option_c: Green self-archiving only, with no formally open access journals involved
option_d: Diamond OA, funded by public institutions rather than authors
option_e: Subscription-only access, with no open access options offered at all
explanation_correct: SciELO, Redalyc, and AmeliCA support a large, long-standing diamond OA ecosystem in Latin America where journals are free to read and free to publish, sustained by universities, governments, and scholarly communities rather than APCs.
explanation_incorrect: The region's model is characteristically APC-free diamond OA, not hybrid/high-APC, green-only, bronze, or subscription.

question: What did Jisc's 'Open Policy Finder' replace, and what does it help users check?
option_a: It replaced Zenodo, and it primarily helps researchers store their datasets
option_b: It replaced Sherpa (Romeo/Juliet/Fact); it helps check OA and self-archiving policies
option_c: It replaced ORCID, and it primarily helps institutions verify author identities
option_d: It replaced Crossref, and it primarily helps publishers register new DOIs
option_e: It replaced DOAJ, and it primarily helps readers check journal impact factors
explanation_correct: Open Policy Finder, launched by Jisc in November 2024, consolidated the Sherpa Romeo (publisher OA/archiving policies), Sherpa Juliet (funder policies), and Sherpa Fact services into one platform for checking OA and self-archiving permissions.
explanation_incorrect: It succeeded the Sherpa tools, not DOAJ, ORCID, Crossref, or Zenodo, and it concerns policies, not metrics, identities, DOI registration, or data storage.

question: What is the difference between a 'fully OA' (pure gold) journal and a 'hybrid' journal?
option_a: There is no meaningful difference between the two types of journal at all
option_b: A fully OA journal opens all articles; a hybrid journal only opens paid ones
option_c: Hybrid journals never charge any kind of fee to authors or readers
option_d: Fully open access journals are always, by definition, diamond open access
option_e: Hybrid journals are restricted to publishing only research datasets
explanation_correct: In a fully/pure gold OA journal every article is open; a hybrid journal remains behind a subscription paywall but offers per-article OA for a fee, meaning readers or institutions may pay twice (subscription plus APC) - a key Plan S concern.
explanation_incorrect: Hybrid journals do charge (APCs); fully OA journals are not necessarily diamond (many charge APCs); hybrids publish normal articles.

question: What are equity concerns associated with the APC-based (gold) open access model?
option_a: Author-side charges can exclude researchers without funding
option_b: Article processing charges completely eliminate every form of inequity in scholarly publishing
option_c: APCs are a cost barrier that affects only the readers of an article, never its actual authors
option_d: Article processing charges are a reliable guarantee that the resulting research will be higher quality
option_e: APCs, taken as a whole, make the overall process of scholarly publishing considerably too cheap
explanation_correct: While APC gold OA removes paywalls for readers, it can create a new barrier for authors who lack grants or institutional funds - a concern especially for the Global South and less-funded disciplines - motivating waivers, diamond OA, and other models.
explanation_incorrect: The concern is that APCs shift the barrier to the author side and can exclude the underfunded; APCs are not reader fees and do not guarantee quality or equity.
review_note: Broad equity point; verify a representative source before publishing.

question: Programs like Research4Life exist primarily to:
option_a: They exist mainly to produce and publish rankings of open access journals
option_b: They exist mainly to provide direct financial support to preprint servers
option_c: They exist mainly to assign persistent DOIs to scholarly publications
option_d: Free or low-cost access to literature for researchers in eligible countries
option_e: They exist mainly to formally certify the trustworthiness of data repositories
explanation_correct: Research4Life is a public-private partnership giving institutions in eligible low- and middle-income countries free or very low-cost access to peer-reviewed content (and related waivers), addressing access inequities in scholarly literature.
explanation_incorrect: It is an access-equity program, not a ranking, DOI agency, preprint funder, or repository certifier.

question: An author wants to comply with a funder's immediate-OA requirement but publish in a subscription journal. Which route most directly enables this without an APC?
option_a: Green open access via self-archiving with rights retention to avoid an embargo
option_b: Posting a preprint of the work and simply stopping there, without further action
option_c: Waiting patiently for the publisher's standard 24-month embargo period to end
option_d: Paying the publisher's fee to access the hybrid open access option
option_e: Formally transferring copyright ownership of the article to the funder
explanation_correct: Depositing the accepted manuscript in a repository (green OA), combined with a rights-retention strategy that secures a CC BY license before submission, lets the author provide immediate open access with no APC, even in a subscription journal.
explanation_incorrect: Hybrid OA requires an APC, waiting out an embargo is not immediate, a preprint alone may not satisfy the policy's version requirements, and copyright is not transferred to funders.

question: How can Creative Commons licenses be combined or 'stacked', and what limitation exists?
option_a: BY, SA, NC, and ND combine into six licenses, but SA and ND cannot coexist
option_b: There are exactly 20 valid combinations that can be formed from the four licensing elements
option_c: Any of the four licensing elements can be freely combined together with no restrictions at all
option_d: Only the NC and ND elements are ever permitted to be combined together in a single license
option_e: Creative Commons licenses can never be combined with one another under any circumstances at all
explanation_correct: Creative Commons offers six licenses formed from the BY, SA, NC, and ND conditions; ShareAlike and NoDerivatives are mutually exclusive (one governs how derivatives are shared, the other bans derivatives), which is why no BY-SA-ND license exists.
explanation_incorrect: The combinations are constrained, not free-form; there are six licenses, not twenty, and the key limitation is the SA/ND incompatibility.

question: What is the significance of a journal being listed in DOAJ for authors assessing where to publish?
option_a: It automatically guarantees that the journal has a high official impact factor
option_b: It signals the journal has met DOAJ's transparency and quality criteria
option_c: It simply means the journal does not charge any fees whatsoever
option_d: It provides a formal numerical ranking for where the journal stands
option_e: It certifies that every single article the journal publishes is factually correct
explanation_correct: DOAJ inclusion indicates a journal meets vetted criteria for openness, transparency, and best practice, which authors can use as one positive signal of legitimacy - useful in avoiding predatory venues - though it is not a quality-of-content guarantee.
explanation_incorrect: DOAJ listing is not an impact-factor guarantee, a no-fee guarantee, an article-correctness certification, or a ranking.

question: What was the 'DOAJ Seal', and what happened to it?
option_a: A decorative logo or badge that any journal could simply purchase outright from DOAJ for a fee
option_b: A mark for meeting seven best-practice criteria; DOAJ retired it in 2025 amid equity concerns
option_c: A ranking system that listed and ordered only the very top open access journals each year
option_d: A specific type of open Creative Commons license that individual journals could choose to formally adopt
option_e: A certificate awarded to individual peer reviewers in recognition of their volunteer service
explanation_correct: The DOAJ Seal recognized journals meeting seven higher-bar best-practice criteria; DOAJ decided to retire the Seal (around March 2025) partly because it was easier for well-resourced journals to earn, favoring the Global North, and planned new equity-oriented labels instead.
explanation_incorrect: The Seal was earned (not bought), applied to journals (not reviewers), was not a ranking or a license, and is being replaced.

question: Why does Plan S's requirement to retain rights (via a CC BY license on the AAM) matter even when a publisher's standard embargo would eventually allow green OA?
option_a: It lets the manuscript be shared openly at publication, defeating the embargo
option_b: Because relying on rights retention substantially increases the article processing charge owed
option_c: Because the rights retention strategy is written to apply only to academic books and monographs
option_d: Because using this strategy transfers the manuscript's copyright entirely to the publisher instead
option_e: Because applying rights retention in this way noticeably shortens the overall length of the article
explanation_correct: Plan S demands zero-embargo open access; standard green routes often impose embargoes. By requiring a prior CC BY license on the AAM, funders let authors post immediately regardless of the publisher's embargo, achieving the immediacy the policy requires.
explanation_incorrect: Rights retention is about immediacy and control, not article length, higher APCs, giving copyright to publishers, or a book-only scope.

question: A key critique of transformative agreements is that they may:
option_a: They reduce a publisher's overall subscription revenue stream far too quickly for it to adapt
option_b: They completely and permanently prevent any article from ever being made open access at all
option_c: They eliminate all reading access whatsoever to the journal's content for every subscriber
option_d: They can entrench incumbent publishers and lock in high spending
option_e: They only ever end up financially benefiting small, independent society publishers
explanation_correct: Critics argue transformative agreements can consolidate spending with the largest publishers and normalize high costs, risking a durable 'transition' that preserves incumbents' dominance instead of enabling cheaper or community-led (e.g., diamond) models - a reason cOAlition S time-limited its support.
explanation_incorrect: The critique is about entrenchment and cost, not too-fast revenue loss, loss of reading access, blocking OA, or favoring small publishers.

question: How does 'bronze' open access complicate open-access measurement and policy compliance?
option_a: It is, in every practical and legal respect, exactly the same thing as gold open access
option_b: It is a designation that applies only to research datasets, and never to journal articles
option_c: It is simply a specific subtype that falls formally under the umbrella of diamond open access
option_d: It requires, by strict definition, that the author pay a substantial article processing charge
option_e: Bronze articles are free but unlicensed, so access can be revoked anytime
explanation_correct: Bronze OA (free to read, no explicit license) can make open-access statistics look larger than the amount of reliably reusable, permanent OA; for funder compliance requiring open licenses and durability, bronze typically does not qualify.
explanation_incorrect: Bronze differs from gold (which has an open license), is not a diamond subtype, does not require an APC, and is not dataset-specific.

question: Why is CC BY generally required rather than CC BY-NC for compliant open-access articles under policies like Plan S?
option_a: NC's ambiguity and its blocking of legitimate reuses conflicts with maximizing reuse
option_b: Because a plain CC BY license itself already completely forbids any commercial reuse
option_c: Because attaching the NonCommercial condition removes the requirement for attribution entirely
option_d: Because using a NonCommercial license substantially increases the overall publishing costs involved
option_e: Because NonCommercial licenses are not recognized as legally valid licenses at all
explanation_correct: Plan S and similar policies require CC BY because NonCommercial terms are ambiguous and restrict legitimate reuses (including commercial text/data mining and reuse by many organizations), undercutting the goal of unrestricted reuse with attribution.
explanation_incorrect: The issue is reuse restriction and ambiguity, not cost, attribution, or validity; CC BY permits commercial use.

question: What distinguishes an institutional open-access mandate with a 'rights-retention' policy (Harvard-style) from a simple deposit request?
option_a: Nothing at all; a formal rights-retention policy and a simple deposit request work in exactly the same way
option_b: It requires that a full article processing charge be paid for absolutely every single article
option_c: It transfers full and complete copyright ownership of every faculty article directly to the university
option_d: It applies only to internally produced teaching materials, and never to peer-reviewed research articles
option_e: Faculty collectively grant the institution a prior, non-exclusive license to their articles
explanation_correct: Harvard-style policies work by a faculty vote granting the institution a standing non-exclusive license to each article before any publisher contract, so the right to deposit openly is secured up front; a plain deposit request has no such legal backing and can be overridden by publisher terms.
explanation_incorrect: The distinction is the prior blanket license (not full copyright transfer), it covers scholarly articles, and it does not require APCs.
review_note: Verify Harvard OSC model policy URL.

question: Why do some argue diamond open access is more sustainable and equitable than APC-based gold OA, and what is its main challenge?
option_a: Diamond removes author charges, but its challenge is securing stable funding
option_b: Diamond open access forbids the use of any form of formal peer review whatsoever
option_c: Diamond open access charges authors the single highest article processing charges available anywhere
option_d: Diamond open access, in practice, systematically and deliberately excludes researchers in the Global South
option_e: Diamond open access is a funding model that faces no real sustainability challenges of any kind
explanation_correct: Diamond OA charges neither readers nor authors, avoiding the equity problems of APCs and the paywalls of subscriptions; its central difficulty is sustainable financing - it depends on institutional, governmental, or volunteer support that can be precarious, motivating coordination efforts like the Global Diamond OA Alliance.
explanation_incorrect: Diamond has no APCs, is widespread in the Global South, and uses peer review; its challenge is funding, not those.

question: cOAlition S's 'Towards Responsible Publishing' proposal (2023-2024) envisions a scholarly communication system in which:
option_a: A system in which only traditional subscription-based journals are able to continue surviving
option_b: Authors retain rights and share the full range of outputs, not just the final article
option_c: A system in which the entire practice of formal peer review is abolished outright
option_d: A system in which article processing charges are made a mandatory requirement for every author
option_e: A system in which journal impact factor scores are what ultimately governs funding decisions
explanation_correct: The Towards Responsible Publishing proposal argues OA should extend beyond the final article to sharing the cumulative outputs of research - preprints, data, peer-review feedback - with authors retaining the rights to do so, reframing publishing around the whole research process.
explanation_incorrect: It does not preserve only subscriptions, abolish peer review, mandate APCs, or elevate impact factors; it emphasizes author-controlled, output-rich openness.

question: How does a 'green' route with immediate deposit but a 'closed' record (deposit-then-release) support both compliance and publisher relations?
option_a: The metadata record is created immediately while the full text opens once permitted
option_b: This kind of workflow only ever works for academic books and edited monographs, not articles
option_c: This kind of workflow works mainly by permanently deleting the original submitted manuscript
option_d: This kind of workflow works by transferring copyright ownership of the article to the reader
option_e: This kind of workflow means that the underlying full-text file is in fact never actually released
explanation_correct: Immediate deposit / optional access workflows record the output at acceptance (meeting 'deposit on acceptance' requirements) and set the full text to open as soon as rights or embargoes permit, balancing prompt capture with publisher terms.
explanation_incorrect: The file is released (not deleted or never released), no copyright passes to readers, and the approach is general, not book-only.
review_note: Verify Open Policy Finder URL; concept is the classic 'immediate-deposit/optional-access' (Harnad).

question: Why is the distinction between 'transformative journals' and 'transformative agreements' important under Plan S?
option_a: Transformative journals are subscription-only publications forever, with absolutely no path toward full open access
option_b: They are simply the exact same underlying mechanism, just described using two different names
option_c: A transformative agreement is a consortium-publisher contract; a transformative journal is one committing to flip to full OA
option_d: Transformative agreements under Plan S apply only to academic books, never to journal articles
option_e: Neither transformative journals nor transformative agreements were ever actually supported under Plan S
explanation_correct: Plan S recognized two transitional mechanisms: transformative agreements (institution/consortium-level deals) and transformative journals (single journals pledging annual OA growth and a flip to full OA), both intended as temporary bridges - with cOAlition S support ending after 2024.
explanation_incorrect: They are distinct mechanisms (not identical), both were Plan S transitional tools, transformative journals aim to flip to OA, and neither is book-restricted.

question: What structural problem does the 'serials crisis' describe, and how does it connect to the push for open access?
option_a: Decades of subscription price increases outpacing library budgets concentrated among a few large publishers
option_b: The existence of far too many competing open access journals chasing the same readers
option_c: A persistent, worsening shortage of qualified editors willing to take on running journals
option_d: A general and ongoing lack of preprint servers available for researchers to use
option_e: An unusual surplus of funding that has become available to university libraries
explanation_correct: The serials crisis refers to journal subscription costs rising faster than library budgets, with high profits concentrated among major commercial publishers; this economic pressure was a key driver of the open-access movement's search for alternatives.
explanation_incorrect: The crisis is about unsustainable subscription costs and market concentration, not editor shortages, OA oversupply, preprint scarcity, or budget surpluses.
review_note: Verify SPARC Big Deal Knowledge Base URL.

question: How can 'read-and-publish' deals paradoxically disadvantage researchers in countries or institutions that publish little but read a lot?
option_a: They cannot; deals structured this way are always completely neutral in cost for every institution that signs one
option_b: They only ever affect the profit margins reported by large commercial publishing companies each year
option_c: They always and reliably lower the overall cost of access for every single institution that adopts one
option_d: They eliminate all reading access to the journal's content entirely for every institution involved
option_e: Bundling costs into publish-oriented deals can burden read-heavy, publish-light institutions
explanation_correct: When spending is tied to publishing output, institutions and countries that publish relatively little but need reading access may pay more or gain less, and the model can entrench a system where cost tracks article volume - a recognized equity critique, especially for the Global South.
explanation_incorrect: The deals are not cost-neutral for everyone; they can raise costs for read-heavy, publish-light institutions rather than lowering them universally.
review_note: Equity critique; verify a representative source before publishing.

question: Why do critics argue that 'flipping' subscription journals to gold OA via APCs may not fundamentally reform scholarly publishing economics?
option_a: Because gold open access publishing does not actually have any real costs associated with it
option_b: Because article processing charges are, in every case, lower than equivalent subscription fees
option_c: Because it can preserve the same publishers, prices, and profit margins
option_d: Because the practice of flipping a subscription journal to gold OA is legally prohibited outright
option_e: Because journals that charge APCs cannot be indexed by any of the major scholarly databases
explanation_correct: A core critique is that APC-based flips leave market structure, pricing power, and margins intact while relocating the payment point to authors, which neither reduces overall system costs nor breaks publisher concentration - and adds equity problems for the underfunded.
explanation_incorrect: APCs are not inherently cheaper, gold OA has real costs, flipping is allowed, and APC journals are indexed; the critique targets unchanged economics and new inequities.
review_note: Broad critique; verify a representative source before publishing.

question: What is the relationship between open access and Creative Commons licensing in the 'libre OA' definition, and why is licensing alone insufficient for full OA?
option_a: Licensing is considered entirely irrelevant to how open access is formally and technically defined
option_b: Libre OA requires both free access and an open license; a license alone doesn't ensure permanence
option_c: Attaching any Creative Commons license at all automatically and by itself makes a work open access
option_d: Achieving genuinely full open access fundamentally requires abolishing copyright law altogether
option_e: Only works that are released specifically under a CC0 public domain license truly count as open access
explanation_correct: Full (libre) open access combines gratis access with an open reuse license; however, applying a CC license to a work that is paywalled, undiscoverable, or not preserved does not deliver open access - the free-to-read, findable, and durable conditions must also be met.
explanation_incorrect: A license by itself does not guarantee access or permanence; OA does not require abolishing copyright, and licenses beyond CC0 (e.g., CC BY) qualify.

question: What are 'altmetrics'?
option_a: Simply alternative spellings that authors sometimes use for their own names
option_b: A specific type of persistent identifier assigned to individual researchers
option_c: A proposed replacement intended to do away with formal peer review
option_d: Simply a count of how many authors are listed on a given paper
option_e: Metrics that capture attention and engagement with research beyond citations
explanation_correct: Altmetrics (alternative metrics) track diverse forms of online attention and engagement with research outputs - news coverage, policy citations, social media, blogs, bookmarks - complementing traditional citation counts.
explanation_incorrect: Altmetrics are not name variants, a review process, an identifier, or an author count; they measure broader attention.

question: What is a 'citation' in bibliometrics?
option_a: A fee that authors are required to pay directly to a journal
option_b: A formal written report produced during the peer-review process
option_c: A brief summary describing the contents of a published article
option_d: A formal reference from one work to another, measuring uptake
option_e: A type of persistent identifier assigned to scholarly outputs
explanation_correct: A citation is a reference in one work pointing to another; counts of citations are widely (if imperfectly) used as indicators of how much a work has been used or acknowledged by later research.
explanation_incorrect: A citation is not a fee, summary, review, or identifier; it is a referencing link between works.

question: Why is using a journal's Impact Factor to judge an individual article considered problematic?
option_a: Because the exact calculation behind the Impact Factor is kept secret
option_b: Because the Impact Factor only counts citations to review articles
option_c: Because individual journal articles cannot technically be cited at all
option_d: Because the Impact Factor is recalculated and changes on a daily basis
option_e: Because citation counts within a journal are highly skewed
explanation_correct: Citations are very unevenly distributed within a journal (a small number of papers attract most citations), so the journal-level average is a poor proxy for the impact or quality of any particular article - a central argument of DORA.
explanation_incorrect: The JIF is published (not secret), annual (not daily), and includes articles; the core issue is skewed within-journal distribution.

question: What is the CRediT taxonomy used for?
option_a: A system used specifically for ranking journals against one another
option_b: A workflow used specifically for assigning DOIs to research outputs
option_c: A tool used by publishers specifically to detect plagiarism in submissions
option_d: A method used specifically for measuring how often a paper is cited
option_e: Describing the specific contributor roles each author played in a research output
explanation_correct: CRediT (Contributor Roles Taxonomy) provides a standardized list of 14 roles so that each contributor's specific involvement in a work can be described transparently, improving fairness and clarity in authorship.
explanation_incorrect: CRediT describes contributions, not journal rankings, identifiers, citation counts, or plagiarism.

question: Which of these is a citation database used to gather bibliometric data?
option_a: Scopus
option_b: Zenodo
option_c: ORCID
option_d: re3data
option_e: CITATION.cff
explanation_correct: Scopus (Elsevier) is a large abstract and citation database; others include Web of Science, Dimensions, and the open OpenAlex, all used to collect citation and publication data.
explanation_incorrect: Zenodo is a repository, ORCID a person identifier, re3data a repository registry, and CITATION.cff a software-citation file - none is a citation database.

question: What is Google Scholar's 'i10-index'?
option_a: A simple count of the total number of authors indexed by the platform
option_b: Publications that have each received at least 10 citations
option_c: The official impact factor belonging to a researcher's tenth-ranked journal
option_d: Specifically, a researcher's tenth most-cited individual paper
option_e: The total number of citations a researcher received over the last 10 years
explanation_correct: The i10-index, used in Google Scholar profiles, simply counts how many of a researcher's papers have at least 10 citations each - a straightforward productivity/impact indicator.
explanation_incorrect: It is not an author count, a journal metric, a single paper, or a time-windowed citation total; it counts papers with 10+ citations.

question: Why do citation counts for the same researcher often differ between Google Scholar, Scopus, and Web of Science?
option_a: Because each database indexes a different set of sources
option_b: Because one of the three databases is always simply incorrect
option_c: Because citation counts are essentially generated at random
option_d: Because Google Scholar is the only one of the three that counts citations at all
option_e: Because the researcher themselves is able to directly edit the counts
explanation_correct: Each platform covers a different (and differently curated) universe of journals, books, conference papers, and other documents; Google Scholar is broadest and least curated, so counts and derived metrics like the h-index differ across sources.
explanation_incorrect: The differences reflect coverage, not error, randomness, a single counting source, or user editing.

question: What does the 'responsible metrics' movement broadly advocate?
option_a: Using as many different metrics as possible for every single decision
option_b: Abolishing the practice of formal peer review across all disciplines
option_c: Using quantitative indicators carefully and in support of
option_d: Publicly ranking every individual researcher against their peers
option_e: Relying solely and exclusively on the Journal Impact Factor for evaluation
explanation_correct: Responsible metrics (as in DORA, the Leiden Manifesto, and the Metric Tide) call for indicators to be used transparently and in context, informing rather than replacing expert judgement, and always mindful of their limitations and potential to distort behavior.
explanation_incorrect: It is about restrained, context-aware use of metrics, not more metrics, public rankings, abolishing review, or relying on the JIF.

question: An 'Altmetric Attention Score' primarily reflects:
option_a: A direct measure of how high-quality a given paper actually is
option_b: A weighted count of online attention across news, policy, and social media
option_c: Simply the total number of formal citations the paper has received
option_d: Simply a count of how many coauthors are listed on the paper
option_e: The official impact factor of the journal that published the paper
explanation_correct: The Altmetric Attention Score is a weighted aggregate of mentions across tracked sources (news outlets, policy documents, social media, blogs, etc.), indicating attention - not quality or scholarly citation.
explanation_incorrect: It measures attention, not quality, citations, journal metrics, or authorship; high attention does not equal high quality.

question: Which statement about the h-index is a recognized limitation?
option_a: It disadvantages early-career researchers and varies by citation rates across fields
option_b: It is a number that can spontaneously decrease over time on its own
option_c: It is a metric that counts only citations a researcher makes to their own work
option_d: It is a value that ends up being identical for everyone working in a field
option_e: It is a metric that entirely ignores a researcher's publications altogether
explanation_correct: The h-index tends to grow with career length and cannot exceed the number of papers, so it structurally disadvantages early-career researchers and is not comparable across fields with different citation cultures.
explanation_incorrect: The h-index generally does not decrease on its own, is not limited to self-citations, varies across individuals, and is built from publications and their citations.

question: What is a 'narrative CV' (e.g., the RÃ©sumÃ© for Research and Innovation)?
option_a: A formal report that simply lists a researcher's total citation counts
option_b: A standard form used specifically to apply for competitive research funding
option_c: A ranked list ordering a researcher's papers from most to least cited
option_d: A structured CV format asking researchers to describe their contributions in prose
option_e: A CV that has been generated automatically from start to finish by an AI system
explanation_correct: Narrative CVs (such as UKRI's RÃ©sumÃ© for Research and Innovation) prompt researchers to describe a fuller range of contributions in structured prose, countering over-reliance on publication lists and journal metrics in assessment.
explanation_incorrect: It is a human-written structured narrative of contributions, not an AI product, a ranked paper list, a citation report, or a grant form.

question: What does 'field normalization' of a citation metric attempt to correct for?
option_a: The fact that citation rates differ greatly between disciplines
option_b: Systematic differences in the ages of the authors being compared
option_c: Simple differences in the spelling of author or journal names
option_d: Differences in the total number of pages an article happens to have
option_e: Differences in how much journals charge libraries for a subscription
explanation_correct: Because fields cite at very different rates (e.g., molecular biology vs mathematics), field-normalized indicators compare a paper's citations to the average for similar papers in the same field and year, enabling fairer cross-field comparison.
explanation_incorrect: Normalization addresses disciplinary citation-rate differences, not author age, spelling, article length, or prices.

question: OpenAlex, Crossref, and OpenCitations differ from Web of Science and Scopus primarily because they:
option_a: They are considerably more expensive for institutions to access
option_b: They only ever cover research from the field of physics
option_c: Provide openly available data rather than proprietary subscription-only data
option_d: They are indexes that do not actually include any citation data at all
option_e: They are indexes that are run directly by national governments
explanation_correct: OpenAlex, Crossref, and OpenCitations make bibliographic and citation data openly available (supporting the open-research-information movement), in contrast to the proprietary, subscription-based Web of Science and Scopus.
explanation_incorrect: They are open (not more expensive), multidisciplinary, do include citation data, and are run by nonprofits/community bodies, not governments.

question: Why is 'number of publications' alone a poor measure of research quality?
option_a: Because publications are, as a matter of principle, impossible to count accurately
option_b: Because it is technically illegal to count a researcher's publications
option_c: Because publication counts are the only form of evidence that meaningfully exists
option_d: It rewards quantity over quality and can incentivize salami-slicing
option_e: Because every researcher publishes at exactly the same steady rate
explanation_correct: A raw publication count measures output volume, not merit; over-weighting it can push researchers toward fragmenting work into least-publishable units and neglecting quality, rigor, and reproducibility.
explanation_incorrect: The point is that counts ignore quality and can distort behavior, not that counting is impossible, illegal, or uniform.

question: How does Scopus's CiteScore differ from the Journal Impact Factor?
option_a: CiteScore is recalculated completely fresh every single day
option_b: CiteScore is a figure that each journal's own editor calculates independently
option_c: CiteScore is a metric that counts only a journal's self-citations
option_d: CiteScore uses Scopus, a 4-year window, and counts document types consistently
option_e: CiteScore is a metric that deliberately excludes all review articles
explanation_correct: CiteScore, from Elsevier's Scopus, uses a longer (usually four-year) window and counts the same document types in both numerator and denominator, unlike the JIF's two-year window and its documented numerator/denominator asymmetry.
explanation_incorrect: CiteScore is computed by Scopus (not editors), is not self-citation-only or daily, and includes reviews; its differences are database, window, and document-type handling.

question: What is the initiative I4OC (Initiative for Open Citations) known for achieving?
option_a: Persuading publishers to make reference lists openly available via Crossref
option_b: Assigning persistent ORCID iDs to individual researchers around the world
option_c: Producing an annual ranking of the world's leading universities
option_d: Creating and popularizing the h-index as a bibliometric measure
option_e: Making the calculation behind the Journal Impact Factor fully open
explanation_correct: I4OC campaigned for publishers to open the reference lists they deposit at Crossref; its success meant the large majority of Crossref-indexed references became openly available, underpinning open citation resources like OpenCitations and OpenAlex.
explanation_incorrect: I4OC concerns opening citation data, not the JIF, rankings, the h-index, or identifiers.

question: The Leiden Manifesto principle that 'quantitative evaluation should support qualitative, expert assessment' implies that metrics should be used to:
option_a: Eliminating the role of qualitative expert review entirely
option_b: Replacing the practice of peer review altogether with numbers
option_c: Automatically ranking researchers purely based on their metrics
option_d: Directly setting researchers' salaries based on their metric scores
option_e: Inform and complement expert peer judgement, not override it
explanation_correct: The Leiden Manifesto positions metrics as an aid to expert judgement: numbers can inform decisions but should not substitute for the contextual, qualitative assessment that peers provide.
explanation_incorrect: The principle explicitly keeps expert judgement central, opposing automatic ranking or metric-driven decisions that replace peer review.

question: What does SNIP (Source Normalized Impact per Paper) add compared to a raw journal citation average?
option_a: It corrects for differences in citation practices between fields
option_b: It simply measures how many times an article has been downloaded
option_c: It measures the average number of authors listed per published paper
option_d: It counts only citations received within the most recent year
option_e: It is a measure that deliberately ignores citations altogether
explanation_correct: SNIP normalizes a journal's citation impact by the citation potential of its subject field, allowing fairer comparison of journals across disciplines with different citation densities and referencing behaviors.
explanation_incorrect: SNIP is a field-normalized citation metric, not a download, recency, authorship, or citation-free measure.
review_note: Verify CWTS Journal Indicators methodology URL.

question: Why can heavy reliance on any single metric trigger 'Goodhart's law' effects in research?
option_a: Because metrics of this kind are always completely accurate reflections of quality
option_b: Because a metric like this one is fundamentally impossible to game or manipulate
option_c: Because 'when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure'
option_d: Because turning a measure into a target always improves the underlying behavior
option_e: Because Goodhart's law is a concept that only ever applies within economics
explanation_correct: Goodhart's law warns that turning an indicator into a target invites gaming - salami-slicing for counts, citation manipulation, chasing high-JIF venues - which can distort or degrade the underlying quality the metric was supposed to capture.
explanation_incorrect: The point is that targeted metrics get gamed and can mislead, not that they are accurate, ungameable, universally beneficial, or economics-only.

question: What is a 'citation cartel' (or citation ring)?
option_a: A perfectly legitimate collaboration network formed between honest colleagues
option_b: A specific type of open license used for sharing research outputs
option_c: Authors or journals that cite each other excessively to inflate their metrics
option_d: A particular model used for conducting formal peer review
option_e: A large database that simply stores and organizes citation records
explanation_correct: A citation cartel is coordinated, manipulative cross-citation - among authors, journals, or editors - designed to boost citation-based metrics artificially; it is a form of metric gaming that undermines the validity of those metrics.
explanation_incorrect: It is manipulation, not a genuine collaboration, a database, a license, or a review model.

question: Under DORA, what practical change is recommended for hiring and promotion committees?
option_a: Give even greater weight to the Journal Impact Factor in every decision
option_b: Assess the content and merit of a candidate's outputs and contributions directly
option_c: Count and consider only papers on which the candidate was first author
option_d: Use raw citation counts as the single, sole hiring criterion
option_e: Require every candidate to have published solely in the highest-JIF journals
explanation_correct: DORA asks evaluators to judge research on its own merits - reading the work and considering diverse contributions - instead of using where it was published (journal metrics) as a shortcut for quality.
explanation_incorrect: DORA discourages JIF-based proxies and sole-metric criteria; it does not favor first-author-only counts or high-JIF requirements.

question: How do OpenCitations and similar open citation indexes support research assessment reform?
option_a: By deliberately keeping their underlying citation data fully proprietary, closed, and inaccessible to outsiders
option_b: By making citation data freely available and transparent
option_c: By producing formal, ranked league tables that compare individual researchers directly against one another
option_d: By working actively to entirely replace the traditional peer-review process across scholarly publishing
option_e: By taking over the role of calculating and formally assigning official journal impact factor scores
explanation_correct: Open citation infrastructure lets anyone access and verify the underlying citation data, so analyses are transparent and reproducible and not locked to a single vendor's opaque, subscription database - supporting both open research information and responsible metrics.
explanation_incorrect: OpenCitations makes data open (not proprietary), does not rank researchers, replace review, or assign impact factors.

question: What distinguishes SJR (SCImago Journal Rank) from a simple citation count?
option_a: It simply counts how many times an article has been downloaded
option_b: It is a measure of journal quality that ignores citation data entirely
option_c: It counts only citations that a paper makes back to its own authors' work
option_d: It weights citations by the prestige of the citing source
option_e: It is essentially a measure of how long a published article happens to be
explanation_correct: SJR uses a prestige-weighted, network-based approach (akin to eigenvector centrality) in which citations from more prestigious journals contribute more, rather than treating all citations equally as a raw count does.
explanation_incorrect: SJR is a prestige-weighted citation metric, not a download, self-citation-only, citation-free, or length measure.

question: Why is the Journal Impact Factor an inappropriate tool for comparing researchers across disciplines?
option_a: Because the Journal Impact Factor is technically calculated on a per-researcher basis
option_b: Because every academic field happens to share exactly identical JIF values
option_c: Because the Journal Impact Factor figure changes on a daily basis
option_d: Because the Journal Impact Factor is a metric that counts only academic books
option_e: Because it is a journal-level metric tied to field-specific citation rates
explanation_correct: The JIF describes journals, and typical JIF values differ sharply between fields due to differing citation behaviors and article volumes; using it to compare individuals across disciplines conflates journal, field, and person, and ignores the individual's actual contributions.
explanation_incorrect: The JIF is not per-researcher, daily, book-only, or uniform across fields; it is a field-dependent journal metric misapplied to individuals.

question: The Metric Tide report (2015) introduced which influential framework, and what were its dimensions?
option_a: The h-index, capturing dimensions of productivity and citation impact
option_b: Altmetrics, capturing attention across news outlets and social media
option_c: The Journal Impact Factor, capturing citations and formal reviews
option_d: CRediT, a taxonomy built around 14 distinct contributor roles
option_e: The concept of 'responsible metrics', built on five core dimensions
explanation_correct: The UK's Metric Tide review popularized 'responsible metrics', characterized by robustness (best available data), humility (metrics support not supplant judgement), transparency, diversity (reflecting varied outputs and career paths), and reflexivity (updating indicators as effects emerge).
explanation_incorrect: The report did not originate the h-index, JIF, CRediT, or altmetrics; its contribution was the responsible-metrics dimensions.

question: How do DORA, the Leiden Manifesto, the Metric Tide, and CoARA relate to one another?
option_a: They are competing standards that were developed independently and directly contradict and undermine one another
option_b: They are all, in fact, simply different printings and translations of the exact same underlying document
option_c: Each of the four frameworks specifically mandates that institutions adopt one particular, named metric
option_d: Only CoARA, among the four listed initiatives, is actually concerned with research metrics at all
option_e: They are complementary contributions to responsible research assessment
explanation_correct: These initiatives form a broadly aligned movement: DORA challenged JIF misuse, the Leiden Manifesto and Metric Tide provided principles for responsible metric use, and CoARA created an implementation coalition with concrete commitments - reinforcing rather than contradicting one another.
explanation_incorrect: They are distinct but complementary, not contradictory, identical, metric-mandating, or limited to CoARA.

question: Why does CoARA's agreement call for 'abandoning inappropriate uses' of the JIF and h-index rather than banning metrics outright?
option_a: Because CoARA has deliberately chosen to take no formal position on the use of metrics whatsoever
option_b: Because every single quantitative metric is, without exception, considered inherently and irredeemably harmful
option_c: Because formally and legally banning the use of specific metrics is considered practically impossible
option_d: Because the problem is misuse of metrics as quality proxies, not their existence
option_e: Because CoARA, somewhat surprisingly, actually prefers relying primarily on the Journal Impact Factor
explanation_correct: CoARA targets the inappropriate use of specific metrics (e.g., JIF/h-index as stand-ins for quality in hiring and funding) while allowing responsible, contextual quantitative input alongside qualitative peer judgement - reform of use, not prohibition of all metrics.
explanation_incorrect: CoARA does not claim all metrics are harmful, lack a position, cite legal impossibility, or favor the JIF; it distinguishes misuse from responsible use.

question: What is the 'Matthew effect' in the context of research recognition and metrics?
option_a: Eminent researchers accrue disproportionately more recognition and citations
option_b: A specific type of statistical error that occurs when counting citations
option_c: A particular method used for normalizing citation counts across fields
option_d: A specific type of open license applied to research publications
option_e: A form of peer-review bias that specifically works against senior researchers
explanation_correct: The Matthew effect describes cumulative advantage: prominence attracts further citations, funding, and attention regardless of intrinsic merit, so citation-based metrics can entrench existing hierarchies rather than neutrally measure quality.
explanation_incorrect: It is a cumulative-advantage phenomenon, not a counting error, normalization method, license, or anti-senior bias.

question: Why are altmetrics considered complementary indicators rather than measures of research quality?
option_a: Because altmetrics actually count citations with greater accuracy than traditional methods
option_b: Because altmetrics track only attention and uptake within academic circles
option_c: Because every piece of altmetric data has itself been through peer review
option_d: Because altmetrics are designed to fully replace traditional citation analysis
option_e: Because they capture attention, which can be gamed and doesn't indicate scholarly merit
explanation_correct: Altmetrics measure how much and where an output is discussed, but attention can stem from controversy or promotion and can be manipulated; they usefully complement citations by showing broader reach, yet do not certify quality.
explanation_incorrect: Altmetrics do not measure quality, are not peer reviewed, do not replace citations, and track non-academic attention too.

question: A committee wants to assess a candidate's 'contribution to team science' fairly. Which approach best reflects responsible assessment principles?
option_a: Use CRediT and narrative evidence to recognize varied roles, not just lead-author JIF outputs
option_b: Rank all of the candidates by summing the Journal Impact Factors of their first-author papers
option_c: Simply count the candidate's total number of publications and stop there
option_d: Use the candidate's raw h-index score as the sole deciding criterion
option_e: Require that the candidate have only ever published sole-authored papers
explanation_correct: Recognizing team contributions responsibly means using contributor-role information (CRediT), narrative evidence, and a range of output types to value collaboration, rather than metrics that reward only first/last-author placement in high-JIF venues.
explanation_incorrect: JIF sums, raw counts, sole-metric h-index use, and sole-authorship requirements all misvalue collaborative contributions.

question: Why does the open-research-information movement (e.g., the Barcelona Declaration) argue that assessment reform depends on open metadata infrastructure?
option_a: Because openly licensed bibliographic data happens to be simply cheaper for institutions to purchase overall
option_b: Because reproducible evaluation needs the underlying data to be open and independently verifiable
option_c: Because moving to open metadata infrastructure entirely eliminates the need for any metrics whatsoever
option_d: Because proprietary citation databases are, without exception, always completely inaccurate in their coverage
option_e: Because the open research information movement specifically mandates universal use of the Journal Impact Factor
explanation_correct: Reforming assessment toward transparency and reproducibility is undercut if the data feeding evaluations is closed and unverifiable; open research information (open metadata, open citations) lets analyses be checked and reduces dependence on opaque commercial sources.
explanation_incorrect: The argument links reform to auditable open data, not cost, metric elimination, blanket claims of inaccuracy, or JIF mandates.

question: Why did eLife's shift to a 'publish-review-curate' model lead Web of Science to stop assigning it a Journal Impact Factor, and what does this reveal about the JIF's assumptions?
option_a: Because eLife's published articles simply began attracting far too few citations from other researchers overall
option_b: Because eLife formally merged its entire publishing operation with a separate, previously independent journal
option_c: Because eLife publishes reviewed preprints with assessments regardless of an accept/reject gate
option_d: Because eLife's entire publishing operation quietly switched over from open access to a subscription-based model
option_e: Because eLife made the deliberate decision to stop conducting any form of peer review on its submissions at all
explanation_correct: eLife's model publishes papers with public reviews and eLife Assessments rather than issuing binary accept/reject decisions; Web of Science's indexing (and thus JIF assignment) presumes conventional selective publishing, so eLife was only partially indexed - exposing the JIF's built-in assumption of accept/reject gatekeeping.
explanation_incorrect: eLife still peer reviews; the issue is model-fit with indexing rules, not citation volume, a subscription switch, or a merger.

question: What is a fundamental methodological criticism of the h-index beyond field and career-stage dependence?
option_a: It is a metric that, in practice, cannot actually be computed for any researcher
option_b: It somehow works by directly altering the content of the underlying papers themselves
option_c: It conflates productivity and impact into one number, insensitive to outliers
option_d: It is a metric that, by design, only ever applies to researchers working in physics
option_e: It is a metric that counts citations intended to negatively criticize a paper
explanation_correct: Once a paper exceeds h citations, additional citations do not raise the h-index, so a researcher with a few extremely influential papers is not distinguished from one with many moderately cited ones; the single number discards information about the citation distribution's shape.
explanation_incorrect: The h-index is computable, field-general (if imperfect), does not count 'negative' citations, and does not alter papers; the criticism is about information loss and insensitivity to outliers.

question: How can well-intentioned quantitative targets in research assessment produce 'perverse incentives', and what does responsible assessment propose instead?
option_a: Quantitative targets used in research assessment are entirely neutral and carry no meaningful behavioral side effects at all
option_b: The proposed solution is simply for every researcher to publish exclusively in the very highest-Impact-Factor journals available
option_c: The proposed solution is to impose even stricter, more demanding, and more narrowly numeric targets on individual researchers
option_d: Targets like publication counts can drive salami-slicing; responsible assessment favors qualitative judgement
option_e: The proposed solution is to remove the practice of formal peer review from the assessment process entirely and permanently
explanation_correct: Numeric targets can incentivize gaming and conservative, least-publishable-unit behavior that undermines good science; frameworks like CoARA, DORA, and the Leiden Manifesto respond by centering expert qualitative judgement, using metrics only as transparent, contextual support, and valuing a fuller range of contributions.
explanation_incorrect: Tighter targets, JIF-chasing, and removing review worsen or ignore the problem; the proposed remedy is responsible, judgement-led assessment.

question: Why is comparing absolute citation counts across time periods methodologically fraught, and how is this typically handled?
option_a: Publication volume grows over time and older papers accumulate more citations, so counts are normalized
option_b: Because major citation databases actively and routinely delete older citation records after a few years
option_c: Because individual citations are understood, under the rules of most databases, to simply disappear after one year
option_d: Because established journal policy dictates that only very recently published papers are eligible to be cited
option_e: It is not methodologically fraught in any way at all; raw, unadjusted citation counts remain perfectly stable and comparable
explanation_correct: Citation counts depend on how long a paper has existed and on the expanding size of the literature; fair comparison requires normalizing to publication year and field and/or using fixed citation windows, rather than comparing raw totals across eras.
explanation_incorrect: Counts are not stable across time, citations do not vanish yearly, older papers remain citable, and databases retain old citations; the fix is temporal/field normalization.

question: What is a 'predatory' journal or publisher?
option_a: A journal whose editorial policy is to publish only negative or null results
option_b: A journal that charges fees while failing to provide real editorial or review services
option_c: A journal that happens to have an unusually high journal impact factor
option_d: A journal that specifically requires all authors to share their underlying data
option_e: A journal that operates on a traditional paywalled subscription model
explanation_correct: Predatory journals take authors' money (typically APCs) but do not deliver genuine peer review, editing, or preservation, prioritizing profit over scholarship and often using deceptive practices.
explanation_incorrect: Predatory venues are defined by exploitation and lack of legitimate services, not by publishing negatives, high impact, open-data requirements, or the subscription model.

question: What is the purpose of the 'Think. Check. Submit.' checklist?
option_a: To rank scholarly journals against each other according to their citation impact
option_b: To help researchers identify trustworthy journals and publishers before submitting
option_c: To handle the technical process of assigning DOIs to new publications
option_d: To calculate and publish the total citation counts of individual articles
option_e: To scan submitted manuscripts and detect instances of textual plagiarism
explanation_correct: Think. Check. Submit. provides a practical checklist (about the journal's transparency, editorial board, indexing, contact details, etc.) to help authors evaluate whether a journal is trustworthy before submitting their work.
explanation_incorrect: It is a vetting checklist for choosing venues, not a ranking, plagiarism tool, metric calculator, or DOI service.

question: What is a 'conflict of interest' in research?
option_a: Any ordinary disagreement that arises between two or more coauthors
option_b: A formal dispute that arises between an author and their peer reviewers
option_c: A citation that an author makes to the work of a scientific competitor
option_d: A secondary interest that could improperly influence the conduct of research
option_e: A simple scheduling clash between two overlapping research commitments
explanation_correct: A conflict of interest exists when competing interests - funding, employment, personal relationships, or the like - could bias, or be perceived to bias, research decisions or reporting; disclosure allows readers and editors to judge potential influence.
explanation_incorrect: A COI is about competing interests biasing the work, not ordinary disagreements, disputes, scheduling, or citations.

question: According to widely used guidance (e.g., ICMJE), can an AI tool like a large language model be listed as an author of a paper?
option_a: No - AI tools cannot be authors because they cannot take responsibility or be accountable for the work
option_b: Yes, an AI tool can be listed as the paper's first and primary author
option_c: Only if the AI tool in question has been assigned its own ORCID iD
option_d: Yes, provided the AI tool is responsible for writing most of the manuscript's text
option_e: Yes, this is considered acceptable across every academic field and discipline
explanation_correct: Authorship requires accountability and the ability to approve and be responsible for the work, which AI tools cannot meet; guidance from ICMJE, COPE, and publishers says AI cannot be an author but its use should be transparently disclosed in the methods or acknowledgements.
explanation_incorrect: AI cannot be an author regardless of how much text it produced, cannot be first author, and an ORCID iD does not change this.

question: What is 'salami slicing' in research publishing?
option_a: Deliberately combining several separate, unrelated studies into one single large paper
option_b: Splitting one study into multiple smaller papers to inflate publication counts
option_c: The practice of sharing a dataset with other researchers in smaller pieces
option_d: Conducting the peer-review process for a manuscript across several separate stages
option_e: Citing one's own previously published work within a newly submitted paper
explanation_correct: Salami slicing (salami publication) fragments what should be one study into several thin papers to inflate publication counts, which can distort the literature and mislead through redundant or overlapping reporting.
explanation_incorrect: It is inappropriate fragmentation, not merging studies, phased review, data sharing, or self-citation.

question: What is PubPeer?
option_a: A platform for posting post-publication comments and critiques of papers
option_b: A traditional peer-reviewed academic journal that publishes original research
option_c: A commercial database used to track and store scholarly citation records
option_d: A government or philanthropic agency that distributes competitive research funding
option_e: A server where researchers post manuscripts before formal peer review begins
explanation_correct: PubPeer is a website for post-publication peer review and discussion, where readers (often anonymously) raise questions about published papers - including image concerns, statistics, or possible misconduct - contributing to scientific self-correction.
explanation_incorrect: PubPeer is a commenting/critique platform, not a journal, citation database, funder, or preprint server.

question: What is the difference between a 'correction' and a 'retraction' of a published article?
option_a: A correction and a retraction are simply two different names for the same action
option_b: A correction results in the entire article being permanently removed
option_c: A retraction is generally considered a less serious action than a correction
option_d: A retraction is issued only to fix minor typographical errors in the text
option_e: A correction fixes an error while the article stays valid; a retraction withdraws it
explanation_correct: A correction (erratum/corrigendum) addresses a discrete error without invalidating the paper, whereas a retraction signals the work is unreliable or unethical and should not be relied upon; the article typically remains visible but is marked retracted.
explanation_incorrect: Retraction is more serious than correction; corrections do not remove articles, and retractions are not for typos.

question: What is 'informed consent' in research involving human participants?
option_a: A formal license governing how a dataset from the study may be shared
option_b: A financial agreement outlining how the study will be funded
option_c: A copyright license that governs how the resulting publication may be reused
option_d: A process ensuring participants understand a study and voluntarily agree to join
option_e: A specific stage within the manuscript's formal peer-review process
explanation_correct: Informed consent means participants are given adequate information (purpose, procedures, risks, benefits, voluntariness, right to withdraw) and freely agree to participate; it is a cornerstone of ethical human-subjects research.
explanation_incorrect: Consent is an ethics process for participants, not a funding, copyright, review, or data-sharing arrangement.

question: Why should authors disclose their use of generative AI tools in preparing a manuscript?
option_a: Because commercial generative AI tools require an ongoing paid subscription
option_b: Because generative AI tools are, under current guidance, considered legitimate authors
option_c: Because disclosing AI use is believed to artificially raise a journal's impact factor
option_d: To maintain transparency, since AI can introduce errors readers should know about
option_e: Because disclosing the use of AI tools is a formal requirement for obtaining a DOI
explanation_correct: Disclosing AI assistance (e.g., for drafting or editing) supports transparency and lets others assess how the manuscript was produced, given that AI can generate plausible but incorrect or fabricated material for which the human authors remain responsible.
explanation_incorrect: Disclosure is about transparency and accountability, not payment, authorship, metrics, or DOI assignment.

question: What is 'ghost authorship'?
option_a: Publishing a piece of work under a fictitious pen name rather than one's own
option_b: Serving as a peer reviewer for a manuscript while remaining fully anonymous
option_c: Including citations to authors who have since passed away
option_d: A substantial contributor is left off the author list, hiding their role
option_e: Arranging for a manuscript to be formally published after the author's death
explanation_correct: Ghost authorship occurs when a substantial contributor - frequently an industry-funded medical writer - is omitted from the author list, obscuring who actually shaped the paper and potential conflicts of interest.
explanation_incorrect: It is undisclosed substantial contribution, not pen names, citing the deceased, anonymous review, or posthumous publication.

question: What does an Institutional Review Board (IRB) or research ethics committee do?
option_a: Reviews research involving human participants to ensure it is ethical
option_b: Conducts formal peer review of manuscripts on behalf of academic journals
option_c: Calculates and assigns official journal impact factor scores
option_d: Handles the technical process of registering new DOIs for publications
option_e: Produces an annual ranking of universities based on research output
explanation_correct: An IRB / research ethics committee evaluates study protocols involving human subjects for ethical acceptability - risks, benefits, consent, privacy - before the research proceeds, safeguarding participants.
explanation_incorrect: An IRB oversees research ethics, not metrics, journal peer review, identifiers, or rankings.

question: What is 'self-plagiarism' (text recycling)?
option_a: Citing one's own earlier published work in an appropriate, transparent way
option_b: Reusing your own prior text or data without disclosure, presenting it as new
option_c: Making one's underlying research data openly available to other researchers
option_d: Posting an early preprint version of one's own paper before formal publication
option_e: Writing a comprehensive literature review summarizing one's field of study
explanation_correct: Self-plagiarism/text recycling means reproducing one's own prior published material (text, figures, data) in a new publication without transparency, which can mislead about originality and inflate output.
explanation_incorrect: Legitimate self-citation, open data, preprints of the same paper, and review writing are not self-plagiarism; the issue is undisclosed reuse presented as new.

question: What is a 'paper mill'?
option_a: A physical factory or industrial facility that manufactures rolls of paper
option_b: A formal training program designed to teach researchers how to peer review
option_c: A business that produces and sells fraudulent research manuscripts
option_d: A commercial database used specifically for tracking citation counts
option_e: A commercial service that handles the physical printing of journals
explanation_correct: Paper mills are organizations that mass-produce fake or manipulated papers - often with fabricated data and images - and sell authorship or publication, posing a serious threat to the integrity of the scholarly record.
explanation_incorrect: A paper mill is a fraud operation, not a printing service, training program, or citation index.

question: Why is transparent reporting of funding sources important for research integrity?
option_a: It is the factor that ultimately determines a journal's article processing charge
option_b: It reveals potential conflicts of interest that might have shaped the study
option_c: It provides an absolute guarantee that the study's results are correct
option_d: It simply makes the resulting published article noticeably longer
option_e: It is the mechanism that formally assigns authorship credit on a paper
explanation_correct: Disclosing who funded research lets readers and editors assess possible bias, since funders may have interests in particular outcomes; transparency supports trust even when no bias occurred.
explanation_incorrect: Funding disclosure addresses potential conflicts, not article length, correctness, fees, or authorship.

question: Under the ICMJE authorship criteria, which is NOT sufficient on its own to qualify someone as an author?
option_a: Agreement to be accountable for the work
option_b: Acquiring funding or general supervision alone
option_c: Final approval of the version to be published
option_d: Substantial contributions to conception or design
option_e: Drafting or critically revising the work
explanation_correct: ICMJE requires all four criteria (substantial contribution; drafting/revising; final approval; accountability). Activities like obtaining funding, collecting data, or general supervision alone do not, by themselves, justify authorship and should be acknowledged instead.
explanation_incorrect: The other listed items are among the four required criteria; funding acquisition or supervision alone is explicitly insufficient.

question: What is 'gift' (or honorary) authorship, and why is it a problem?
option_a: Simply citing a colleague's earlier published work within one's own paper
option_b: Naming a non-contributor as an author, e.g., out of deference
option_c: Formally thanking a study's funders in the acknowledgements section
option_d: Listing a mentor who provided genuine guidance throughout the project
option_e: Adding a data collector's name to the paper's acknowledgements section
explanation_correct: Gift/honorary authorship credits someone who did not substantially contribute - often a senior figure added by custom or pressure - which misrepresents who did the work and undermines the accountability authorship is meant to convey.
explanation_incorrect: Legitimately crediting real contributors, thanking funders, citing colleagues, or acknowledging helpers is not gift authorship; the problem is naming non-contributors as authors.

question: What is an 'expression of concern' issued by a journal?
option_a: A minor correction that fixes a single typographical error in the text
option_b: A formal letter rejecting a manuscript that was submitted for publication
option_c: A notice alerting readers to unresolved concerns during an investigation
option_d: A formal notice that permanently withdraws a published article altogether
option_e: A written report produced by a reviewer during the peer-review process
explanation_correct: An expression of concern is a formal notice flagging credible unresolved worries about a paper's integrity or reliability - for example during an investigation - without yet correcting or retracting it, so readers can exercise caution.
explanation_incorrect: It is neither a rejection, a minor correction, a full retraction, nor a review report; it signals unresolved concern pending resolution.

question: Why do fabricated or manipulated images pose a particular integrity challenge in fields like biomedicine?
option_a: Because scientific images are generally considered to carry no real scientific value
option_b: Because, in practice, every scientific image published is ultimately fabricated
option_c: Because scientific images are, as a rule, never actually published in journals
option_d: Because the authenticity of a published image can never be technically checked
option_e: Figures can be duplicated or altered, and detecting this needs forensic screening
explanation_correct: Image integrity issues - inappropriate duplication, splicing, or manipulation of figures - are a frequent basis for corrections and retractions, and identifying them requires trained screening or software, making them a significant and hard-to-detect integrity problem.
explanation_incorrect: Images can be and are checked (increasingly with software); they are widely published and scientifically important, and most are not fabricated.

question: How do 'hijacked' (clone) journals threaten research integrity?
option_a: Because this kind of fraud is understood to only ever affect subscription-based journals
option_b: Because fraudulent journals of this kind end up assigning far too many DOIs
option_c: Because these journals are, somewhat paradoxically, known for providing genuinely free peer review
option_d: Because journals affected in this way end up publishing far too many open access articles
option_e: Fake websites impersonate real journals to collect fees and deceive authors
