explanation_correct: A hijacked journal is a counterfeit that mimics a genuine journal's title, ISSN, or website to trick authors into submitting and paying, producing deceptive publications and undermining trust in the impersonated journal.
explanation_incorrect: The threat is impersonation and deception, not too much OA, free review, DOI volume, or a subscription-only scope.
review_note: Verify a stable source for hijacked-journal checker; Retraction Watch/Cabells maintain lists.

question: What is problematic about citing a retracted paper as if its findings were valid?
option_a: Nothing at all; the findings of retracted papers generally remain fully reliable
option_b: Retracted papers are, as a matter of policy, simply impossible to cite at all
option_c: Doing so always ends up improving the quality of the paper citing it
option_d: Doing so has the effect of formally correcting the original retraction
option_e: It spreads discredited claims that have been formally withdrawn
explanation_correct: Citing a retracted article uncritically perpetuates findings that the community has judged unreliable or unethical; authors should check retraction status (e.g., via Crossref/Retraction Watch data) and avoid relying on withdrawn work.
explanation_incorrect: Retracted findings are not reliable, citing them does not help the citing paper or fix the retraction, and while discouraged, such citation is technically possible and unfortunately common.

question: What is a 'fake peer review' scandal, as documented by COPE and publishers?
option_a: It refers to a scandal in which peer reviewers are secretly paid for their reports
option_b: Authors or agents manipulating the review process
option_c: It refers to a situation in which journal editors personally review every submitted paper
option_d: It refers to a practice in which reviewers voluntarily sign their names to their reports
option_e: It refers to the broader practice of conducting peer review openly and transparently
explanation_correct: Fake/manipulated peer review occurs when the process is gamed - for instance, providing fake reviewer contacts the author controls - so bogus positive reviews are returned; discovery has led publishers to retract large batches of affected papers.
explanation_incorrect: The problem is fraudulent manipulation of review, not paying reviewers, open review, signed reports, or editor involvement.

question: Why is disclosing generative-AI use in research increasingly formalized in journal policies, and what remains the authors' responsibility?
option_a: Because once its use is disclosed, the AI tool itself formally becomes a listed coauthor
option_b: Because current journal policy only ever requires disclosing AI use for generated images
option_c: Because disclosing AI use has the legal effect of transferring liability to the AI vendor
option_d: Because disclosing AI use provides an absolute guarantee that the resulting text is original
option_e: Because AI can fabricate errors, though human authors remain fully responsible
explanation_correct: Journals now commonly require authors to state how generative AI was used, precisely because such tools can produce inaccurate or invented content; crucially, accountability stays with the human authors, who must verify all content regardless of AI assistance.
explanation_incorrect: Disclosure does not make AI an author, transfer liability, guarantee originality, or apply only to images; responsibility remains with the authors.

question: What is 'duplicate' (redundant) publication?
option_a: Simply publishing a formal correction to an already-published article
option_b: Publishing overlapping content in more than one venue as if each were original
option_c: Translating an already-published paper into another language with permission
option_d: Publishing a preprint and later a journal version of the same paper
option_e: Publishing a short abstract summarizing a study at an academic conference
explanation_correct: Duplicate/redundant publication is republishing the same or largely overlapping work in multiple places without transparency, which distorts the literature, wastes resources, and can double-count evidence in meta-analyses.
explanation_incorrect: Transparent preprint-then-journal, authorized translations, corrections, and conference abstracts are generally acceptable; the issue is undisclosed redundant publication.

question: What role does the COPE 'flowcharts' resource serve for editors?
option_a: It exists mainly to help editors formally rank competing journals
option_b: It is designed specifically to replace the role that human editors currently play
option_c: It handles the technical process of assigning DOIs to published articles
option_d: It calculates and publishes official journal impact factor figures
option_e: It gives editors step-by-step guidance for handling ethics cases fairly
explanation_correct: COPE flowcharts give editors practical, standardized procedures for responding to ethics problems - what to check, whom to contact, and how to escalate - promoting consistent, fair handling of cases.
explanation_incorrect: The flowcharts guide case handling, not journal ranking, DOI assignment, metrics, or replacing editorial judgement.

question: Why is 'tortured phrases' a red flag investigators look for in potentially fraudulent papers?
option_a: Because phrases of this kind are generally considered a hallmark of genuinely excellent writing
option_b: Because using unusual paraphrased terminology is typically taken as a signal of open access publishing
option_c: Because rewording standard technical terms in this way is thought to improve overall clarity
option_d: Odd paraphrases of standard terms can signal text manipulation used to evade detection
option_e: Because using this kind of unusual paraphrasing is a stylistic requirement of most journal style guides
explanation_correct: 'Tortured phrases' are awkward substitutions for established terminology that often result from software rewording text to dodge plagiarism checkers; their presence has helped identify machine-generated or paper-mill content.
explanation_incorrect: Such phrases signal likely manipulation, not good writing, clarity, style compliance, or open access.

question: What is the purpose of a data or image integrity screening step before publication?
option_a: To simply increase the total number of figures included in a manuscript
option_b: To handle the formal process of assigning a reuse license to the work
option_c: To produce a formal ranking of the paper's listed authors
option_d: To detect problems like image manipulation before an article is published
option_e: To help speed up the technical process of typesetting the final article
explanation_correct: Pre-publication integrity screening (increasingly aided by software) checks figures and data for manipulation, duplication, or anomalies, catching issues early and protecting the integrity of the published record.
explanation_incorrect: Screening aims to catch problems, not add figures, rank authors, license the work, or speed typesetting.

question: Why are paper mills considered a systemic (not just individual) threat to the scholarly record?
option_a: Because they industrialize fraud at scale, contaminating the literature and outpacing detection
option_b: Because the existence of paper mills, on balance, actually improves overall scientific reproducibility
option_c: Because the fraud produced by paper mills is understood to affect only one individual author at a time
option_d: Because paper mills are believed to exclusively and deliberately target predatory journals
option_e: Because fraudulent papers produced by mills are, in practice, easily detected by every journal editor
explanation_correct: Paper mills operate at industrial scale, injecting large volumes of fake research and gaming review and authorship across many journals, which pollutes evidence syntheses and strains the community's ability to detect and correct it - a structural threat, not isolated misconduct.
explanation_incorrect: The threat is systemic scale, they harm reproducibility, they infiltrate legitimate journals too, and they are notoriously hard to detect.

question: How does opening retraction data (e.g., the Crossref/Retraction Watch database) strengthen research integrity infrastructure?
option_a: By deliberately hiding retraction notices from public view rather than surfacing them
option_b: It lets tools automatically flag retracted works, reducing inadvertent citation
option_c: By systematically deleting retracted papers from the scholarly record altogether
option_d: By using the opened retraction data to produce formal rankings of journals
option_e: By using the opened retraction data to entirely replace the peer-review process
explanation_correct: Open, machine-readable retraction data lets tools and platforms surface retraction notices at the point of citing or reading, helping authors and systems avoid relying on withdrawn work and improving the self-correcting capacity of the literature.
explanation_incorrect: Open retraction data increases visibility (not hiding or deletion), and is unrelated to ranking or replacing review.

question: Why do authorship disputes often arise, and how do frameworks like ICMJE and CRediT help address them?
option_a: Unclear expectations cause disputes; ICMJE and CRediT add clarity and reduce ambiguity
option_b: Authorship disputes of this kind are generally prevented by high journal impact factors
option_c: Disputes of this kind arise only in cases involving deliberate scientific fraud
option_d: They arise mainly because journals require far too few listed authors per paper
option_e: Disputes of this kind are typically resolved simply by comparing citation counts
explanation_correct: Disputes stem from ambiguity over contribution and credit; ICMJE clarifies the threshold for authorship and CRediT records precisely what each person did, so expectations and recognition are explicit and disagreements are less likely or easier to resolve.
explanation_incorrect: Disputes are not about too few authors, not solely fraud, and are not resolved by metrics; clear criteria and role documentation help.

question: What integrity risk arises specifically from generative AI's ability to fabricate plausible references and data, and what safeguard is emphasized?
option_a: The recommended safeguard is to let the AI system check and verify its own work
option_b: There is no real risk at all, since AI systems essentially never make errors
option_c: AI can fabricate citations; the safeguard is human verification of all AI-assisted content
option_d: The main risk described here is simply that manuscript writing becomes slower
option_e: The recommended safeguard is to remove every citation from the manuscript entirely
explanation_correct: Generative AI can 'hallucinate' realistic-looking references and data; because these can be hard to spot, guidance stresses that authors must verify every AI-assisted element and disclose AI use, keeping accountability with humans.
explanation_incorrect: AI does err, cannot be relied on to self-verify, the risk is fabrication not speed, and removing citations is not the safeguard.

question: How do questionable research practices (QRPs) like p-hacking and selective reporting differ from outright misconduct (FFP), and why do they still harm the literature?
option_a: QRPs exploit analytic flexibility without meeting the definition of misconduct, yet still bias findings
option_b: QRPs of this kind are, in practice, actively encouraged and rewarded by most journals
option_c: Practices like p-hacking are generally considered far worse than outright fabrication
option_d: Practices like p-hacking and selective reporting have essentially no effect on published results
option_e: QRPs of this kind are, by definition, always the result of deliberate, intentional fraud
explanation_correct: QRPs occupy a grey zone: they are not the clear-cut fabrication/falsification/plagiarism of formal misconduct, but by capitalizing on flexible analysis and selectively reporting, they distort the evidence base and contribute to the reproducibility crisis - often without conscious intent to deceive.
explanation_incorrect: QRPs are generally less clear-cut than FFP, not necessarily intentional, do affect results, and are discouraged, not encouraged.
review_note: John, Loewenstein & Prelec 2012 on QRPs; verify link resolves.

question: Why is defining 'authorship accountability' central to debates about AI and large collaborations in research?
option_a: Because the level of accountability an author holds is ultimately set by their citation counts
option_b: Because responsibility for a paper's integrity ultimately transfers entirely to the publishing journal
option_c: Because, under current norms, only the very first listed author is ever held accountable
option_d: Because authorship accountability is, in practice, treated as an entirely optional consideration
option_e: Authorship confers responsibility; AI cannot bear it, and large teams strain individual accountability
explanation_correct: Authorship is fundamentally about accountability for the work's integrity; this is why AI - unable to be accountable - cannot be an author, and why massive multi-author collaborations use contributorship statements, guarantors, and consortium models to preserve traceable responsibility.
explanation_incorrect: Accountability is not optional, not limited to the first author, not held by the journal, and not defined by metrics.

question: What structural incentives are frequently blamed for driving misconduct and QRPs, and how does research-assessment reform aim to address them?
option_a: The specific incentive to formally replicate other researchers' prior work
option_b: 'Publish or perish' pressures reward publication counts and novelty over rigor
option_c: The specific incentive to preregister a study's hypotheses before data collection
option_d: The specific incentive to openly share the underlying data behind a study
option_e: The specific incentive to publish results that turn out to be negative or null
explanation_correct: When careers hinge on volume, novelty, and prestige metrics, researchers face pressure toward QRPs and, at the extreme, misconduct; reforms like DORA and CoARA aim to change what is rewarded - valuing methodological rigor, openness, replication, and honest reporting - to reduce these perverse incentives.
explanation_incorrect: Sharing data, preregistration, replication, and publishing negatives are remedies, not the incentives blamed; the culprit is metric- and volume-driven prestige pressure.

question: Why is the scholarly community's capacity for 'self-correction' both a strength and a vulnerability, as illustrated by paper mills and mass retractions?
option_a: Self-correction of this kind is, in practice, handled entirely by external research funders
option_b: The scholarly community's capacity for self-correction is, in every respect, purely a strength
option_c: Self-correction can catch errors, but limited effort means fraud can outpace it
option_d: The process of self-correction in science happens completely automatically and instantaneously
option_e: The scholarly community's capacity for self-correction is, in every respect, purely a weakness
explanation_correct: Mechanisms like PubPeer scrutiny, replication, and retraction embody science's self-correcting ideal, but they rely on scarce human attention and imperfect detection; paper mills exploit this by producing fraud faster than it can be found, and retractions frequently arrive years after the damage.
explanation_incorrect: Self-correction is neither purely a strength nor purely a weakness; it is not automatic/instant and is not funder-run - it depends on stretched community effort.

question: How does the tension between anonymity and accountability play out in post-publication peer review platforms like PubPeer?
option_a: There is, in fact, no real tension between anonymity and accountability on such platforms
option_b: Allowing commenters to remain anonymous provides an absolute guarantee of factual accuracy
option_c: Comments posted anonymously on such platforms are, without exception, always false
option_d: Anonymity protects critics but can also enable unfounded or malicious claims
option_e: Genuine accountability on such platforms can only be achieved by banning anonymity outright
explanation_correct: Allowing anonymous critique lowers the risk of reprisal and surfaces concerns that might otherwise stay hidden, yet it can also permit baseless attacks; the resolution is to evaluate the substance and evidence of claims rather than trusting or dismissing them based on identity.
explanation_incorrect: The tension is real; anonymity neither guarantees accuracy nor makes comments always false, and productive platforms manage rather than simply ban it.

question: What is a computational notebook (e.g., Jupyter, R Markdown, Quarto) useful for in reproducible research?
option_a: A secure place specifically designed for storing login passwords
option_b: Combining code, outputs, and narrative in one rerunnable document
option_c: A tool used specifically for ranking academic journals
option_d: A tool intended to entirely replace the formal peer-review process
option_e: A service used specifically for assigning persistent DOIs
explanation_correct: Computational notebooks interleave executable code, results (tables, figures), and prose, letting others follow and re-execute the exact analytical steps - supporting transparency and computational reproducibility.
explanation_incorrect: Notebooks are for literate, runnable analysis, not password storage, ranking, review, or identifier assignment.

question: Why is sharing analysis code alongside a paper important for reproducibility?
option_a: Sharing the code is thought to remove the need to share any underlying data
option_b: It lets others see how data were processed and rerun the analysis
option_c: Sharing the analysis code is believed to directly raise a journal's impact factor
option_d: Sharing the analysis code is a formal requirement for obtaining a DOI
option_e: Sharing the code mainly serves to make the resulting paper noticeably shorter
explanation_correct: Publishing the code makes the analytical steps transparent and allows independent verification and reuse; text descriptions of methods are often too incomplete to reproduce results without the actual code.
explanation_incorrect: Sharing code supports verification, not shorter papers, metrics, DOIs, or replacing the data.

question: What is protocols.io used for?
option_a: A service used specifically for the long-term storage of research datasets
option_b: Creating and sharing detailed, versioned research protocols
option_c: A tool used specifically to rank published protocols by their citation count
option_d: A service used specifically for registering researchers' ORCID iDs
option_e: A tool used by publishers specifically to detect plagiarism in manuscripts
explanation_correct: protocols.io is a platform for authoring, sharing, and updating detailed research protocols (often with DOIs), improving methodological transparency and letting others reproduce procedures exactly.
explanation_incorrect: It is a protocol-sharing platform, not a dataset store, ranking service, identifier registry, or plagiarism tool.

question: What is the 'replication crisis' (or reproducibility crisis)?
option_a: A general shortage of academic journals willing to publish new research
option_b: A situation in which far too many replication studies are being published
option_c: A general and pervasive lack of citations across the scientific literature
option_d: A broader crisis concerning how research data is stored and archived
option_e: Many published results are difficult or impossible to reproduce
explanation_correct: The replication crisis refers to widespread difficulty reproducing or replicating published findings - notably highlighted in psychology and biomedicine - prompting reforms in transparency, preregistration, and statistics.
explanation_incorrect: It is about results failing to reproduce/replicate, not journal shortages, an excess of replications, storage, or citations.

question: What is an 'electronic lab notebook' (ELN)?
option_a: A tablet device used casually for jotting down personal notes
option_b: A piece of software used specifically to manage bibliographic citations
option_c: Software for recording research activities, protocols, and data digitally
option_d: A specific type of preprint posted before formal peer review
option_e: A system used by journals specifically to manage manuscript submissions
explanation_correct: An ELN is digital software for documenting experiments, methods, and results, making records searchable, shareable, time-stamped, and easier to preserve and audit than paper lab notebooks - supporting reproducibility and integrity.
explanation_incorrect: An ELN is research documentation software, not a generic notepad, reference manager, preprint, or submission system.

question: Why should a random 'seed' be set and reported in analyses involving randomness (e.g., simulations, machine learning)?
option_a: So that the underlying computational analysis simply runs faster overall
option_b: So that the resulting paper accumulates a higher number of citations
option_c: So that operations involving random numbers produce the same results every time
option_d: So that the final results of the analysis become fully encrypted
option_e: So that the resulting analysis can be formally assigned its own DOI
explanation_correct: Setting and reporting a random seed fixes the sequence of pseudo-random numbers, so a simulation, train/test split, or stochastic model yields identical results on re-execution - a small step that greatly aids computational reproducibility.
explanation_incorrect: A seed governs reproducible randomness, not speed, citations, encryption, or identifiers.

question: What are 'reporting guidelines' such as CONSORT (trials) and PRISMA (systematic reviews)?
option_a: Specialized software platforms used to manage the peer-review process
option_b: A formal set of rules governing how bibliographic references should be formatted
option_c: Systems used specifically for ranking academic journals against each other
option_d: Standardized legal frameworks that govern how research outputs may be licensed
option_e: Structured checklists specifying what information a study of a given type must report
explanation_correct: Reporting guidelines are checklists (e.g., CONSORT for randomized trials, PRISMA for systematic reviews, ARRIVE for animal studies) that define the minimum information needed for transparent, complete, and assessable reporting.
explanation_incorrect: They govern completeness of reporting, not reference formatting, rankings, licensing, or review software.

question: What is 'open materials' in the context of open methods?
option_a: Office supplies such as pens and paper that are provided completely free
option_b: Open access journal articles specifically, and nothing else
option_c: Making study materials like stimuli and protocols openly available for reuse
option_d: Textbooks that have entered the public domain and can be freely reused
option_e: Editorial pieces published openly by an academic journal
explanation_correct: Open materials means sharing the actual instruments and resources behind a study (surveys, stimuli, analysis code, protocols) so that others can scrutinize, reuse, and attempt to reproduce it - one of the components rewarded by Open Science Badges.
explanation_incorrect: It refers to research materials, not supplies, articles alone, textbooks, or editorials.

question: What does 'The Turing Way' provide to the research community?
option_a: A ranking that orders universities according to their research output
option_b: A tool used specifically for indexing and tracking scholarly citations
option_c: A commercial software package used for performing statistical analysis
option_d: A server specifically dedicated to hosting early preprint manuscripts
option_e: An open handbook of good practices for reproducible research
explanation_correct: The Turing Way is an openly licensed, community-authored guide covering reproducible research, project design, communication, collaboration, and ethics, serving as a widely used reference for good practice.
explanation_incorrect: It is an open handbook, not a ranking, commercial software, preprint server, or citation database.

question: What are 'Open Science Badges'?
option_a: Formal awards given specifically to a field's single most-cited paper each year
option_b: Formal certificates issued to individuals who serve as peer reviewers
option_c: Badges certifying that authors made data or materials openly available
option_d: Visual labels indicating that a journal has an unusually high impact factor
option_e: Name tags that identify attendees at academic conferences
explanation_correct: Open Science Badges (from the Center for Open Science) are earned incentives shown on articles indicating open data, open materials, or preregistration, nudging authors toward transparent practices.
explanation_incorrect: Badges certify openness practices, not citation counts, reviewer status, impact factors, or event attendance.

question: Why is documenting the software versions and dependencies used in an analysis important?
option_a: Documenting this information is mainly a way to make the resulting files smaller
option_b: Because results can change with different software or package versions
option_c: Documenting this information is believed to directly increase citation counts
option_d: Documenting this information is a formal requirement for assigning a software license
option_e: Documenting software versions is considered relevant only for physical computing hardware
explanation_correct: Analyses can produce different results under different versions of languages, packages, or libraries; capturing exact versions and dependencies (e.g., in an environment file) lets others rebuild the same environment and reproduce results.
explanation_incorrect: Recording dependencies supports reproducibility, not smaller files, citations, licensing, or hardware-only concerns.

question: What does 'preregistration' primarily help distinguish?
option_a: It primarily helps distinguish raw underlying data from analysis code
option_b: It primarily helps distinguish the paper's authors from its peer reviewers
option_c: It primarily helps distinguish open access publishing from subscription publishing
option_d: Confirmatory analyses planned in advance from exploratory ones
option_e: It primarily helps distinguish journals from institutional repositories
explanation_correct: By recording hypotheses and analysis plans before seeing the data, preregistration makes clear which analyses were planned (confirmatory) versus generated after looking at the data (exploratory), curbing practices like HARKing.
explanation_incorrect: Preregistration separates confirmatory from exploratory work, not the other pairs listed.

question: What is a 'container' (e.g., Docker) in the context of reproducible computation?
option_a: A physical box or crate used to store printed materials
option_b: A specific standardized format used for citing scholarly sources
option_c: A type of online service used specifically for storing research data
option_d: A specific category or type of academic journal
option_e: A packaged environment bundling code with its dependencies
explanation_correct: A container encapsulates an application together with its libraries and configuration, so the analysis behaves consistently across computers, greatly aiding reproducibility of complex computational workflows.
explanation_incorrect: A container is an environment-packaging technology, not physical storage, a repository, citation format, or journal.

question: Why is publishing negative or null results valuable for reproducibility and the literature?
option_a: Because negative or null results are, as a general rule, always incorrect
option_b: Because studies with negative or null results simply cannot undergo peer review
option_c: Because publishing negative results is believed to raise a journal's impact factor
option_d: Because omitting them creates publication bias
option_e: Because publishing this kind of result is a formal requirement for obtaining a DOI
explanation_correct: When only positive findings are published, the literature overstates effects and wastes effort on already-failed approaches; publishing null/negative results counters publication bias and supports accurate meta-analysis and reproducibility.
explanation_incorrect: Null results are legitimate and reviewable; the value is reducing bias, not metrics or DOIs, and they are not inherently wrong.

question: What did the Reproducibility Project: Psychology (2015) find?
option_a: The project found that psychology, as a field, has essentially no reproducibility issues at all
option_b: The project found that every single study included in the sample replicated perfectly
option_c: The project's overall conclusion was that replication is inherently impossible
option_d: The project concluded that fraud is the only possible explanation for non-replication
option_e: Many psychology studies did not reproduce when independently replicated
explanation_correct: The Open Science Collaboration's large-scale replication of 100 psychology studies found that a large share failed to replicate, with replicated effect sizes typically much smaller than the originals - a landmark empirical demonstration of the replication crisis.
explanation_incorrect: The project showed many did not replicate; it did not find perfect replication, prove replication impossible, attribute all failures to fraud, or dismiss the problem.

question: What is 'p-hacking'?
option_a: The unethical practice of directly stealing p-values from other researchers' work
option_b: Selectively reporting analyses that reach statistical significance
option_c: The practice of formally registering a study's hypotheses before collecting data
option_d: The practice of openly sharing one's analysis code alongside a publication
option_e: The straightforward, correct statistical procedure for computing a p-value
explanation_correct: P-hacking exploits analytic flexibility - testing multiple specifications, adding/dropping cases, or trying many outcomes - and reporting only significant results, which produces spurious 'significant' findings and undermines reproducibility.
explanation_incorrect: P-hacking is selective analysis/reporting to reach significance, not theft, preregistration, correct computation, or code sharing (which counter it).

question: What is Binder (mybinder.org) used for?
option_a: A traditional bookbinding service used to physically bind printed journal articles
option_b: Turning a code repository into a live, interactive computing environment
option_c: A cloud service used specifically for the permanent, long-term storage of large datasets
option_d: A service used specifically for assigning persistent DOIs to shared code
option_e: A platform used specifically for ranking different pieces of research software
explanation_correct: Binder builds an executable environment from a repository's contents and dependency files, letting anyone run the notebooks interactively online - lowering the barrier to reproducing and exploring an analysis.
explanation_incorrect: Binder provides on-demand runnable environments, not physical binding, permanent data storage, DOI minting, or software ranking.

question: How does the 'garden of forking paths' concept relate to reproducibility problems?
option_a: It describes a particular kind of network built from citation relationships between papers
option_b: It describes a specific technical layout used for organizing stored data files
option_c: Undeclared analytic choices can lead to false positives without conscious p-hacking
option_d: It refers to a standardized template used when writing a study preregistration
option_e: It refers to a specific type of software container used in computing
explanation_correct: The 'garden of forking paths' (Gelman and Loken) notes that researcher degrees of freedom - defensible choices made contingent on the data - can inflate false positives even without intentional p-hacking, which is why preregistration of analysis plans matters.
explanation_incorrect: It is a conceptual account of analytic flexibility and false positives, not a storage layout, container, template, or citation network.

question: Why is the American Statistical Association's guidance often summarized as 'a p-value is not a measure of effect size or importance'?
option_a: Because reporting an effect size alongside a p-value is technically illegal in most fields
option_b: Because p-values, understood correctly, are always fundamentally wrong to compute
option_c: Because a p-value reflects evidence against a null, not the size or importance of an effect
option_d: Because a p-value is understood to directly and precisely measure a study's reproducibility
option_e: Because a p-value is, mathematically, exactly the same thing as a confidence interval
explanation_correct: The ASA statement warns against conflating statistical significance with importance: a p-value speaks to compatibility with a null model, not to how large or consequential an effect is, so reporting effect sizes and uncertainty is essential.
explanation_incorrect: P-values are not always wrong, effect sizes are encouraged, p-values do not directly measure reproducibility, and they differ from confidence intervals.

question: What is the difference between 'methods reproducibility' and 'results reproducibility' as sometimes distinguished in the literature?
option_a: The two concepts are, in every meaningful respect, completely identical
option_b: Methods reproducibility is providing enough detail (code, data, protocols) to repeat the study exactly
option_c: Methods reproducibility is a concept that is understood to apply only to survey-based research
option_d: Results reproducibility is, by definition, always established before methods reproducibility
option_e: Neither of these two related concepts has anything to do with sharing code
explanation_correct: One useful distinction: methods reproducibility concerns whether the procedures are documented in enough detail to be repeated, whereas results reproducibility concerns whether repeating them actually yields the same findings - the former enables the latter.
explanation_incorrect: The two are distinct and ordered (methods enable results), apply broadly, and both can involve code.
review_note: Goodman, Fanelli, Ioannidis reproducibility taxonomy; verify link.

question: Why can proprietary or point-and-click software workflows hinder reproducibility compared with scripted analyses?
option_a: Using proprietary, point-and-click software for analysis is considered outright illegal
option_b: Scripted analyses are, in every single case, simply faster to run than GUI-based ones
option_c: Point-and-click software workflows are technically incapable of producing figures
option_d: Manual, GUI-based steps are hard to document exactly and repeat
option_e: Graphical software interfaces are what handle the technical process of assigning DOIs
explanation_correct: Clicking through menus leaves no complete, shareable record of what was done, so others (and the original analyst) struggle to reproduce it; a script captures the full sequence of operations, which can be inspected and re-executed exactly.
explanation_incorrect: The issue is documentability/repeatability, not speed, legality, figure capability, or DOIs.

question: What is the aim of workflow-management tools like Snakemake or Nextflow in reproducible research?
option_a: To produce a formal ranking that compares different analysis pipelines
option_b: To manage and coordinate the formal peer review of submitted manuscripts
option_c: To provide long-term, centralized storage for large research datasets
option_d: Automating multi-step analysis pipelines in a documented, rerunnable way
option_e: To handle the formal process of assigning open-source software licenses
explanation_correct: Workflow managers let researchers specify each processing step and its inputs/outputs so an entire pipeline can be reproduced, scaled, and rerun consistently across systems, with the workflow itself serving as documentation.
explanation_incorrect: They automate and document pipelines, not rank them, store data, license work, or review papers.
review_note: Verify Turing Way URL; Snakemake/Nextflow are standard examples.

question: How does a Registered Report guard against publication bias differently from simply preregistering a study?
option_a: The study is peer reviewed and accepted in principle before results are known
option_b: A Registered Report is a publishing format that only becomes relevant after the study has already been published
option_c: This publishing format works by guaranteeing in advance that the study's results will turn out positive
option_d: This particular publishing format is notable specifically for skipping the peer-review stage entirely
option_e: There is, in fact, no meaningful difference at all between this format and simple preregistration
explanation_correct: Preregistration records the plan but does not secure publication; a Registered Report adds pre-results peer review and in-principle acceptance, so outcome-independent publication is guaranteed, directly counteracting the file-drawer/publication-bias problem.
explanation_incorrect: Registered Reports are not identical to plain preregistration, do not guarantee positive results, include (earlier) peer review, and act before results are known.

question: Why is 'open data plus closed code' often insufficient for computational reproducibility?
option_a: Because the underlying dataset itself is considered entirely unnecessary for reproducibility
option_b: Without the code, others can't know how data were transformed into the results
option_c: Because the analysis code is thought to functionally replace the need for the data itself
option_d: Because open data, on its own, is never sufficient to achieve anything useful at all
option_e: Because keeping the analysis code closed actually makes the overall computation run faster
explanation_correct: Reproducing computational results generally requires both the data and the exact processing steps; if the code is unavailable, the transformation from raw data to results is opaque and cannot be faithfully repeated.
explanation_incorrect: Data is necessary, code does not replace it, open data is often useful on its own, and the point is transparency, not speed.

question: Why did the Reproducibility Project: Cancer Biology report that many original cancer studies could not even be fully assessed, beyond questions of whether they replicated?
option_a: Because the original cancer studies in question were later found to be outright fraudulent
option_b: Original papers lacked methodological detail, and authors often couldn't share materials
option_c: Because the field of cancer biology is considered fundamentally unreproducible as a matter of principle
option_d: Because, in fact, every single one of the original studies included ended up replicating successfully
option_e: Because attempting to replicate a published study is considered illegal under research regulations
explanation_correct: A striking finding of the Cancer Biology project was that incomplete reporting and lack of access to protocols, reagents, and data made many studies impossible to reproduce as written - a transparency failure distinct from outright replication success or failure.
explanation_incorrect: The barrier was missing detail and materials, not proven fraud, inherent impossibility, universal success, or legality.

question: How do preregistration and Registered Reports specifically counter HARKing and the garden of forking paths?
option_a: Because using these approaches is believed to eliminate the need for any statistical analysis at all
option_b: Because adopting these approaches automatically and mechanically increases a study's sample size
option_c: Fixing hypotheses in advance prevents HARKing and undeclared analytic flexibility
option_d: Because using these approaches provides an absolute, ironclad guarantee that a finding will replicate
option_e: Because these particular safeguards are understood to work only within formal clinical trials
explanation_correct: Committing to hypotheses and analyses in advance means researchers cannot later reframe exploratory findings as predictions (HARKing) or exploit data-dependent choices among many paths, both of which otherwise drive spurious confirmatory results.
explanation_incorrect: They constrain analytic flexibility, not sample size, do not guarantee replication or remove statistics, and apply well beyond clinical trials.

question: Why is a shared container image often considered stronger evidence of computational reproducibility than a dependency list alone?
option_a: Because containers are, by design, the mechanism used to formally assign persistent DOIs
option_b: Because a simple dependency list is technically incapable of ever being shared with others
option_c: Because sharing a plain dependency list is considered outright illegal under most licenses
option_d: A container captures the entire working environment as an executable artifact
option_e: Because a packaged container image is, in every case, simply smaller in file size than a list
explanation_correct: A dependency list may omit system-level libraries or resolve to different versions when rebuilt, whereas a container preserves the actual environment as a runnable artifact, making it more likely others obtain identical results.
explanation_incorrect: Containers are usually larger, lists are legal and shareable, and neither assigns DOIs; the advantage is fidelity of the captured environment.

question: What is the significance of 'many analysts, one dataset' studies for understanding reproducibility?
option_a: These kinds of studies conclusively prove that data analysis is an entirely objective process
option_b: Independent teams analyzing the same data can reach different results from analytic choices
option_c: These kinds of studies consistently show that independent analyst teams always reach identical conclusions
option_d: Studies of this kind are, in essence, primarily concerned with how data should be physically stored
option_e: Findings from these kinds of studies are understood to apply only within the field of physics
explanation_correct: Multi-analyst studies (e.g., Silberzahn et al.) revealed wide variation in outcomes when many teams analyzed identical data, demonstrating that reasonable methodological choices can materially change conclusions - underscoring the value of preregistration, multiverse analysis, and transparency.
explanation_incorrect: These studies reveal variability rather than objectivity or unanimity, concern analysis not storage, and generalize beyond physics.
review_note: Silberzahn et al. 2018; verify link resolves.

question: Why is documenting provenance and computational environment increasingly framed as part of making workflows 'FAIR'?
option_a: Because treating workflows as FAIR research objects lets others discover and rerun them
option_b: Because the FAIR principles, as originally framed, were designed to apply only to research data
option_c: Because recording provenance information is, in practice, considered largely irrelevant to reproducibility
option_d: Because computational workflows are, by their very nature, fundamentally incapable of being shared
option_e: Because the computational environments used to run analyses essentially never meaningfully change over time
explanation_correct: Extending FAIR to workflows (e.g., FAIR Principles for Research Software and workflow initiatives) recognizes that reproducible science needs the pipelines themselves to be documented, shareable, and rerunnable, with provenance and environment captured - not only the underlying data.
explanation_incorrect: Workflows can and should be shared; FAIR is being extended beyond data, provenance is central, and environments do change, which is why they must be recorded.
review_note: General FAIR-for-workflows framing; verify a representative source.

question: How does selective reporting of outcomes in clinical trials undermine reproducibility, and what mechanism addresses it?
option_a: Selective reporting of trial outcomes is generally understood to have no meaningful effect at all
option_b: The recommended mechanism for addressing this problem is to stop registering clinical trials altogether
option_c: Reporting only the most favorable outcomes actually tends to improve a study's overall reproducibility
option_d: Outcome-switching biases evidence; prospective trial registration lets others detect it
option_e: The recommended mechanism for addressing this problem is simply to publish trial results faster
explanation_correct: When trialists emphasize outcomes that turned out significant and drop pre-specified ones, results are biased and hard to reproduce; mandatory prospective registration of primary outcomes enables comparison of what was planned versus reported, deterring outcome-switching.
explanation_incorrect: Selective reporting harms reproducibility, registration is the safeguard (not its removal), and speed is not the issue.

question: Why is 'reproducible' not the same as 'correct'?
option_a: The two concepts, reproducible and correct, are, in every practical sense, exactly the same thing
option_b: An analysis can be reproducible yet still be based on flawed data or wrong assumptions
option_c: An analysis being reproducible is understood to always and necessarily mean that it is also correct
option_d: An analysis being correct is understood to always and necessarily mean that it is also reproducible
option_e: Neither of these two related concepts actually matters when evaluating a piece of research
explanation_correct: Reproducibility ensures the reported results follow from the stated data and code, enabling scrutiny; but it says nothing about whether the data are sound or the methods appropriate, so a reproducible result can still be wrong.
explanation_incorrect: Reproducibility and correctness are distinct: the former is about consistency of the computation, not the validity of the science.

question: Why do some scholars argue that a degree of non-replication is a normal and even healthy feature of science rather than a pure 'crisis'?
option_a: Because the concept of replication is, in the view of these scholars, simply not important at all
option_b: Because attempting to replicate an existing study is, by definition, itself a form of scientific fraud
option_c: Because these scholars hold that all published scientific findings are equally and uniformly true
option_d: Because these scholars believe that science, as an enterprise, should never attempt to self-correct
option_e: Variability in complex systems means some non-replication is expected and can inform discovery
explanation_correct: As the National Academies noted, some non-replication legitimately arises from the complexity and stochasticity of what is studied and can drive discovery; the crisis framing is better aimed at the avoidable causes - bias, questionable practices, and inadequate transparency - than at all non-replication.
explanation_incorrect: The argument distinguishes expected from avoidable non-replication; it does not dismiss replication, claim all findings are true, oppose self-correction, or equate replication with fraud.

question: How does the incentive structure of academic publishing contribute to the reproducibility crisis, and what reforms target this?
option_a: The incentive structures of academic publishing are understood to play no meaningful role in this problem at all
option_b: The proposed solution is simply to place even greater editorial emphasis on producing novel findings
option_c: A preference for novel results over replications discourages verification; reforms reward rigor instead
option_d: The proposed solution favored by these reformers is to ban the use of statistical methods altogether
option_e: The reproducibility crisis is, in fact, considered to be entirely unrelated to publishing incentives
explanation_correct: Because journals and evaluators have favored striking positive findings, researchers are pushed toward practices that reduce reproducibility and away from replication; structural fixes - outcome-independent publishing, registered replications, transparency mandates, and reforming what counts in assessment - realign incentives with reliability.
explanation_incorrect: Incentives matter; the remedy is less novelty-chasing and more rigor/transparency, not banning statistics or ignoring incentives.

question: What is a 'multiverse analysis', and how does it improve transparency about analytic flexibility?
option_a: A technique in which every result except the single best-looking one is simply deleted
option_b: An approach in which a single analysis is run once and its result is simply reported as-is
option_c: A speculative technique that involves analyzing data collected from parallel universes
option_d: Reporting how results vary across reasonable analytic choices
option_e: A specific technical term referring to a type of software container used in computing
explanation_correct: A multiverse (or specification-curve) analysis runs and reports the outcome across the full set of defensible analytic choices, making the dependence of conclusions on those choices explicit rather than hidden - directly countering selective reporting and the garden of forking paths.
explanation_incorrect: It exposes, rather than hides, the effect of analytic choices; it is not single-run reporting, cherry-picking, or a container.
review_note: Steegen et al. 2016 multiverse analysis; verify link.

question: Why can strict computational reproducibility still fail to be achievable over long timescales even with shared code and data, and what mitigations exist?
option_a: Long-term computational reproducibility, in practice, essentially never fails once code is shared
option_b: Software ecosystems evolve and old code may stop running; mitigations include containerization
option_c: Because attempting to reproduce results is considered legally prohibited after just one year
option_d: Because the underlying data involved is understood to simply disappear instantly over time
option_e: Because academic journals, as a matter of policy, routinely delete the code they receive
explanation_correct: Even well-shared analyses can stop running years later as languages, libraries, and platforms change or vanish; strategies like containerizing and archiving the full environment, preferring open formats, and emulation help preserve executability, though none is a permanent guarantee.
explanation_incorrect: Long-term reproducibility is genuinely fragile due to evolving software stacks; the mitigations are preservation-oriented, not claims that nothing fails or that code is deleted by rule.
review_note: Verify Turing Way URL; 'software collapse' term from Konrad Hinsen.

question: What is the main purpose of digital preservation services like CLOCKSS and Portico?
option_a: To handle the technical process of assigning DOIs to journal content
option_b: To calculate and publish official journal impact factor scores
option_c: To ensure scholarly content remains accessible in the long term
option_d: To scan published content and detect instances of plagiarism
option_e: To produce an annual ranking of academic journals by prestige
explanation_correct: CLOCKSS, Portico, and similar dark or trigger archives preserve digital scholarly content so it survives publisher closures, platform loss, or catastrophic events, protecting the long-term scholarly record.
explanation_incorrect: Preservation services safeguard content over time; they are not ranking, DOI, plagiarism, or metrics services.

question: What does OpenAIRE do?
option_a: Sells discounted airline tickets specifically to traveling researchers
option_b: Handles the technical process of assigning ORCID iDs to researchers
option_c: Directly publishes its own line of peer-reviewed academic journals
option_d: Provides open infrastructure to support open science across Europe
option_e: Produces an annual ranking of European universities by research output
explanation_correct: OpenAIRE is a European-rooted nonprofit infrastructure that aggregates metadata on research outputs, supports open-science policy and practice, and offers services connecting publications, data, software, and funding across a network of national hubs.
explanation_incorrect: OpenAIRE is open-science infrastructure, not a publisher, identifier registry, ranking body, or travel service.

question: What is the Public Knowledge Project (PKP) best known for?
option_a: A commercial citation database sold on a subscription basis
option_b: A government funding agency that distributes competitive research grants
option_c: Developing free, open-source software such as Open Journal Systems
option_d: A commercial firm that produces annual university rankings
option_e: A preprint server where researchers post manuscripts before review
explanation_correct: PKP develops open-source publishing software, most prominently Open Journal Systems (OJS), which powers thousands of journals worldwide, especially supporting low-cost and diamond open-access publishing.
explanation_incorrect: PKP is an open-source software project for publishing, not a citation database, ranking, funder, or preprint server.

question: What is COAR (the Confederation of Open Access Repositories)?
option_a: A single, centralized global repository that hosts all open access content
option_b: A commercial vendor that sells repository software to institutions
option_c: An association that helps align open-access repositories worldwide
option_d: A citation index used to track how often research outputs are referenced
option_e: A funding body that distributes grants for repository development
explanation_correct: COAR is a global, member-based association that promotes and coordinates repositories - developing interoperability, shared practices, and initiatives like COAR Notify - to strengthen the worldwide network of open-access repositories.
explanation_incorrect: COAR is a repository-community association, not a vendor, a single repository, a citation index, or a funder.

question: What service does BASE (Bielefeld Academic Search Engine) provide?
option_a: A search engine indexing metadata of open documents worldwide
option_b: A formal peer-review service offered directly to academic journals
option_c: A commercial platform for selling discounted academic books
option_d: A service that produces formal rankings of individual researchers
option_e: A registration agency that handles the technical process of assigning DOIs
explanation_correct: BASE, run by Bielefeld University Library, is one of the largest search engines for open-access and repository content, harvesting metadata from thousands of sources to help users discover scholarly documents.
explanation_incorrect: BASE is a discovery/search service, not a bookseller, DOI agency, ranking, or review service.

question: What is CORE (core.ac.uk) known for?
option_a: An annual ranking service that ranks universities by research output
option_b: A commercial platform that sells journal subscription packages
option_c: A repository that hosts only research datasets and nothing else
option_d: A registry that handles the technical process of assigning ORCID iDs
option_e: Aggregating access to millions of open-access research papers
explanation_correct: CORE aggregates open-access full-text research papers from repositories and journals worldwide, offering search, text, and data-mining services and APIs over one of the largest collections of OA content.
explanation_incorrect: CORE is an OA aggregator/discovery service, not a subscription seller, ranking body, identifier registry, or data-only host.

question: What is the Center for Open Science (COS) primarily known for building?
option_a: A commercial citation database sold to libraries on subscription
option_b: A government funding agency that distributes competitive research grants
option_c: The Open Science Framework and initiatives like preregistration
option_d: A commercial firm that produces an annual ranking of universities
option_e: A subscription-based academic journal covering open science topics
explanation_correct: COS is a nonprofit that builds the Open Science Framework (a free project-management and sharing platform) and drives open-science initiatives including preregistration, Registered Reports, Open Science Badges, and the TOP Guidelines.
explanation_incorrect: COS builds open tools and initiatives, not a commercial database, ranking, subscription journal, or funder.

question: What is the Open Science Framework (OSF)?
option_a: A traditional peer-reviewed journal that publishes original research articles
option_b: A free platform to manage, share, and preregister research projects
option_c: A specific citation metric used to measure a researcher's overall impact
option_d: A government funding scheme that distributes competitive research grants
option_e: A commercial cloud storage platform available exclusively to private industry
explanation_correct: The OSF, from the Center for Open Science, is a free web platform supporting the whole research workflow - organizing projects, connecting storage and tools, sharing outputs, and preregistering studies - to promote transparency and collaboration.
explanation_incorrect: The OSF is a research-workflow platform, not a journal, metric, funding scheme, or industry-only cloud.

question: What kind of organization is DataCite?
option_a: A commercial publisher that sells subscriptions to its own journal titles
option_b: A nonprofit DOI registration agency for research data and software
option_c: A commercial agency that produces annual rankings of research institutions
option_d: A social networking platform designed specifically for academic researchers
option_e: A government council that distributes competitive research funding grants
explanation_correct: DataCite is a nonprofit membership organization and DOI registration agency focused on making research data and other outputs citable and findable through DOIs and rich metadata, and it co-operates services like ROR.
explanation_incorrect: DataCite is nonprofit PID infrastructure, not a publisher, ranking agency, social network, or funder.

question: Why are Crossref, DataCite, and ORCID sometimes described as key 'open' infrastructure organizations?
option_a: Because all three organizations are directly owned and operated by national governments
option_b: Because each organization sells exclusive access to its underlying data for profit
option_c: Because they are community-governed nonprofits many systems depend on
option_d: Because their primary function is to produce formal rankings of academic journals
option_e: Because each of the three organizations only serves researchers in a single country
explanation_correct: Crossref (publication DOIs/metadata), DataCite (data DOIs/metadata), and ORCID (researcher IDs) are member-governed nonprofits whose open identifiers and metadata underpin much of the scholarly ecosystem, exemplifying open scholarly infrastructure.
explanation_incorrect: They are community-governed nonprofits with open outputs, not government-owned, exclusive-data vendors, ranking bodies, or single-country services.

question: What does a 'dark archive' (e.g., CLOCKSS) mean?
option_a: An unofficial website that hosts pirated copies of copyrighted journal articles
option_b: An archive that specifically collects and stores books that have been formally banned
option_c: An archive released to the public only if the source becomes unavailable
option_d: An archive that preserves raw files but stores absolutely no descriptive metadata
option_e: A secret database maintained and operated exclusively by a national government
explanation_correct: A dark archive stores content securely without providing routine access; it makes material openly available only when a 'trigger event' (like a publisher ceasing operations) permanently removes the original, safeguarding continuity of access.
explanation_incorrect: It is a preservation mechanism with triggered release, not banned books, a secret database, a metadata-free store, or a pirate site.

question: What is Crossref's 'Similarity Check' service used for?
option_a: A tool used specifically to detect and flag duplicate DOI registrations
option_b: Helping publishers screen manuscripts for plagiarism using iThenticate
option_c: A tool used specifically for comparing journals' official impact factors
option_d: A tool used specifically to match submitted manuscripts with suitable reviewers
option_e: A tool used specifically to check for similarity between different authors' names
explanation_correct: Similarity Check gives Crossref member publishers access to iThenticate to compare submissions against a large database of published content, supporting plagiarism screening during editorial evaluation.
explanation_incorrect: It screens text similarity for plagiarism, not author names, metrics, reviewer matching, or DOI duplication.

question: What is OpenDOAR?
option_a: A curated global directory of open-access repositories and their policies
option_b: A platform that coordinates and manages the formal peer-review process
option_c: A registration agency responsible for issuing new DOIs to publishers
option_d: A formal technical standard governing the manufacturing of doors
option_e: A database that tracks funding awards made by research grant agencies
explanation_correct: OpenDOAR is an authoritative, curated directory of open-access repositories worldwide, listing their content types, policies, and features to help users and services find and assess repositories.
explanation_incorrect: OpenDOAR is a repository directory, not a DOI agency, review platform, or funding database.
review_note: Verify OpenDOAR URL (now under Jisc/Open Policy Finder ecosystem).

question: What is the role of the ISSN International Centre?
option_a: To handle the technical process of assigning DOIs to journal articles
option_b: To provide long-term hosting and storage for research datasets
option_c: To produce an annual ranking of journals by their overall prestige
option_d: To coordinate the ISSN network and assign ISSNs to serials
option_e: To handle the technical process of registering new ORCID iDs
explanation_correct: The ISSN International Centre, with a network of national centres, manages the assignment and registry of ISSNs - the standard identifiers for serial publications - ensuring serials can be reliably identified worldwide.
explanation_incorrect: It manages ISSNs for serials, not DOIs, rankings, datasets, or researcher IDs.

question: What does Crossref's 'Crossmark' feature provide to readers?
option_a: A numerical score indicating the likelihood that the article was plagiarized
option_b: The official journal impact factor of the publication in question
option_c: The corresponding author's registered ORCID iD, displayed for readers
option_d: A running total of how many times the article has been formally cited
option_e: A logo showing whether a document is current or has been updated
explanation_correct: Crossmark gives readers a way to check a document's status - confirming it is up to date and revealing publisher updates like corrections or retractions - helping ensure they are relying on the current, trustworthy version.
explanation_incorrect: Crossmark surfaces update/status information, not plagiarism scores, metrics, citation counts, or identifiers.

question: What is SCOSS (the Global Sustainability Coalition for Open Science Services)?
option_a: A single, centralized repository that hosts open-science content directly
option_b: A specific citation metric used to measure a service's overall impact
option_c: A commercial funder that provides grants to for-profit publishing companies
option_d: An initiative that coordinates community funding for open infrastructure
option_e: A ranking system that compares different open-science services to each other
explanation_correct: SCOSS runs a coordinated process to select vital open infrastructures (e.g., DOAJ, SHERPA, OpenCitations) and mobilize collective financial support from the community, addressing the sustainability of shared open services.
explanation_incorrect: SCOSS coordinates sustainable funding for open infrastructure, not rankings, a single repository, or metrics, and it is a community initiative, not a commercial funder.

question: What is the 'Keepers Registry' used to check?
option_a: Which archiving agencies happen to charge a fee for their preservation services
option_b: Which academic journals currently have the highest official impact factor
option_c: Which individual authors keep their personal ORCID records fully updated
option_d: Which archiving agencies are preserving which continuing (serial) resources
option_e: Which specific DOIs have mistakenly been registered more than once
explanation_correct: The Keepers Registry monitors the archiving activity of participating preservation services, showing which serials/e-journals are being 'kept' and helping the community spot content at risk of loss - supporting collective preservation.
explanation_incorrect: It tracks preservation coverage of serials, not impact, author behavior, fees, or DOI duplication.
review_note: Verify Keepers Registry URL (hosted by ISSN International Centre).

question: How does DSpace or Fedora relate to institutional repositories?
option_a: They are commercial systems used specifically to rank institutional repositories
option_b: They are commercial databases used specifically to track scholarly citations
option_c: They are preprint servers used specifically for posting early manuscripts
option_d: They are government funding agencies that support repository development
option_e: Open-source software platforms used to build institutional repositories
explanation_correct: DSpace and Fedora (stewarded within the Lyrasis/DuraSpace community) are widely used open-source platforms on which institutions build repositories to store, manage, and provide open access to their outputs.
explanation_incorrect: They are repository software, not rankings, citation databases, funders, or preprint servers.

question: What distinguishes OpenAlex from proprietary databases like Scopus and Web of Science in the scholarly-information landscape?
option_a: OpenAlex is, in fact, considerably more expensive to access than either alternative
option_b: OpenAlex is a resource that only ever covers research from a single discipline
option_c: OpenAlex is a resource that is directly owned and operated by a national government
option_d: OpenAlex is an index that does not actually include any citation data at all
option_e: OpenAlex provides an openly licensed (CC0), free index of works, authors, institutions, sources, and more with an open API
explanation_correct: OpenAlex (by the nonprofit OurResearch) offers a comprehensive, openly licensed, freely accessible index with an open API, contrasting with the closed, subscription-based Scopus and Web of Science - central to the open-research-information movement.
explanation_incorrect: OpenAlex is free and open, multidisciplinary, includes citations, and is nonprofit- (not government-) run.

question: What is the significance of Crossref acquiring and openly releasing the Retraction Watch database (2023) for scholarly infrastructure?
option_a: It integrated a comprehensive, curated retraction dataset into open infrastructure (the Crossref API)
option_b: It made the underlying retraction data considerably more proprietary and restricted
option_c: It resulted in a formal ranking of journals based on their retraction counts
option_d: It led to every previously retracted paper being permanently deleted from record
option_e: It effectively replaced the need for any form of formal peer review
explanation_correct: By acquiring the Retraction Watch database and making it openly available through its API, Crossref embedded a key integrity dataset into open scholarly infrastructure, enabling widespread, free reuse in discovery and integrity tools.
explanation_incorrect: The move opened (not closed) the data, and did not rank journals, delete papers, or replace review.

question: What role does Wikidata increasingly play in open scholarly infrastructure?
option_a: It is expected to eventually replace academic journals as the primary venue for publishing
option_b: It provides an open, linked knowledge base connecting scholarly entities
option_c: It primarily functions as a service that produces formal rankings of universities
option_d: Its main role is to handle the technical process of assigning new DOIs
option_e: Its main role is to host the complete full text of published articles
explanation_correct: Wikidata, a free and open knowledge graph, stores structured, linked data about scholarly entities and their identifiers, enabling open, interoperable connections across the research information ecosystem (e.g., via projects like WikiCite/Scholia).
explanation_incorrect: Wikidata is an open structured knowledge base, not a journal replacement, ranking, DOI agency, or full-text host.

question: Why is repository interoperability (e.g., shared metadata formats, OAI-PMH, and COAR Notify) important?
option_a: It mainly serves to increase the total physical storage capacity that repositories require
option_b: It is mainly a mechanism used to calculate and assign official impact factor scores
option_c: It is mainly used to produce a formal ranking that compares different repositories
option_d: It lets repositories, aggregators, and services exchange metadata and notifications consistently
option_e: It works primarily by encrypting the content stored within participating repositories
explanation_correct: Common protocols and vocabularies (e.g., OAI-PMH for harvesting, COAR Notify for linked notifications) allow distributed repositories to be aggregated, searched, and connected to external services, creating a functioning network rather than isolated silos.
explanation_incorrect: Interoperability enables exchange and discovery, not size, ranking, metrics, or encryption.

question: What is the difference in preservation approach between LOCKSS and a 'trigger'-based archive like Portico?
option_a: LOCKSS distributes many copies across libraries; Portico is centralized
option_b: There is, in practice, no meaningful difference at all between the two approaches
option_c: LOCKSS functions mainly as a service that produces formal rankings of journals
option_d: Portico, unlike LOCKSS, only ever preserves research datasets and nothing else
option_e: Both LOCKSS and Portico, in fact, explicitly refuse to preserve any journal content
explanation_correct: LOCKSS uses a decentralized, peer-to-peer model where member libraries each hold and audit copies; Portico operates a centralized preservation archive that releases content upon defined trigger events - two complementary models of e-journal preservation.
explanation_incorrect: The approaches differ (distributed vs centralized-trigger); neither ranks journals, Portico is not dataset-only, and both do preserve journals.

question: What does the Directory of Open Access Repositories ecosystem (OpenDOAR, re3data) collectively help users and services do?
option_a: It primarily helps produce formal rankings that compare individual researchers
option_b: It primarily handles the technical process of assigning new DOIs to outputs
option_c: It primarily functions as a tool for detecting plagiarism in submitted work
option_d: Find and compare repositories - OpenDOAR for publications, re3data for data
option_e: It primarily functions as a service for calculating official impact factor scores
explanation_correct: OpenDOAR (publications) and re3data (data) are complementary registries that let researchers, funders, and systems locate and evaluate appropriate repositories based on discipline, policies, certifications, and capabilities.
explanation_incorrect: These registries support repository discovery/assessment, not researcher ranking, DOI assignment, plagiarism detection, or metrics.

question: Why is the sustainability of open scholarly infrastructure (addressed by SCOSS, IOI, and POSI) considered a systemic concern?
option_a: Because these essential open infrastructures typically charge unusually high fees to users
option_b: Because operating this kind of open infrastructure is, in practice, always highly profitable
option_c: Because many essential open services rely on precarious grant or volunteer funding
option_d: Because these infrastructures are, in every case, securely funded directly by governments
option_e: Because sustaining this kind of shared infrastructure is not actually considered important
explanation_correct: Open, community-serving infrastructures often operate on thin, uncertain funding while providing services the whole system depends on; initiatives like SCOSS (coordinated funding), IOI (research/advocacy), and POSI (governance principles) exist precisely because their sustainability and independence are at systemic risk.
explanation_incorrect: The concern arises because these services are not reliably profitable, government-secured, or high-fee; their fragility makes coordinated support necessary.

question: How does the POSI 'insurance' principle of open source code and available data mitigate the risk of infrastructure lock-in?
option_a: By converting the entire service into a closed, proprietary commercial product
option_b: The community can migrate the service elsewhere using the open code and data
option_c: By actively preventing other developers from forking or reusing the codebase
option_d: By imposing significant financial exit fees on organizations that wish to leave
option_e: By permanently deleting the underlying data once the organization winds down
explanation_correct: POSI's insurance principles (open source software plus data deposited so it can be recovered) mean the community is not trapped if an organization collapses or is acquired: the service can be rebuilt or migrated, reducing lock-in and single points of failure.
explanation_incorrect: The principle enables continuity and forking, the opposite of proprietary lock-in, data deletion, or exit fees.

question: Why do Crossref's grant identifiers and funder registry matter for the broader research-information graph?
option_a: They exist mainly to produce a formal ranking that compares different funders
option_b: They let grants and funders be identified with persistent identifiers and linked to outputs
option_c: They exist mainly to determine the specific article processing charges journals bill
option_d: They exist mainly to entirely replace the traditional peer-review process
option_e: They exist mainly to anonymize the identities of a paper's listed authors
explanation_correct: By assigning identifiers to grants and standardizing funder identification, Crossref enables outputs to be linked back to specific awards and funders in machine-readable ways, strengthening the connected metadata graph used for reporting, compliance, and analysis.
explanation_incorrect: Grant/funder IDs enable linking and tracking, not APC-setting, funder ranking, review replacement, or anonymization.
review_note: Verify Crossref Grant Linking System URL.

question: What tension does the reliance on commercial platforms (e.g., for citation data, repositories, or workflow tools) create for the open-research movement?
option_a: Open, community-governed alternatives to these commercial platforms essentially never exist
option_b: Dependence on proprietary services can create lock-in and access risks
option_c: Relying on these commercial platforms is, in every case, actually the cheaper long-term option
option_d: There is, in practice, no meaningful tension created by this kind of commercial dependence
option_e: These commercial platforms are, in fact, already governed collectively by their user community
explanation_correct: When core functions run on closed commercial platforms, the community faces lock-in, non-transparent algorithms/coverage, and pricing or access risks that sit uneasily with open-science aims - a key reason movements support open, community-governed infrastructure and open research information.
explanation_incorrect: The tension is real; commercial options are not inherently cheaper, open alternatives do exist, and commercial platforms are not community-governed.

question: How does the 'Metadata 2020' style push for richer, open metadata connect to the value of scholarly infrastructure?
option_a: This kind of push works mainly by making metadata records deliberately secret
option_b: This kind of push is designed in a way that ends up benefiting only one single publisher
option_c: This kind of push actually works to reduce the overall need for persistent identifiers
option_d: This kind of push functions mainly to produce a formal ranking of metadata quality
option_e: Better, more open metadata increases discoverability across the ecosystem
explanation_correct: Collaborative efforts to improve and open metadata recognize that the connective power of infrastructure - discovery, linking outputs to people/funders, analytics - depends on rich, accurate, shareable metadata; poor metadata limits what any service can do.
explanation_incorrect: Richer open metadata amplifies infrastructure value broadly; it does not make metadata secret, remove identifiers, rank metadata, or benefit only one party.
review_note: Metadata2020 concluded; frame as the general principle and verify link.

question: Why is preservation infrastructure (dark archives, distributed preservation, the Keepers Registry) essential even when content is 'openly available' online?
option_a: Because content that is openly available online is understood to essentially never disappear
option_b: Because open availability today does not guarantee long-term survival
option_c: Because preservation infrastructure of this kind mainly functions to rank competing journals
option_d: Because openly available content is assumed to be automatically archived everywhere by default
option_e: Because formal preservation infrastructure is considered relevant only for physical paper records
explanation_correct: Being online and open now is not the same as being preserved; without deliberate, monitored preservation and coverage tracking, openly available works can still be lost when hosts fail, which is why preservation infrastructure underpins the durable record.
explanation_incorrect: Open content can and does disappear, is not automatically archived everywhere, and preservation applies to digital content, not just paper, and is unrelated to ranking.

question: What is the significance of open-source publishing platforms (OJS, Janeway, Open Journal Systems) for global equity in scholarly publishing?
option_a: Their main significance lies in how they help produce formal rankings of competing journals
