option_d: It formally bans grantees from posting their work as preprints
option_e: It imposes no licensing requirements of any kind on funded authors
explanation_correct: Wellcome's 2021 policy aligns with Plan S: it requires immediate open access under a CC BY licence and lets authors comply via rights retention on their accepted manuscript (or by posting a CC BY preprint), with no embargoes.
explanation_incorrect: Wellcome requires immediate OA (no long embargoes), encourages preprints, and mandates open licensing.

question: What was the key change introduced by the 2022 US OSTP 'Nelson memo' compared with the earlier 2013 policy?
option_a: It reintroduced paywalls that had previously been removed by earlier policy
option_b: It limited public access to research exclusively to government staff
option_c: It brought an end to public access requirements altogether
option_d: It removed the embargo and extended requirements to all agencies
option_e: It applied its requirements only to federal agencies with small budgets
explanation_correct: The 2013 (Holdren) memo allowed a 12-month embargo and applied to larger agencies; the 2022 Nelson memo eliminated the embargo (requiring immediate free access) and extended the requirement to all federal agencies that fund research.
explanation_incorrect: The Nelson memo broadened and strengthened access (no embargo, all agencies), rather than reintroducing paywalls, limiting to staff, or ending access.

question: What is the general purpose of a funder open access or public access mandate?
option_a: To produce a formal ranking that compares researchers funded by the same agency
option_b: To handle the technical process of assigning DOIs to funded outputs
option_c: To deliberately restrict who is legally permitted to read the research
option_d: To require that funded research be made freely available to the public
option_e: To substantially increase the profit margins earned by commercial publishers
explanation_correct: Funder OA mandates aim to ensure that publicly or philanthropically funded research is openly accessible, so taxpayers and the public benefit from and can build on the research they financed.
explanation_incorrect: Mandates aim to open access, not increase profits, restrict readership, rank researchers, or assign identifiers.

question: Under Horizon Europe, what is required regarding research data from funded projects?
option_a: Projects must have a DMP and make data open as possible, closed as necessary
option_b: All research data generated must formally be sold to interested third parties
option_c: All research data generated under the project must be kept entirely secret
option_d: Sharing of research data under the programme is entirely optional and unregulated
option_e: Only data coming from projects that ultimately failed must be shared
explanation_correct: Horizon Europe mandates responsible research data management: a Data Management Plan, adherence to FAIR principles, and depositing data in a trusted repository with open access provided as soon as possible under the 'as open as possible, as closed as necessary' principle.
explanation_incorrect: Data must be managed and shared (not kept secret, sold, or left unregulated), balancing openness with legitimate constraints.

question: Why do many funders now require or encourage the use of persistent identifiers (ORCID, DOIs, ROR) in funded outputs?
option_a: To make the resulting research outputs considerably harder to find
option_b: To artificially increase the total word count of published articles
option_c: To formally replace the traditional process of peer review
option_d: To deliberately place new restrictions on how research data can be shared
option_e: To enable reliable tracking of outputs back to grants and institutions
explanation_correct: Requiring PIDs lets funders and systems connect outputs to specific awards, researchers, and organizations in machine-readable ways, supporting accurate attribution, impact reporting, and monitoring of policy compliance.
explanation_incorrect: PIDs improve findability and linking, not obscurity, length, restriction, or review replacement.

question: What does 'immediate open access' (zero embargo) mean in funder policies?
option_a: Access to the publication is only granted after a period of several months
option_b: The publication is freely available from the moment it is published
option_c: Access to the publication expires again shortly after it is granted
option_d: Access to the publication is limited exclusively to the funder itself
option_e: Access under this policy is limited only to the underlying raw data
explanation_correct: Zero-embargo/immediate open access means there is no waiting period: the research publication is openly and freely available to all readers as soon as it is published, a requirement now common to Plan S, the Nelson memo, NIH 2024, Horizon Europe, and others.
explanation_incorrect: Immediate OA is available to everyone at publication with no delay, not restricted, delayed, expiring, or data-only.

question: How can a researcher typically comply with an immediate-OA funder policy without paying an APC?
option_a: There is, in practice, simply no available way to achieve this at all
option_b: By choosing to keep the resulting manuscript entirely closed to readers
option_c: By waiting patiently until the publisher's embargo period comes to an end
option_d: By publishing exclusively in the very most expensive journals available
option_e: By using the green/repository route with rights retention
explanation_correct: Rights retention combined with repository deposit lets authors satisfy immediate-OA policies at no APC: they retain the right to apply a CC BY licence to the accepted manuscript and deposit it openly on publication, regardless of the journal's model.
explanation_incorrect: Compliance without an APC is possible via green/rights retention; it does not require costly APCs, keeping work closed, or waiting out embargoes.

question: Why did NIH accelerate its 2024 Public Access Policy to 1 July 2025 (from an original end-of-2025 date)?
option_a: To formally reintroduce embargoes that had previously been eliminated
option_b: To deliberately delay the point at which research becomes openly available
option_c: To speed up public access by bringing the zero-embargo requirement in sooner
option_d: To specifically exempt clinical research from the policy's requirements
option_e: To limit public access to research exclusively to NIH's own staff
explanation_correct: NIH moved the effective date earlier (announced in NOT-OD-25-101) to accelerate free public access to the results of NIH-funded research, implementing the zero-embargo deposit requirement sooner than initially planned.
explanation_incorrect: Acceleration hastened (not delayed) access, kept the zero-embargo rule, and did not exempt research or restrict access to staff.

question: Under Horizon Europe, what is the funding stance on hybrid journals?
option_a: Article processing charges for hybrid journals are fully funded by the programme
option_b: Publishing in hybrid journals is formally and outright banned under the programme
option_c: Hybrid publishing is permitted, but its APC costs are not eligible for reimbursement
option_d: Researchers funded under the programme may only ever publish in hybrid journals
option_e: Publishing through the hybrid route automatically satisfies the policy's requirements
explanation_correct: Horizon Europe does not reimburse OA fees in hybrid journals (those mixing paywalled and paid-OA content); researchers may still publish there but must meet the immediate-OA requirement another way (e.g., repository deposit), and the hybrid APC is not an eligible cost.
explanation_incorrect: Hybrid APCs are not funded (nor fully banned), hybrid is not required or automatically compliant.

question: What licence does Horizon Europe require for open access journal articles, and what flexibility exists for long-form works?
option_a: A CC0 public domain dedication is required for every single type of output
option_b: Outputs are published under a standard 'all rights reserved' arrangement
option_c: No formal license of any kind is required for any published output
option_d: CC BY for articles, with CC BY-NC or CC BY-ND for long-form works
option_e: A CC BY-NC licence is required uniformly across every kind of output
explanation_correct: Horizon Europe mandates CC BY for peer-reviewed journal articles, while allowing more restrictive open licences (CC BY-NC or CC BY-ND) for monographs and other long-form outputs, recognizing their different reuse considerations.
explanation_incorrect: It is not CC0 for everything, all-rights-reserved, unlicensed, or CC BY-NC across the board; CC BY is required for articles with limited flexibility for books.

question: How does the Gates Foundation's 2025 'preprint-first' approach aim to achieve open access differently from an APC model?
option_a: By requiring manuscripts be shared as preprints instead of paying for OA
option_b: By substantially increasing the level of APCs the foundation is willing to pay
option_c: By deliberately keeping the resulting research closed rather than open
option_d: By restricting its funding to journals that operate on a subscription basis
option_e: By formally mandating that grantees publish through the hybrid route
explanation_correct: Instead of funding APCs to open the journal version, Gates requires grantees to make their manuscripts openly available as preprints, delivering immediate free access without pay-to-publish charges and reducing reliance on APC-based OA.
explanation_incorrect: The approach avoids APCs (not raises them), keeps research open (not closed), and does not fund subscription-only or mandate hybrid publishing.

question: What distinguishes the cOAlition S 'Towards Responsible Publishing' (TRP) proposal from the original Plan S requirements?
option_a: TRP, in effect, works to abolish open access as a policy goal entirely
option_b: TRP is centered mainly on mandating considerably higher article processing charges
option_c: TRP broadens the vision toward authors retaining rights and sharing the full range of outputs
option_d: TRP explicitly endorses continued publishing through subscription-based journals
option_e: TRP works, in effect, by removing every existing funder policy altogether
explanation_correct: Where Plan S focuses on immediate OA to the published article, TRP (2023-2024) envisions a scholar-led system in which researchers retain rights and share the cumulative outputs of research - preprints, data, peer reviews - shifting emphasis from the accepted version to the whole research process.
explanation_incorrect: TRP expands openness (not abolishes it), does not mandate APCs, endorse subscriptions, or remove funder policy.

question: Beyond free access to publications, what does the 2022 Nelson memo emphasize about underlying data and identifiers?
option_a: It works mainly by substantially lengthening the embargoes applied to shared data
option_b: It works mainly by formally banning the use of persistent identifiers
option_c: It works mainly by placing new restrictions on access to research data
option_d: It also directs agencies to enable immediate access to underlying data
option_e: It focuses exclusively on publications and says nothing at all about data
explanation_correct: The Nelson memo goes beyond publications, instructing agencies to make the scientific data underlying peer-reviewed publications freely available (with appropriate protections) and to promote persistent identifiers for people, awards, and outputs to improve transparency and tracking.
explanation_incorrect: The memo expands data access and encourages PIDs, rather than ignoring data, restricting it, extending embargoes, or banning identifiers.

question: What is a data management plan (DMP) requirement in funder policies intended to ensure?
option_a: That the underlying research data is, as a matter of policy, never shared
option_b: That the underlying research data is formally sold directly to publishers
option_c: That only the study's positive, favorable results are ultimately retained
option_d: That researchers plan up front how data will be documented and shared
option_e: That the resulting DMP formally takes the place of ethics review
explanation_correct: Funder DMP requirements (e.g., NIH DMS, Horizon Europe, UKRI) make researchers specify data handling and sharing plans at the outset, promoting FAIR, responsible management and appropriate openness of research data throughout and after the project.
explanation_incorrect: DMPs aim to enable responsible sharing/management, not prevent sharing, sell data, discard results, or replace ethics review.
review_note: Verify a DMP-specific UKRI/NIH URL if desired; concept is standard.

question: How does rights retention help funders enforce immediate open access despite publisher embargoes?
option_a: By requiring a prior CC BY licence on accepted manuscripts as a funding condition
option_b: By substantially lengthening the embargo period a publisher can impose
option_c: By formally transferring the manuscript's copyright over to the publisher
option_d: By placing an outright ban on the use of institutional repositories
option_e: By directly paying publishers a fee in exchange for waiving their embargoes
explanation_correct: Funders like cOAlition S members and Wellcome require, as a grant condition, that authors retain rights and apply a CC BY licence to the accepted manuscript before submission; this prior licence lets them deposit openly on publication, overriding publisher embargoes without extra payment.
explanation_incorrect: Rights retention uses a prior funder-imposed licence, not payment, longer embargoes, repository bans, or copyright transfer to publishers.

question: Why are Wellcome and other funders requiring or accepting preprints as part of open access compliance significant?
option_a: Because preprints of this kind remain formally banned in most other contexts
option_b: Because this practice, in effect, forbids authors from journal publication
option_c: Because preprints, under this arrangement, formally replace ethics review
option_d: Because it legitimizes preprints as a compliant route to open dissemination
option_e: Because preprints accepted this way are, in fact, considered closed access
explanation_correct: By accepting a CC BY preprint (e.g., indexed in Europe PMC) as a compliant route, funders formally recognize preprints as a legitimate means of achieving immediate open access, reinforcing preprinting's role in the scholarly communication system.
explanation_incorrect: Preprints are recognized as a compliant open route, not banned, closed, a replacement for ethics review, or a bar to journal publication.

question: What is the significance of cOAlition S ending financial support for 'transformative arrangements' after 2024?
option_a: It has the effect of reintroducing embargoes that had previously been removed
option_b: It effectively signals that the entire Plan S initiative has been abandoned
option_c: It signals the end of transitional funding, moving toward 'pure publish' models
option_d: It results in hybrid journals being funded by cOAlition S on a permanent basis
option_e: It amounts to an outright ban on open access publishing altogether
explanation_correct: cOAlition S designed transformative arrangements as time-limited bridges; ending support after 2024 marks the close of that transitional phase, pushing toward arrangements without subsidizing hybrid subscription models - part of Plan S's ongoing implementation, not its abandonment.
explanation_incorrect: The move ends transitional subsidies (it does not abandon Plan S, permanently fund hybrids, reintroduce embargoes, or ban OA).
review_note: Verify current cOAlition S framing given the 2026-2030 strategy.

question: What is one reason funder open access policies increasingly converge on CC BY as the required licence?
option_a: Because a CC BY licence, in fact, forbids most forms of reuse
option_b: Because using a CC BY licence has the effect of hiding the underlying work
option_c: Because a CC BY licence is understood to prevent any commercial use
option_d: Because CC BY happens to be the only licence that is legally recognized
option_e: Because CC BY maximizes reuse while still ensuring attribution
explanation_correct: Funders such as cOAlition S members, Wellcome, UKRI, and Horizon Europe converge on CC BY because it removes permission barriers to reuse (while requiring credit), letting funded research be maximally used, mined, and built upon.
explanation_incorrect: CC BY enables (not forbids) reuse and commercial use, is not the only legal licence, and makes work more, not less, open.

question: How do the NIH 2024 Public Access Policy and Plan S/Horizon Europe compare on embargoes?
option_a: All of them require immediate, zero-embargo public access
option_b: NIH permits embargoes while Plan S and Horizon Europe do not allow any
option_c: Only Plan S, among the three, actually requires immediate open access
option_d: All three of these policies permit a standard 12-month embargo period
option_e: None of these three particular policies actually addresses embargoes at all
explanation_correct: A shared feature across NIH 2024, Plan S, and Horizon Europe is the elimination of embargoes: each requires the funded peer-reviewed publication to be made freely available immediately upon publication.
explanation_incorrect: All three require zero embargo; none uniquely allows embargoes, and the requirement is not limited to Plan S.

question: As of 2025-2026, what has been happening to the US OSTP Nelson memo, and why does this create uncertainty?
option_a: The memo was, in fact, fully repealed and formally replaced back in 2023
option_b: The administration has been repealing the Nelson memo, creating uncertainty
option_c: The memo was made permanently binding through a formal international treaty
option_d: The memo's requirements were subsequently expanded to apply to every country
option_e: The Nelson memo, in point of fact, never actually existed as a real policy
explanation_correct: Reporting indicates the OSTP has been moving to repeal the Nelson memo, with Congress asking for status updates; because agencies had been implementing zero-embargo policies (targeted for around end of 2025), the potential repeal leaves the future of US federal public access policy uncertain.
explanation_incorrect: The memo was not simply repealed-and-replaced in 2023, made a treaty, globalized, or nonexistent; the situation is an in-progress, uncertain repeal.
review_note: Volatile: re-verify the Nelson memo's status before publishing, as it may change with administration guidance.

question: Why is the Gates Foundation's decision to stop paying APCs and require preprints seen as a notable signal in OA policy debates?
option_a: Because this decision effectively mandates that grantees publish in subscription journals
option_b: Because this decision, somewhat paradoxically, increases the foundation's reliance on APCs
option_c: Because a major funder concluded that paying APCs was costly and ineffective
option_d: Because this decision is widely understood to represent an abandonment of open access
option_e: Because this decision is understood to formally ban the sharing of underlying data
explanation_correct: Gates spent millions on APCs yet judged the model expensive and insufficient; pivoting to preprint-first, no-APC compliance signaled a major funder moving away from pay-to-publish OA - a stance aligned with the broader shift (e.g., TRP) toward author-retained rights and preprint sharing.
explanation_incorrect: The decision reduces (not increases) APC reliance, strengthens OA rather than abandoning it, and does not mandate subscriptions or ban data sharing.

question: What implementation challenge do zero-embargo green-OA policies (e.g., via rights retention) create with publishers, and how is it navigated?
option_a: In practice, publishers always agree readily to this kind of arrangement with no friction
option_b: In practice, funders simply pay publishers directly whenever compliance is required
option_c: In practice, the repositories involved end up being shut down entirely
option_d: Some publishers contest or add terms against immediate AAM sharing
option_e: In practice, authors are typically forced to abandon publishing altogether
explanation_correct: Publishers may resist immediate accepted-manuscript sharing; the rights-retention mechanism counters this by having the funder require a CC BY licence before any publisher contract exists, so the author's prior obligation to the funder legally precedes and overrides conflicting publisher terms.
explanation_incorrect: The tension is real and navigated via prior licensing/precedence, not universal publisher agreement, payment, abandoning publishing, or closing repositories.

question: How do funder mandates interact with research-assessment reform (DORA/CoARA) to reshape incentives?
option_a: Funder mandates and research-assessment reform are, in practice, entirely unrelated to each other
option_b: Both of these efforts, in practice, rely primarily on the Journal Impact Factor
option_c: Open access and data-sharing mandates change what researchers must do
option_d: Funder mandates and assessment reform, in practice, directly contradict one another
option_e: Both of these efforts, somewhat paradoxically, actively discourage open access
explanation_correct: Mandates set requirements (open access, FAIR data, PIDs) but can feel like compliance burdens unless evaluation also rewards these practices; pairing mandates with assessment reform (valuing diverse open outputs over journal prestige) aligns obligations with incentives to make open research the norm.
explanation_incorrect: The two are complementary, not unrelated or contradictory; both move away from JIF reliance and toward, not against, open access.

question: Why do equity advocates caution that APC-funding mandates can disadvantage some researchers, and how are funders responding?
option_a: Because funder-paid APCs can exclude unfunded or lower-income researchers
option_b: Funders are responding to this concern mainly by raising APC amounts even further
option_c: In practice, APCs are understood to benefit every researcher completely equally
option_d: Funders are responding to this concern by ending open access requirements altogether
option_e: Funders are responding to this concern mainly by mandating subscription publishing
explanation_correct: When compliance leans on paying APCs, well-funded authors are served while others are shut out, worsening global inequities; in response, funders increasingly emphasize APC-free routes - repository deposit with rights retention, preprints, and support for diamond OA - as Gates and others illustrate.
explanation_incorrect: APCs do not help everyone equally; the response is to reduce APC dependence, not raise APCs, end OA, ban repositories, or mandate subscriptions.

question: What is the significance of major funders (e.g., NIH, Horizon Europe, UKRI, Wellcome) converging on immediate, zero-embargo open access?
option_a: This kind of convergence has the effect of weakening existing open access requirements
option_b: This kind of convergence, in practice, applies only within a single academic discipline
option_c: This kind of convergence has the effect of reintroducing paywalls across the system
option_d: This kind of convergence tends to fragment open access policy across different funders
option_e: It creates a global baseline that immediate open access is expected for funded research
explanation_correct: When the largest public and philanthropic funders independently require immediate, embargo-free open access, they collectively establish a global norm that shifts publisher practices and author expectations toward zero-embargo access as the default for funded research.
explanation_incorrect: Convergence strengthens and aligns (not fragments or weakens) OA, spans disciplines, and does not restore paywalls.

question: Why does the potential repeal of the US Nelson memo illustrate the fragility of policy-driven open access, and what provides more durable alternatives?
option_a: Because access requirements enacted by executive or administrative action can be reversed by later administrations
option_b: Genuine durability in this context is understood to come mainly from imposing longer embargoes
option_c: A formal repeal of this kind would, in fact, make open access permanent rather than fragile
option_d: Only commercial publishers are understood to be capable of guaranteeing lasting open access
option_e: Open access that rests solely on administrative policy is, in fact, considered inherently permanent
explanation_correct: OA achieved through administrative policy is vulnerable to political reversal, as the Nelson memo situation shows; more resilient routes rely on legislation, author-vested rights (rights retention), community-governed infrastructure, and institutional mandates that do not hinge on one administration's stance.
explanation_incorrect: Policy-based OA is not permanent and can be reversed; durability comes from law, author rights, and community infrastructure, not publishers or embargoes.
review_note: Volatile: re-verify Nelson memo status before publishing.

question: How does cOAlition S's shift toward 'responsible publishing' reflect a critique of the outcomes of Plan S's first phase?
option_a: It suggests that Plan S, in its first phase, was considered a complete and total success needing no further change
option_b: It reflects concern that flipping to OA via APCs entrenched incumbent publishers
option_c: This particular shift is understood to represent an outright abandonment of open access altogether
option_d: This particular shift is understood to endorse permanently funding hybrid journals going forward
option_e: This particular shift is understood to remove every existing open licensing requirement entirely
explanation_correct: TRP embodies a self-critique: Plan S's article-and-APC focus risked preserving publisher dominance and high costs, so cOAlition S pivots toward a scholar-led vision emphasizing rights retention and sharing the full research process, aiming for deeper structural change than the first phase achieved.
explanation_incorrect: TRP is a course-correction, not a claim of full success, an abandonment of OA, an endorsement of permanent hybrid funding, or removal of licensing.

question: Why can the coexistence of many different national and funder OA policies create both progress and complexity for researchers?
option_a: Because, in practice, every one of these national and funder policies is essentially identical
option_b: Because these various open access policies, in practice, never actually differ from one another
option_c: Differing terms across funders can burden researchers who must satisfy several policies
option_d: Because the resulting complexity is generally taken as evidence that open access itself is failing
option_e: Because, in practice, only a single funder anywhere maintains an open access policy
explanation_correct: The proliferation of OA mandates drives systemic progress toward openness, yet their variation (licences, accepted routes, data rules, monitoring) imposes real compliance complexity, especially for researchers funded by multiple agencies or working across countries - motivating alignment efforts.
explanation_incorrect: Policies are not identical; the tension is between accelerating progress and added compliance complexity, not proof that OA is failing or that only one funder acts.

question: How does the movement of funder policy from 'publications' toward 'the full research lifecycle' (data, code, preprints, PIDs, DMPs) reflect a broader conception of open science?
option_a: This particular shift in policy has the effect of removing every existing requirement altogether
option_b: It treats openness as spanning data, software, methods, and infrastructure, not just articles
option_c: This particular shift is understood to abandon open access to publications entirely
option_d: This particular shift in policy is understood to apply only narrowly to preprints
option_e: This particular shift is understood to narrow open science down to journal articles alone
explanation_correct: Funder requirements increasingly cover data sharing, software, preprints, DMPs, and persistent identifiers - not just the final article - mirroring the broad, systemic understanding of open science (as in the UNESCO Recommendation) that spans the entire research lifecycle and its infrastructures.
explanation_incorrect: The shift broadens (not narrows or abandons) open science beyond articles, is not preprint-only, and adds rather than removes requirements.

question: What is a key benefit of embedding ORCID iDs in a manuscript's metadata at publication?
option_a: It artificially raises the impact factor of the journal that published the article
option_b: It noticeably shortens how long the entire peer-review process takes to complete
option_c: It lets the publication be automatically and reliably connected to the correct authors' ORCID records
option_d: It encrypts the full list of listed authors so it cannot be read by outsiders
option_e: It causes the article to automatically be assigned an entirely new, second DOI
explanation_correct: When an author's ORCID iD travels in the publication metadata, systems can push the work to the right ORCID record and disambiguate authors with similar names, giving trustworthy, machine-readable attribution.
explanation_incorrect: Embedding an ORCID iD does not change impact factors, review speed, encryption, or DOI assignment; it solves attribution and name ambiguity.

question: Why might a single research output legitimately carry identifiers from more than one scheme (e.g., a DOI and, for its authors, ORCID iDs, and for its affiliations, ROR IDs)?
option_a: Because the different identifier schemes are competing with each other to become dominant
option_b: Because funders specifically require that outputs carry exactly three separate identifiers
option_c: Because different PID schemes identify different entity types (outputs, people, organizations)
option_d: Because carrying multiple identifiers effectively doubles the output's recorded citation count
option_e: Because at least one of the attached identifiers is always broken or non-functional
explanation_correct: PID schemes are complementary: a DOI names the output, ORCID iDs name the people, and ROR IDs name the organizations; using them together in metadata connects the output to its contributors and affiliations across the PID graph.
explanation_incorrect: The schemes coexist by design (not competition or breakage), and combining them does not inflate citations or reflect a fixed identifier quota.

question: What does it mean that ROR data is released under CC0 and via an open API?
option_a: ROR charges research organizations a fee whenever they request bulk access to its data
option_b: ROR data technically exists but cannot actually be downloaded in bulk by anyone at all
option_c: Anyone can freely access, reuse, and integrate ROR organization identifiers and metadata without permission or fees
option_d: ROR data is restricted and made available only to paying member organizations
option_e: ROR sells exclusive access to its organizational data directly to commercial vendors
explanation_correct: Because ROR provides its data under a CC0 public-domain dedication with an open API and downloadable dumps, any system can incorporate ROR IDs without licensing barriers - a deliberate design choice enabling ecosystem-wide use.
explanation_incorrect: CC0 plus open API means no fees, no membership restriction, and full downloadability - the opposite of restricted or sold data.

question: If a DOI link stops resolving to the right page, whose responsibility is it to fix the underlying target?
option_a: The individual reader who happened to click on the broken link
option_b: No one; once a DOI link breaks, it can never be fixed again
option_c: The DOI Foundation, which automatically fixes every broken link itself
option_d: The search engine that originally indexed the now-broken page
option_e: The organization that registered the DOI (e.g., the publisher or repository)
explanation_correct: DOIs stay reliable only because registrants maintain them: the publisher or repository that registered the DOI is responsible for updating its target location when content moves, which is what makes persistence a stewardship commitment.
explanation_incorrect: Readers, search engines, and the DOI Foundation do not maintain individual targets, and broken DOIs are fixable by the registrant updating the record.

question: Why is the assertion 'a DOI guarantees a document will always be available' technically inaccurate?
option_a: Because DOIs are intentionally designed from the outset to be short-lived, temporary identifiers
option_b: Because a DOI guarantees a persistent, resolvable identifier and up-to-date link only if the registrant maintains it
option_c: Because every DOI is set, by design, to automatically expire after exactly ten years of use
option_d: Because the full text and content of the document is stored directly inside the DOI string itself
option_e: Because DOI links can only ever be read, opened, and accessed by publishers themselves
explanation_correct: A DOI ensures a stable, resolvable name that can be kept pointing to the current location, but preservation of the actual content is a separate concern handled by archiving; a DOI can resolve to a 'tombstone' page if the content is gone.
explanation_incorrect: DOIs are not temporary or expiring, they do not store content, and their value depends on maintenance plus separate preservation, not on the identifier alone guaranteeing availability.

question: Why is choosing a repository that issues a persistent identifier preferable to sharing data via a personal or lab website?
option_a: Personal or lab websites are technically illegal venues for hosting research data
option_b: Personal websites are, in every case, slower to load than dedicated repositories
option_c: Repositories provide PIDs and stable, preserved access, unlike personal websites
option_d: Repositories are structured in a way that deliberately hides the data from public view
option_e: Personal websites are technically incapable of storing any kind of data files at all
explanation_correct: A trustworthy repository assigns a DOI, preserves the data, and offers stable, documented access; personal or lab pages frequently disappear or change, breaking links and offering no preservation guarantee.
explanation_incorrect: Personal websites are legal and can host files, but they lack persistence and preservation, which is the reason repositories are preferred.

question: What is the purpose of a 'data availability statement' pointing to a repository DOI rather than 'available on request'?
option_a: To deliberately hide the underlying data from potential scientific competitors
option_b: Simply to make the resulting published paper appear noticeably longer
option_c: To give readers reliable, persistent access, avoiding on-request failures
option_d: To automatically and artificially increase the article's recorded citation count
option_e: To ensure that the paper formally satisfies applicable national copyright law
explanation_correct: A statement citing a repository DOI lets readers obtain the data directly and durably; studies show on-request access often fails, so a persistent link is more transparent and reproducible.
explanation_incorrect: A DOI-based statement improves access and reproducibility, not paper length, secrecy, automatic citations, or copyright compliance per se.

question: Why is documenting units, variable definitions, and collection methods essential even for openly shared data?
option_a: Because without this context, reusers cannot correctly interpret the data
option_b: It is a strict, formal requirement imposed by repositories in order to obtain a DOI for the dataset
option_c: Adding this kind of extra documentation simply makes the overall dataset's files noticeably larger
option_d: Documenting the data in this thorough way effectively encrypts its contents from unauthorized readers
option_e: This kind of thorough documentation mainly serves to improve the dataset's ranking in search engines
explanation_correct: Data stripped of documentation can be misinterpreted or misused; recording variable meanings, units, and methods provides the context needed for accurate, meaningful reuse, illustrating that access is necessary but not sufficient.
explanation_incorrect: Documentation aids interpretation, not file size, DOI eligibility, encryption, or ranking.

question: What is 'metadata' commonly described as?
option_a: The single largest individual file contained anywhere within a dataset
option_b: 'Data about data' - descriptive information such as title, creator, and date
option_c: A backup copy of the dataset that is kept stored somewhere entirely separate
option_d: The specific piece of software that originally generated the data
option_e: A formal citation that simply points back to the dataset itself
explanation_correct: Metadata is descriptive information about a dataset (who, what, when, how) that supports discovery and understanding; it is distinct from the data values themselves.
explanation_incorrect: Metadata is not the data's largest file, a backup, the creating software, or merely a citation - it is the descriptive layer.

question: How does controlled/managed access reconcile the FAIR principle of accessibility with the protection of sensitive data?
option_a: It works by making every dataset, including the most sensitive kinds, fully open with no restrictions whatsoever
option_b: It works by simply refusing outright to share the sensitive data with anyone at all, under any circumstances
option_c: It works by permanently and immediately deleting all sensitive data so that no privacy risk can ever remain
option_d: By keeping metadata findable while restricting the sensitive data to approved users
option_e: It works by anonymizing the data so thoroughly and repeatedly that it eventually becomes essentially useless
explanation_correct: FAIR's 'A' allows access to be conditional; controlled access publishes discoverable metadata and provides the data through an approval process and data-use agreement, protecting privacy while remaining FAIR.
explanation_incorrect: Controlled access is neither fully open, deletion, blanket refusal, nor destructive anonymization; it is conditional, governed access.
review_note: Verify UK Data Service access-control URL.

question: Why do some open access advocates prefer 'diamond' OA over APC-funded 'gold' OA on equity grounds?
option_a: Diamond OA is a model that still charges ordinary readers to access content
option_b: Diamond OA imposes significant restrictions on how the work can be reused
option_c: Diamond OA journals actually charge higher article processing charges than gold
option_d: Because diamond OA charges neither readers nor authors
option_e: Diamond OA operates on a subscription-based model similar to traditional journals
explanation_correct: Diamond (no-fee) OA avoids both paywalls and author charges, so researchers lacking grants are not excluded from publishing - an equity advantage over APC-based gold OA.
explanation_incorrect: Diamond OA has no APCs and no reader charges, permits open reuse, and is not subscription-based.

question: What does an author gain by depositing an accepted manuscript in a repository even after publishing gold OA?
option_a: Nothing at all; doing this is considered a pointless, redundant exercise
option_b: It results in the removal of the article's official version of record
option_c: It results in a second DOI being minted that replaces the original one
option_d: A preserved, openly accessible copy independent of the publisher's platform
option_e: It results in the article receiving a noticeably higher impact factor
explanation_correct: Repository deposit adds a preserved, harvestable copy that remains accessible via repository aggregators even if the publisher platform changes, complementing the gold-OA version of record.
explanation_incorrect: Deposit is not pointless, does not replace the VoR or its DOI, and does not affect impact factors.

question: What does 'open access' primarily remove for readers?
option_a: The ongoing need to cite the original sources used in scholarly work
option_b: The need to use persistent identifiers when citing scholarly sources
option_c: The requirement that a manuscript undergo formal peer review
option_d: The price barrier to reading and reusing scholarly work
option_e: The original author's underlying legal copyright over the work
explanation_correct: Open access removes the cost barrier so anyone can read the work, and in its libre form also removes permission barriers via open licensing; it does not remove citation, peer review, copyright, or identifiers.
explanation_incorrect: OA is about removing price/permission barriers, not eliminating citation, review, copyright, or PIDs.

question: Why can a hybrid journal lead to concerns about 'double dipping'?
option_a: Because the publisher can collect both subscription income and APCs
option_b: Because a hybrid journal is able to publish new articles twice as fast
option_c: Because authors are required to submit their manuscript to two separate journals
option_d: Because individual readers end up having to pay twice in order to read the article
option_e: Because the journal assigns two separate DOIs to the very same article
explanation_correct: In hybrid journals, libraries still pay subscriptions while authors also pay APCs to open individual articles, raising the concern that publishers are paid twice ('double dipping') for the same content - a key reason Plan S discouraged funding hybrid.
explanation_incorrect: Double dipping refers to dual revenue (subscriptions plus APCs), not publishing speed, dual submission, reader double-payment, or DOIs.

question: Why is the growth of diamond OA seen as important for bibliodiversity in scholarly publishing?
option_a: Because it forces every open access journal to adopt one single standardized publishing format
option_b: Because it concentrates all scholarly publishing activity onto a small number of commercial platforms
option_c: Because it removes the longstanding requirement for submitted articles to undergo peer review
option_d: Because no-fee journals sustain a plurality of venues, languages, and disciplines outside commercial models
option_e: Because it requires that all scholarly publishing take place exclusively in the English language
explanation_correct: Diamond OA underpins bibliodiversity by enabling many small, scholar-led, multilingual, and regional journals to exist without author charges, countering consolidation around a few large commercial publishers.
explanation_incorrect: Diamond OA increases diversity rather than standardizing, centralizing, or restricting language, and it retains peer review.

question: Why should citation counts be interpreted with attention to the field and age of a publication?
option_a: Because papers that are sufficiently old can no longer be cited by anyone
option_b: Because the raw citation count is a number that is directly set by the paper's own author
option_c: Because which papers get cited is ultimately governed by pure random chance
option_d: Because citation rates vary widely by discipline and accumulate over time
option_e: Because citations are only ever accumulated by formal review articles
explanation_correct: Different fields cite at very different rates and citations build up over years, so a fair reading requires normalizing by field and publication age rather than comparing raw totals directly.
explanation_incorrect: Older papers can be cited, citations are not random or author-set, and articles beyond reviews are cited; the issue is field and time dependence.

question: What is the main idea behind using a 'basket of metrics' rather than a single indicator in evaluation?
option_a: Because no single metric captures research quality or impact
option_b: To automatically generate a ranking of researchers with no human input
option_c: To provide an absolute, mathematical guarantee of complete objectivity
option_d: To entirely replace the role that formal peer review currently plays
option_e: Simply to make the overall evaluation process faster to complete
explanation_correct: Relying on one number invites gaming and misses dimensions of research; a thoughtfully chosen set of indicators, used alongside qualitative judgement, better reflects varied contributions and reduces the distortion any single metric creates.
explanation_incorrect: A basket of metrics aims for balance and context, not speed, guaranteed objectivity, replacing peer review, or automatic ranking.

question: What does the term 'bibliometrics' refer to?
option_a: The visual and aesthetic design work that goes into a book's cover
option_b: The academic study of how library buildings are physically designed
option_c: The study of how journals set and adjust their subscription pricing
option_d: The quantitative analysis of publications and their citations
option_e: The formal ethical rules that govern who counts as an article's author
explanation_correct: Bibliometrics is the use of quantitative methods - counting and analyzing publications, citations, and related data - to study and measure aspects of scholarly output and communication.
explanation_incorrect: Bibliometrics concerns quantitative analysis of the literature, not library buildings, pricing, design, or ethics.

question: Why is it problematic to compare researchers using the raw h-index across different career stages?
option_a: Because the specific value of the h-index is something the researcher personally chooses
option_b: Because the h-index is, in fact, a number that cannot be computed for anyone at all
option_c: Because the h-index is a metric that counts only citations to published books
option_d: Because a researcher's h-index score inevitably decreases by some amount every year
option_e: Because the h-index tends to grow with time and total output
explanation_correct: Since the h-index depends on accumulated citations and number of publications, it rises over a career; comparing an early-career researcher's h-index directly with a senior one's disadvantages the former irrespective of quality.
explanation_incorrect: The h-index is computable, does not decrease yearly, is not author-chosen, and is not book-only; the issue is career-stage dependence.

question: How does reliance on journal prestige as a proxy for quality conflict with responsible-metrics principles?
option_a: It substitutes where a work was published for evaluation of the work itself
option_b: It is an approach that, in practice, genuinely and faithfully reflects sound, independent expert judgement
option_c: It is an approach that is, in fact, explicitly required, recommended, and endorsed by DORA itself
option_d: It is an approach that is built entirely on properly applying field-normalized citation statistics
option_e: It is an approach that aligns perfectly and completely with the core responsible-metrics principles
explanation_correct: Judging a paper by its journal's prestige replaces direct assessment with a weak proxy; responsible-metrics principles insist on evaluating the actual content and diverse contributions, since journal-level metrics do not reliably indicate an individual article's quality.
explanation_incorrect: Journal-prestige proxying conflicts with (is not required by) DORA and the responsible-metrics movement, is not field normalization, and is not direct expert judgement.

question: Why is transparent disclosure of competing interests important even when no bias actually occurred?
option_a: Because disclosing a conflict of interest provides an absolute guarantee that the results are correct
option_b: It lets others judge whether interests may have influenced the work
option_c: Because disclosing a conflict of interest is believed to artificially raise a paper's impact factor
option_d: Because disclosing a conflict of interest happens to be a formal requirement for obtaining a DOI
option_e: Because disclosure of this kind is actually intended to hide the true source of funding
explanation_correct: Even absent actual bias, undisclosed interests can undermine trust if later revealed; transparent disclosure allows others to assess potential influence themselves, which is why it is expected regardless of outcome.
explanation_incorrect: Disclosure supports trust and independent judgement, not correctness guarantees, metrics, DOIs, or hiding funders.

question: What is the appropriate way to credit someone who helped with a study but does not meet authorship criteria?
option_a: Acknowledge their contribution in the acknowledgements, not as an author
option_b: Formally listing them on the paper as its corresponding author
option_c: Simply omitting any mention of their contribution from the paper entirely
option_d: Listing them as a full co-author on the paper regardless of their actual role
option_e: Citing their contribution formally as an external reference in the bibliography
explanation_correct: Contributors who do not meet authorship criteria (e.g., providing funding, technical help, or general supervision) should be named in the acknowledgements with their role, not listed as authors, to keep authorship accountable and accurate.
explanation_incorrect: Naming non-qualifying contributors as authors (including corresponding author) is inappropriate, and omitting or merely citing them fails to credit their help properly.

question: What is the safest first step when unsure whether a journal is legitimate or predatory?
option_a: Simply submit the manuscript and see what kind of response comes back
option_b: Use a checklist to verify the journal's transparency and legitimacy
option_c: Pay the journal's submission fee as a way of testing its review process
option_d: Assume that any journal with a functioning website must be legitimate
option_e: Choose whichever journal happens to charge the highest publication fee
explanation_correct: Before submitting, authors should evaluate the journal against a checklist (e.g., Think. Check. Submit.), checking indexing, editorial transparency, peer-review process, and legitimate contact information to avoid predatory venues.
explanation_incorrect: Submitting or paying to test, or assuming legitimacy from a website or high fee, all risk falling for predatory journals.

question: Why do many journals now require authors to disclose the use of generative AI tools but prohibit listing them as authors?
option_a: Because commercial generative AI tools are generally considered too expensive to use
option_b: Because generative AI tools do not currently have their own registered ORCID iDs
option_c: Because AI cannot be accountable for the work, though its use must be disclosed
option_d: Because any text produced with the help of AI is automatically considered plagiarized
option_e: Because disclosing the use of AI tools is believed to raise a journal's impact factor
explanation_correct: Authorship entails accountability that AI cannot bear, so AI is not an author; because AI can shape or err in content, its use must nonetheless be disclosed for transparency and to keep responsibility with the human authors.
explanation_incorrect: The reason is accountability and transparency, not cost, identifiers, an assumption of plagiarism, or metrics.

question: Why are paper mills difficult to detect through traditional single-manuscript peer review alone?
option_a: Because traditional peer review is, in every single case, guaranteed to catch fraud
option_b: Because the reviewers involved are secretly paid by the paper mill to ignore the fraud
option_c: Because the entire peer-review process at most journals is, in practice, fully automated
option_d: Because paper mills are understood to exclusively target a single specific journal at a time
option_e: A lone reviewer sees one paper in isolation, while mill patterns emerge only across many papers
explanation_correct: Fraud from paper mills frequently becomes apparent only when many outputs are compared (shared figures, boilerplate phrasing, tortured phrases, coordinated author swaps); a single reviewer assessing one manuscript in isolation is poorly placed to spot these cross-submission patterns.
explanation_incorrect: Peer review does not always catch fraud, is not automated, and paper mills span many journals; the detection gap is the per-manuscript view versus systemic patterns.

question: Why does sharing both data and the analysis code better support reproducibility than sharing data alone?
option_a: Because including the analysis code somehow makes the underlying dataset physically smaller
option_b: Because sharing the analysis code is a formal requirement for obtaining a persistent DOI
option_c: Because the analysis code is thought to functionally replace the need for the data itself
option_d: Because the code documents exactly how the data were transformed into results
option_e: Because sharing the analysis code has the effect of anonymizing the underlying dataset
explanation_correct: Reproducing results usually requires the precise processing steps; without the code, the path from data to reported findings is opaque, so sharing both is needed for others to verify and reuse the analysis.
explanation_incorrect: Code does not shrink or replace data, assign DOIs, or anonymize; it makes the analysis transparent and rerunnable.

question: What is the value of preregistering analysis plans in observational (non-experimental) studies?
option_a: Preregistering an observational study's analysis plan is thought to replace formal peer review
option_b: Preregistration, as a practice, is understood to be useful only for true experimental studies
option_c: Preregistering an analysis plan is believed to guarantee that any resulting conclusions are causal
option_d: Preregistering an analysis plan is thought to remove the need to actually collect any data
option_e: It constrains analytic flexibility, distinguishing confirmatory from exploratory analyses
explanation_correct: Even without an experiment, observational analyses face many analytic choices; preregistering the plan separates confirmatory from exploratory analyses and curbs the selective, data-dependent decisions that inflate false positives.
explanation_incorrect: Preregistration applies beyond experiments, does not guarantee causality, and does not replace data or peer review.

question: What is the difference between reproducing and replicating a result, as commonly distinguished?
option_a: Reproducing means getting the same result using the original data and code
option_b: The two terms, reproducing and replicating, are, in every respect, completely identical
option_c: Reproducing a result is understood to specifically require collecting an entirely new dataset
option_d: Replicating a result is understood to specifically involve reusing the exact same original dataset
option_e: Neither of these two related concepts actually involves working with any data at all
explanation_correct: In the common (National Academies) framing, reproducibility reuses the same data and analysis to obtain the same result, while replicability uses newly collected data to test whether the finding holds again.
explanation_incorrect: The two differ in whether new data are collected; reproducing reuses the original data, replicating gathers new data.

question: Why can publication bias distort systematic reviews and meta-analyses?
option_a: Because systematic reviews, by their nature, simply ignore every individual study available
option_b: Because researchers conducting meta-analyses are known to routinely fabricate their underlying data
option_c: Positive-result studies are more likely to be published, so reviews overestimate effects
option_d: Because studies that report null or negative results are, as a rule, always found to be incorrect
option_e: Because systematic reviews of this kind draw their evidence exclusively from preprint servers
explanation_correct: If positive findings are preferentially published, a meta-analysis built on the visible literature will over-represent positive effects and overstate the true effect, which is why registries and searching for unpublished results matter.
explanation_incorrect: Reviews use published studies (the bias is in what gets published), do not fabricate data, and null results are legitimate; the distortion comes from selective publication.

question: Why is 'reproducible but not replicable' a coherent and informative outcome?
option_a: This outcome would specifically indicate that the original analysis had been fraudulently conducted
option_b: This particular combination of outcomes is, on its face, a logical contradiction
option_c: A result can be computationally reproducible yet fail to replicate with fresh data
option_d: This outcome would specifically mean that the underlying dataset had since been deleted
option_e: It would simply mean that the two terms, reproducible and replicable, are actually synonyms
explanation_correct: Because reproducibility and replicability are distinct, a study can be perfectly reproducible while a fresh-data replication does not reproduce the effect, pointing to generalizability or robustness issues rather than a mistake in the original computation.
explanation_incorrect: The outcome is coherent (not a contradiction, fraud, or data loss) precisely because the two concepts differ.

question: Why is community governance considered a defining feature of 'open' scholarly infrastructure?
option_a: Because governance structured this way is understood to primarily maximize commercial profit
option_b: Governance by the community helps resist capture by narrow interests
option_c: Because governance structured this way is what makes a service completely free to build
option_d: Because governance structured this way removes the underlying need for any ongoing funding
option_e: Because governance structured this way is what guarantees a service a high journal impact factor
explanation_correct: Open infrastructure is characterized not just by open code and data but by accountable, stakeholder-based governance, which keeps the service aligned with community interests and guards against enclosure - a core POSI principle.
explanation_incorrect: Community governance concerns accountability and independence, not profit maximization, cost-free building, eliminating funding needs, or metrics.

question: What role do metadata aggregators (e.g., OpenAIRE, BASE, CORE) play in the open-access ecosystem?
option_a: They function primarily as venues that publish entirely new, original research findings
option_b: They function primarily as services that produce formal rankings of universities
option_c: They function primarily as services that calculate official journal impact factor scores
option_d: They harvest and combine metadata (and sometimes full text) from many repositories and journals
option_e: They function primarily as services that coordinate and conduct formal peer review
explanation_correct: Aggregators gather records from thousands of sources into unified indexes, so users can find open-access outputs scattered across many repositories and journals from one place - a crucial discovery layer over distributed infrastructure.
explanation_incorrect: Aggregators index and surface others' content; they do not publish original research, assign metrics, rank institutions, or review papers.

question: What is a 'repository' in scholarly infrastructure?
option_a: An online archive storing scholarly outputs, often with persistent identifiers
option_b: A specific citation metric used to measure the overall impact of a scholarly work
option_c: A commercial bookstore that specializes in selling academic and scholarly titles
option_d: A formal committee responsible for coordinating a journal's peer-review process
option_e: A funding body that distributes competitive grants to support new research
explanation_correct: A repository is a platform - institutional, subject-based, or general-purpose - that collects, preserves, and provides access to scholarly outputs, frequently assigning persistent identifiers to support discovery and citation.
explanation_incorrect: A repository is an access/preservation archive, not a bookstore, metric, review committee, or funder.

question: Why do initiatives like SCOSS and Invest in Open Infrastructure exist?
option_a: Their main purpose is to sell open-source software licenses to institutions
option_b: Their main purpose is to handle the technical process of assigning new DOIs
option_c: Their main purpose is to produce a formal ranking of open services by usage
option_d: Their main purpose is to work toward privatizing existing scholarly infrastructure
option_e: Because many open, community-serving infrastructures lack stable funding
explanation_correct: Open infrastructures often depend on precarious funding despite being widely relied upon; SCOSS coordinates community funding and IOI provides research and advocacy, both aiming to secure the sustainability and independence of these shared services.
explanation_incorrect: These initiatives sustain (not sell, rank, or privatize) open infrastructure and do not assign identifiers.

question: How does dependence on proprietary citation databases complicate reproducible bibliometric analysis?
option_a: Because data drawn from proprietary sources is, without exception, always factually wrong
option_b: It has essentially no meaningful effect on bibliometric analysis at all
option_c: Opaque, subscription-gated data cannot be fully audited or reproduced
option_d: Because openly licensed citation data is, in every case, considerably more expensive to obtain
option_e: Because bibliometric analysis, by definition, cannot make any use of citation data whatsoever
explanation_correct: When the underlying data and its curation are closed and paywalled, others cannot verify or reproduce a bibliometric study; open, transparent citation sources allow auditable, reproducible analysis, motivating the open-research-information movement.
explanation_incorrect: The issue is opacity and access, not that proprietary data is always wrong or that open data costs more; bibliometrics does use citation data.

question: Why is labeling a preprint clearly as 'not peer reviewed' considered important?
option_a: Because preprints are, by their very nature, considered inherently unreliable
option_b: Because labeling a preprint this way is believed to lower its eventual citation count
option_c: Because most academic journals formally require this label for indexing purposes
option_d: Because it hides the authors
option_e: So readers know the work hasn't undergone formal peer review yet
explanation_correct: Clear labeling signals that a preprint's claims have not been vetted by peer review, helping audiences - especially non-specialists and media - interpret the results cautiously, a lesson reinforced during the pandemic.
explanation_incorrect: Labeling supports responsible interpretation; preprints are not inherently unreliable, and labeling is about transparency, not citations, hiding authors, or indexing rules.

question: How can posting a preprint benefit a study's eventual peer-reviewed version?
option_a: Posting a preprint effectively replaces the need for formal peer review
option_b: Posting a preprint provides an automatic guarantee that the paper will be accepted
option_c: Posting a preprint effectively hides the underlying work from potential reviewers
option_d: Posting a preprint removes the need for the author to make any later revisions
option_e: Community feedback can help authors identify errors before formal review
explanation_correct: Making a preprint public invites comments and scrutiny that authors can use to strengthen the paper prior to or alongside formal review, potentially improving the final published version.
explanation_incorrect: Preprinting complements peer review; it does not replace it, guarantee acceptance, remove revisions, or hide the work.

question: What does peer review aim to assess about a manuscript?
option_a: Simply the total number of figures included in the manuscript
option_b: Only its grammar
option_c: The specific citation count accumulated by the manuscript's author
option_d: The official impact factor of the journal considering the manuscript
option_e: Its validity, rigor, and significance, judged by experts
explanation_correct: Peer review evaluates whether a study's methods are sound, its claims supported, and its contribution significant, through assessment by qualified independent experts - going well beyond surface features like grammar.
explanation_incorrect: Peer review targets scholarly quality, not just grammar, and is unrelated to citation counts, impact factors, or figure counts.

question: What is a recognized advantage of open (published) peer-review reports for readers?
option_a: They tend to actively hide any concerns that reviewers may have raised
option_b: They mainly serve to make the resulting published article noticeably shorter
option_c: They provide an absolute guarantee that the published paper is correct
option_d: They effectively replace the need to publish the article itself
option_e: They let readers see how the paper was scrutinized and revised
explanation_correct: Publishing the review reports (and author responses) lets readers understand how a paper was assessed and improved, increasing transparency and accountability in the evaluation process.
explanation_incorrect: Open reports add transparency; they do not shorten or replace the article, guarantee correctness, or conceal concerns.

question: Why do journal-independent review services (e.g., Review Commons, PCI) potentially reduce reviewer burden across the system?
option_a: They actually work by adding several extra rounds of review to the process
option_b: They function by requiring authors to pay for each individual review conducted
option_c: They work primarily by eliminating the need for human reviewers altogether
option_d: They function by having reviewers evaluate only the manuscript's abstract
option_e: A manuscript is reviewed once, and reports travel to the chosen journal
explanation_correct: By decoupling review from a specific journal, these services let a single high-quality review follow the paper to a compatible venue, cutting the duplicated reviewing effort that occurs when sequential journals each commission new reviews.
explanation_incorrect: They reduce, not add, review rounds, still use reviewers and full manuscripts, and are typically free rather than paid.

question: Why is an explicit license essential for others to legally build on shared research code?
option_a: Adding an explicit license file is mainly understood to make the underlying code run faster
option_b: Adding an explicit license file works mainly by encrypting the underlying source code
option_c: Adding an explicit license file is a formal requirement for assigning the code a DOI
option_d: Because without a license the code defaults to all-rights-reserved copyright
option_e: Adding an explicit license file is mainly a way to produce a formal software ranking
explanation_correct: Publishing code does not grant rights; only an explicit open-source license gives others legal permission to reuse and adapt it, so an unlicensed public repository is not truly reusable.
explanation_incorrect: A license grants legal rights, not speed, DOIs, encryption, or ranking.

question: What is the practical difference between a permissive (MIT) and a copyleft (GPL) license for someone reusing the code in a commercial product?
option_a: The MIT license explicitly forbids any commercial use of the licensed software
option_b: There is, in fact, no meaningful practical difference between the two license types
option_c: MIT allows incorporation into closed/commercial software with minimal conditions
option_d: The GPL license permits a company to freely close off the source code afterward
option_e: Both of these particular license types strictly prevent any commercial use whatsoever
explanation_correct: With MIT, a company can reuse the code in a proprietary product by preserving the notice; with GPL, redistributing a derivative obligates them to license it under GPL-compatible open terms, which can be incompatible with keeping it closed.
explanation_incorrect: There is a real difference: MIT permits proprietary reuse, GPL requires openness for distributed derivatives; neither forbids commercial use outright.

question: What does a CITATION.cff file in a code repository do?
option_a: It directly executes and runs the software described in the repository
option_b: It functions primarily as a place to store the project's underlying data
option_c: It handles the technical process of compiling the project's source code
option_d: It provides machine-readable metadata telling people how to cite the software
option_e: It handles the formal process of assigning an open-source license
explanation_correct: A CITATION.cff file supplies structured citation information (authors, title, version, DOI) so users and tools (like GitHub's 'Cite this repository' feature) can generate correct citations for the software.
explanation_incorrect: CITATION.cff conveys citation metadata; it does not run or compile code, store data, or serve as a license.

question: Why is archiving a tagged software release (e.g., in Zenodo) important for citing the software used in a study?
option_a: Because archiving the release is what actually runs the underlying analysis
option_b: Because archiving the release helps produce a formal ranking of the software
option_c: Because archiving the release removes the need to attach any kind of license
option_d: Because archiving the release has the effect of making the code fully proprietary
option_e: Because it creates a preserved, version-specific snapshot with a DOI
explanation_correct: A Zenodo archive of a specific release yields a citable, preserved, version-pinned record with a DOI; a bare repository link is mutable and may change or vanish, undermining reproducibility and precise citation.
explanation_incorrect: Archiving preserves and identifies a version; it does not run analyses, rank code, make it proprietary, or replace licensing.

question: Why is recognizing software as a citable research output relevant to research assessment reform?
option_a: Recognizing software this way, somewhat paradoxically, makes it impossible to cite
option_b: Recognizing software this way is intended to eventually replace journal articles entirely
option_c: Because valuing and citing software credits the people who build and maintain research tools
option_d: Recognizing software as a citable output is understood to matter only for physical hardware
option_e: Recognizing software as a citable research output is believed to actually lower reproducibility
explanation_correct: Treating software as a first-class, citable output gives credit to developers and maintainers whose work underpins research; this aligns with assessment reforms that seek to recognize contributions beyond traditional papers.
explanation_incorrect: Recognizing software makes it citable (not uncitable), complements rather than replaces articles, is not hardware-only, and supports reproducibility.
review_note: Verify FORCE11 Software Citation Principles URL.

question: Why do funder mandates increasingly require immediate (zero-embargo) open access rather than allowing delays?
option_a: To substantially increase the subscription revenue collected by publishers
option_b: To deliberately reduce the overall number of publications produced
option_c: To deliberately restrict who is legally permitted to read the research
option_d: To formally eliminate the use of persistent identifiers altogether
option_e: Because immediate access maximizes public benefit from publicly funded research
explanation_correct: Zero-embargo requirements (as in Plan S, the Nelson memo, NIH 2024, and Horizon Europe) ensure the public and other researchers can use funded findings without delay, reflecting that access should not be withheld for content the public financed.
explanation_incorrect: Immediate access serves timeliness and public benefit, not subscription revenue, restricting readership, cutting output, or removing identifiers.

question: How does the rights-retention route let authors comply with an immediate-OA mandate at no cost?
option_a: By having the author agree to pay a substantially higher APC
option_b: By simply waiting patiently until the embargo period eventually lapses
option_c: By formally transferring the manuscript's copyright to the publisher
option_d: By retaining rights to apply a CC BY licence and depositing openly at publication
option_e: By publishing the work exclusively through the hybrid journal route
explanation_correct: Under rights retention, the funder requires a prior CC BY license on the author accepted manuscript, so the author can deposit it openly on publication with no APC and no embargo, satisfying an immediate-OA policy for free.
explanation_incorrect: Rights retention avoids APCs and embargoes; it does not involve higher fees, copyright transfer, waiting out embargoes, or hybrid publishing.

question: What is the basic goal of a funder public-access or open-access policy?
option_a: To limit access to the funder's staff
option_b: To increase journal subscription prices
option_c: To make funder-paid research freely available
option_d: To rank funded researchers
option_e: To reduce the amount of research shared
explanation_correct: Funder access policies aim to ensure that the outputs of funded research are freely available to the public, so society benefits from and can build on research it financed.
explanation_incorrect: The goal is public access, not higher prices, staff-only access, ranking, or reducing sharing.

question: Why can researchers funded by multiple agencies face compliance complexity?
option_a: Because, in practice, only a single funder is ever able to maintain a policy
option_b: Because, in practice, every existing funder policy turns out to be identical
option_c: Because different funders require different licenses, routes, and terms
option_d: Because funder policies of this kind essentially never change over time
option_e: Because full compliance with these policies is, in practice, treated as optional
explanation_correct: When a project is co-funded, its outputs may need to meet several funders' distinct requirements simultaneously (licenses, accepted OA routes, data rules), creating real administrative complexity despite shared open-access goals.
explanation_incorrect: Funder policies are not identical or static, multiple funders can each have policies, and compliance is generally required, not optional.

question: Why is open access achieved solely through administrative policy (like the US Nelson memo) considered less durable than other routes?
option_a: Because administrative directives can be reversed by later administrations
option_b: Administrative policies of this kind are, in fact, considered inherently permanent
option_c: Administrative policies of this kind are understood to guarantee funding forever
option_d: Only commercial publishers are understood to be capable of providing durable open access
option_e: Administrative policy of this kind is understood to override every form of binding law
explanation_correct: OA that depends on an executive or agency directive can be undone by a change in administration, as the potential repeal of the Nelson memo illustrates; embedding OA in law, author-held rights, or community infrastructure makes it more resilient to political shifts.
explanation_incorrect: Administrative policy is not permanent or funding-guaranteeing and does not override law; durability comes from legal and community foundations, not publishers alone.
review_note: Volatile: re-verify Nelson memo status before publishing.
